From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia     [Unitary](/wiki/Unitary\_state)[parliamentary](/wiki/Parliamentary\_system)[republic](/wiki/Republic)  Constitution    [Parliament of Malta](/wiki/Parliament\_of\_Malta)  Type   [Parliament House](/wiki/Parliament\_House\_(Malta))  [Executive branch](/wiki/Executive\_(government))  [Head of state](/wiki/Head\_of\_state)  Title   [Myriam Spiteri Debono](/wiki/Myriam\_Spiteri\_Debono)  [Head of government](/wiki/Head\_of\_government)  Title   [Robert Abela](/wiki/Robert\_Abela)  Appointer    [Cabinet of Malta](/wiki/Cabinet\_of\_Malta)  Current cabinet   Prime Minister  [Judicial branch](/wiki/Judiciary)  Name                The \*\*politics of Malta\*\* takes place within a framework of a [parliamentary](/wiki/Parliamentary\_system)[representative democratic](/wiki/Representative\_democracy)[republic](/wiki/Republic), whereby the [president of Malta](/wiki/President\_of\_Malta) is the constitutional head of state. Executive authority is vested in the president of Malta, with the general direction and control of the [Government of Malta](/wiki/Government\_of\_Malta) remaining with the [prime minister of Malta](/wiki/Prime\_minister\_of\_Malta), who is the [head of government](/wiki/Head\_of\_government) and the cabinet. [Legislative power](/wiki/Legislative\_power) is vested in the [Parliament of Malta](/wiki/Parliament\_of\_Malta), which consists of the president of Malta and the unicameral [House of Representatives of Malta](/wiki/House\_of\_Representatives\_of\_Malta) with the [speaker](/wiki/Speaker\_of\_the\_House\_of\_Representatives\_of\_Malta) as the presiding officer of the legislative body. [Judicial power](/wiki/Judicial\_power) remains with the [chief justice](/wiki/Chief\_Justice\_of\_Malta) and the [judiciary of Malta](/wiki/Judiciary\_of\_Malta). Since [independence](/wiki/Independence\_Day\_(Malta)), the party electoral system has been dominated by the [Christian democratic](/wiki/Christian\_democratic)[Nationalist Party](/wiki/Nationalist\_Party\_(Malta)) (\*Partit Nazzjonalista\*) and the [social democratic](/wiki/Social\_democratic)[Labour Party](/wiki/Labour\_Party\_(Malta)) (\*Partit Laburista\*).

The [Economist Intelligence Unit](/wiki/Economist\_Intelligence\_Unit) rated Malta a "[flawed democracy](/wiki/Democracy\_Index#Definitions)" in 2022.[[1]](#cite\_note-democracy-index-1)[\*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual\_of\_Style/Dates\_and\_numbers#Chronological\_items)\*] ## Political developments since independence [(/w/index.php?title=Politics\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=1)] Since independence on 1964, two parties have dominated Malta's polarized and evenly divided politics during this period: the centre-right [Nationalist Party](/wiki/Nationalist\_Party\_(Malta)) and the centre-left [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour\_Party\_(Malta)).[[2]](#cite\_note-2) From the pre-independence [1962 general election](/wiki/1962\_Maltese\_general\_election) until 2017, third parties failed to score any electoral success. In the [2013 election](/wiki/2013\_Maltese\_general\_election), the [Democratic Alternative](/wiki/Democratic\_Alternative\_(Malta)) (a [green](/wiki/Green\_politics) party established in 1989) managed to secure only 1.8% of the first preference votes nationwide. The 1996 elections resulted in the election of the Labour Party, by 8,000 votes, to replace the Nationalists, who had won in 1987 and 1992. Voter turnout was characteristically high at 96%, with the Labour Party receiving 50.72%, the Nationalist Party 47.8%, the [Democratic Alternative](/wiki/Democratic\_Alternative\_(Malta)) 1.46%, and independent candidates 0.02%. In 1998, the Labour Party's loss in a parliamentary vote led the prime minister to call an early election. The Nationalist Party was returned to office in September 1998 by a majority of 13,000 votes, holding a five-seat majority in Parliament. Voter turnout was 95%, with the Nationalist Party receiving 51.81%, the Labour Party 46.97%, the Democratic Alternative 1.21%, and independent candidates 0.01%. By the end of 2002 the Nationalist government wrapped up negotiations for [European Union](/wiki/European\_Union) membership.

A [referendum](/wiki/Referendum) on the issue was called in March 2003, for which the Nationalists and the Democratic Alternative campaigned for a "yes" vote while Labour campaigned heavily for "no" vote, invalidate their vote or abstain. Turnout was 91%, with more than 53% voting "yes".[[3]](#cite\_note-3) The Labour Party argued that the "yes" votes amounted to less than 50% of the overall votes, hence, and citing the [1956 Maltese United Kingdom integration referendum](/wiki/1956\_Maltese\_United\_Kingdom\_integration\_referendum) as an example, they claimed that the "yes" had not in fact won the referendum. The then MLP Leader [Alfred Sant](/wiki/Alfred\_Sant) said that the general election which was to be held within a month would settle the affair. In the general elections the Nationalists were returned to office with 51.79% of the vote to Labour's 47.51%. The Democratic Alternative polled 0.68%. The Nationalists were thus able to form a government and sign and ratify the EU Accession Treaty on 16 April 2003. On 1 May 2004 Malta joined the EU and on 1 January 2008, the [Eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone) with the [euro](/wiki/Euro) as the national currency.[[4]](#cite\_note-4) The first elections after membership were held in March 2008 resulting in a narrow victory for the Nationalist Party with 49.34% of first preference votes. In May 2011, a nationwide [referendum](/wiki/2011\_Maltese\_divorce\_referendum) was held on the introduction of [divorce](/wiki/Divorce).

This was the first time in the history of parliament that Parliament approved a motion originating outside from the Cabinet.[[5]](#cite\_note-5) In March 2013, the Labour Party returned to government after fifteen years in opposition with a record-breaking lead of 36,000 votes leading to the resignation of the Nationalist leader [Lawrence Gonzi](/wiki/Lawrence\_Gonzi), and [Joseph Muscat](/wiki/Joseph\_Muscat) became prime minister.[[6]](#cite\_note-6)[[7]](#cite\_note-7) In June 2017, the Labour Party called in a snap election on its [May Day](/wiki/May\_Day) celebrations and increased its vote disparity to around 40,000 votes.[[8]](#cite\_note-8) The then leader of the opposition, [Simon Busuttil](/wiki/Simon\_Busuttil), announced his resignation shortly thereafter. This election saw the first third party elected to Malta's Parliament since independence, with the election of [Marlene Farrugia](/wiki/Marlene\_Farrugia) in the 10th District representing the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic\_Party\_(Malta)).[[9]](#cite\_note-9)[Joseph Muscat](/wiki/Joseph\_Muscat) continued to be prime minister[[10]](#cite\_note-times-10) In January 2020, he stepped down after the [2019 Malta political crisis](/wiki/2019\_Malta\_political\_crisis) surrounding the car bombing of investigative journalist [Daphne Caruana Galizia](/wiki/Daphne\_Caruana\_Galizia).[[11]](#cite\_note-11)[Robert Abela](/wiki/Robert\_Abela) - the son of Malta's former president [George Abela](/wiki/George\_Abela) - elected a new leader of Labour Party and new prime minister of Malta in January 2020.[[12]](#cite\_note-12) Democratic Alternative and the Democratic Party merged into a new party, [AD+PD](/wiki/AD%2BPD), on 17 October 2020.[[13]](#cite\_note-13)[[14]](#cite\_note-14) In March 2022, the ruling Labour party, led by Prime Minister Robert Abela, won its third successive [election](/wiki/2022\_Maltese\_general\_election).

It gained even bigger victory than in 2013 and in 2017.[[15]](#cite\_note-15) ## Executive branch [(/w/index.php?title=Politics\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=2)] Under its 1964 constitution, Malta became a [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary\_democracy) within the Commonwealth. Queen [Elizabeth II of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Elizabeth\_II\_of\_the\_United\_Kingdom) was sovereign of Malta, and a [governor-general](/wiki/Governor-general) exercised executive authority on her behalf, while the actual direction and control of the government and the nation's affairs were in the hands of the cabinet under the leadership of a Maltese prime minister. On December 13, 1974, the constitution was revised, and Malta became a republic within the Commonwealth, with executive authority vested in the [president of Malta](/wiki/President\_of\_Malta), which can be exercised directly or through officers subordinate to him. The president is elected by the House of Representatives for a five-year term. They appoint as prime minister the leader of the party with a majority of seats in the [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameral) House of Representatives, known in Maltese as \*Kamra tar-Rappreżentanti\*. [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Malta\_Attard\_San\_Anton\_Palace\_BW\_2011-10-09\_10-06-16.jpg/250px-Malta\_Attard\_San\_Anton\_Palace\_BW\_2011-10-09\_10-06-16.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_Attard\_San\_Anton\_Palace\_BW\_2011-10-09\_10-06-16.jpg) [San Anton Palace](/wiki/San\_Anton\_Palace) in [Attard](/wiki/Attard) serves as the official residence of the president of Malta The president also nominally appoints, upon recommendation of the prime minister, the individual ministers. Ministers are selected from among the members of the House of Representatives, which usually consists of 65 members unless bonus seats are given to a party which gains an absolute majority of votes but not a parliamentary majority.

Elections must be held at least every five years and the electoral system used is [single transferable vote](/wiki/Single\_transferable\_vote). ### Administrative divisions [(/w/index.php?title=Politics\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=3)] Malta is divided into 68 elected [local councils](/wiki/Local\_councils\_of\_Malta), with each council responsible for the administration of cities or regions of varying sizes. Administrative responsibility is distributed between the local councils and the central government in Valletta. The [Local Councils Act, 1993](/w/index.php?title=Local\_Councils\_Act,\_1993&action=edit&redlink=1) (Act XV of 1993), was published on June 30, 1993, subdividing Malta into 54 local councils in Malta and 14 in [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo). The inhabitants who are registered elect the Council every three years, as voters in the [Local Councils' Electoral Register](/w/index.php?title=Local\_Councils%27\_Electoral\_Register&action=edit&redlink=1). Elections are held by means of the system of proportional representation using the single transferable vote. The mayor is the head of the Local Council and the representative of the Council for all effects under the Act. The executive secretary, who is appointed by the Council, is the executive, administrative, and financial head of the Council. All decisions are taken collectively with the other members of the Council. Local councils are responsible for the general upkeep and embellishment of the locality, local wardens, and refuse collection, and carry out general administrative duties for the central government such as collection of government rents and funds, and answering government-related public inquiries.

There are also Administrative Committees elected with responsibility for smaller regions. ## Legislative branch [(/w/index.php?title=Politics\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=4)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Maltese\_Parliament\_building.jpg/250px-Maltese\_Parliament\_building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maltese\_Parliament\_building.jpg) The [Parliament House](/wiki/Parliament\_House\_(Malta)) in Valletta Elections to the [House of Representatives](/wiki/House\_of\_Representatives\_of\_Malta) (\*Kamra tad-Deputati\*) are based on the [single transferable vote](/wiki/Single\_transferable\_vote) system, a variant of the [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional\_representation) electoral system. First, [vacancies are filled](/wiki/Casual\_vacancy) through [casual election](/w/index.php?title=Casual\_election&action=edit&redlink=1) and subsequent vacancies through co-option, meaning that no [by-elections](/wiki/By-election) are held between one general election and the other. The parliamentary term cannot exceed five years. Ordinarily, 65 members are elected to the House from 13 multi-seat [constituencies](/wiki/Constituency) each returning 5 MPs. Additional MPs are elected in two circumstances: \* When a party achieves 50%+1 of first-preference valid votes in the election but does not secure a parliamentary majority it is awarded enough seats (filled by the best runner-up candidates) to make a parliamentary majority \* When in an election contested by more than two parties only two parties are elected to Parliament and the relative parliamentary strength is not proportionate to the first preference votes obtained, additional seats are allocated to establish proportionality A third electoral amendment has been enacted which guarantees strict-proportionality with respect to votes and seats to parliamentary political groups. ## Political party standings as of the most recent election [(/w/index.php?title=Politics\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=5)] For other political parties, see [List of political parties in Malta](/wiki/List\_of\_political\_parties\_in\_Malta).

An overview on elections and election results is included in [Elections in Malta](/wiki/Elections\_in\_Malta). Main article: [2022 Maltese general election](/wiki/2022\_Maltese\_general\_election) Main articles: [Maltese Government 2022–2027](/wiki/Maltese\_Government\_2022%E2%80%932027) and [List of members of the parliament of Malta, 2022–2027](/wiki/List\_of\_members\_of\_the\_parliament\_of\_Malta,\_2022%E2%80%932027) Voting stations were opened from 07:00 to 22:00, and in total, there were 355,075 citizens that had the right to vote in the general election.[[16]](#cite\_note-16)[[17]](#cite\_note-17) The turnout at 14:00 was reported at 44.8%, which was eight points lower than in 2017.[[18]](#cite\_note-18) According to the Electoral Commission of Malta, a total of 304,050 citizens voted, which made it the lowest turnout since the [1955 election](/wiki/1955\_Maltese\_general\_election).[[19]](#cite\_note-19)[[20]](#cite\_note-20) In addition to the 65 regularly elected members, two seats were awarded to the Nationalist Party to restore proportionality between votes obtained and parliamentary representation and twelve more women were appointed (six for each of the parties with elected members) to comply with the gender-corrective mechanism.[[21]](#cite\_note-21)    Votes  Seats   162,707  44   123,233  35   4,747  0   1,533  0   1,364  0   382  0   1,282  0   295,248  79   295,248   8,802   304,050   355,075   ## Judicial branch [(/w/index.php?title=Politics\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=6)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Malta\_Valletta\_BW\_2011-10-07\_10-41-05.JPG/250px-Malta\_Valletta\_BW\_2011-10-07\_10-41-05.JPG)](/wiki/File:Malta\_Valletta\_BW\_2011-10-07\_10-41-05.JPG) The [Courts of Justice building](/wiki/Courts\_of\_Justice\_building\_(Valletta)) in Valletta The judicial system in Malta comprises inferior courts, civil and criminal courts of appeal, and a Constitutional Court.[[23]](#cite\_note-COM2003-23) Inferior courts are presided over by magistrates which have original jurisdiction in criminal and civil actions.

In the criminal courts, the presiding judge sits with a jury of nine. The Court of Appeal and the Court of Criminal Appeal hear appeals from decisions of the civil and criminal actions respectively. The highest court, the Constitutional Court, has both original and appellate jurisdiction. In its appellate jurisdiction it adjudicates cases involving violations of human rights and interpretation of the Constitution. It can also perform [judicial review](/wiki/Judicial\_review). In its original jurisdiction it has jurisdiction over disputed parliamentary elections and electoral corrupt practices. There is a legal aid scheme offered to citizens lacking the means to afford legal defence.[[23]](#cite\_note-COM2003-23) According to the Constitution, the president appoints the [chief justice of Malta](/wiki/Chief\_justice\_of\_Malta) acting in accordance with a resolution of the House supported by the votes of not less than two-thirds of all the members of the [House of Representatives](/wiki/House\_of\_Representatives\_of\_Malta). The judges of the Superior Court and the magistrates of the inferior courts are appointed through the [Judicial Appointments Committee of Malta](/w/index.php?title=Judicial\_Appointments\_Committee\_of\_Malta&action=edit&redlink=1). Guarantees for the independence of the judiciary include the security of tenure for judges until their retiring age set at 65 (with a choice to extend retirement till 68), or until [impeachment](/wiki/Impeachment). The impeachment procedure for judges foresees a removal decision of the president upon request by the [Commission for the Administration for Justice](/w/index.php?title=Commission\_for\_the\_Administration\_for\_Justice&action=edit&redlink=1).

The independence of the judiciary is also guaranteed by the constitutional requirement that the judges’ salaries are paid from the Consolidated Fund and thus the government may not diminish or amend them to their prejudice. The Maltese system is considered in line with the principles of separation of powers and of independence of the judiciary. However, in its pre-accession evaluation reports, the [European Commission](/wiki/European\_Commission) has suggested in 2003 the need to reform the procedure for appointment of the members of the judiciary, currently "controlled by political bodies" (i.e. the Parliament and parties therein), in order to improve its objectivity.[[23]](#cite\_note-COM2003-23) The Commission has also pointed to the need to check the compliance of the procedure for challenging judges and magistrates provided for by Article 738 of the Code of Organisation and Civil Procedure with the principle of an impartial tribunal enshrined in the [European Convention on Human Rights](/wiki/European\_Convention\_on\_Human\_Rights).[[24]](#cite\_note-24) ## International organization participation [(/w/index.php?title=Politics\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=7)] Malta is a member of the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth\_of\_Nations), [CE](/wiki/Council\_of\_Europe), [EBRD](/wiki/European\_Bank\_for\_Reconstruction\_and\_Development), [ECE](/wiki/United\_Nations\_Economic\_Commission\_for\_Europe), [EU](/wiki/European\_Union) (member from 1 May 2004), [FAO](/wiki/Food\_and\_Agriculture\_Organization), [IAEA](/wiki/International\_Atomic\_Energy\_Agency), [IBRD](/wiki/International\_Bank\_for\_Reconstruction\_and\_Development), [ICAO](/wiki/International\_Civil\_Aviation\_Organization), [ICCt](/wiki/International\_Criminal\_Court), [ICFTU](/wiki/International\_Confederation\_of\_Free\_Trade\_Unions), [ICRM](/wiki/International\_Red\_Cross\_and\_Red\_Crescent\_Movement), [IFAD](/wiki/International\_Fund\_for\_Agricultural\_Development), [IFRCS](/wiki/International\_Red\_Cross\_and\_Red\_Crescent\_Movement), [ILO](/wiki/International\_Labour\_Organization), [IMF](/wiki/International\_Monetary\_Fund), [IMO](/wiki/International\_Maritime\_Organization), [Inmarsat](/wiki/International\_Mobile\_Satellite\_Organization), [Intelsat](/wiki/Intelsat), [Interpol](/wiki/Interpol\_(organization)), [IOC](/wiki/IOC), [IOM](/wiki/International\_Organization\_for\_Migration), [ISO](/wiki/International\_Organization\_for\_Standardization), [ITU](/wiki/International\_Telecommunication\_Union), [OPCW](/wiki/Organisation\_for\_the\_Prohibition\_of\_Chemical\_Weapons), [OSCE](/wiki/Organization\_for\_Security\_and\_Co-operation\_in\_Europe), [PCA](/wiki/Permanent\_Court\_of\_Arbitration), [UN](/wiki/United\_Nations), [UNCTAD](/wiki/United\_Nations\_Conference\_on\_Trade\_and\_Development), [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO), [UNIDO](/wiki/United\_Nations\_Industrial\_Development\_Organization), [UPU](/wiki/Universal\_Postal\_Union), [WCL](/wiki/World\_Confederation\_of\_Labour), [WCO](/wiki/World\_Customs\_Organization), [WHO](/wiki/World\_Health\_Organization), [WIPO](/wiki/World\_Intellectual\_Property\_Organization), [WMO](/wiki/World\_Meteorological\_Organization), [WToO](/wiki/World\_Tourism\_Organization), [WTrO](/wiki/World\_Trade\_Organization) Malta was a long-time member of the [Non-Aligned Movement](/wiki/Non-Aligned\_Movement).

It ceased to be part of the movement when it joined the European Union. ## See also [(/w/index.php?title=Politics\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=8)] \* ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Flag\_of\_Malta.svg/40px-Flag\_of\_Malta.svg.png)[Malta portal](/wiki/Portal:Malta) \* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/0/01/A\_coloured\_voting\_box.svg/40px-A\_coloured\_voting\_box.svg.png)](/wiki/File:A\_coloured\_voting\_box.svg)[Politics portal](/wiki/Portal:Politics) \* [Nationalist Party](/wiki/Nationalist\_Party\_(Malta)) \* [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour\_Party\_(Malta)) \* [People's Party](/wiki/People%27s\_Party\_(Malta)) \* [Volt Malta](/wiki/Volt\_Malta) \* [Mass meeting](/wiki/Mass\_meeting) ## Notes [(/w/index.php?title=Politics\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=9)] ## References [(/w/index.php?title=Politics\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=10)] 1. \*\*[^](#cite\_ref-democracy-index\_1-0)\*\*[Democracy Index 2023: Age of Conflict](https://pages.eiu.com/rs/753-RIQ-438/images/Democracy-Index-2023-Final-report.pdf)(PDF). \*[Economist Intelligence Unit](/wiki/Economist\_Intelligence\_Unit)\* (Report). 2024. [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20240609152810/https://pages.eiu.com/rs/753-RIQ-438/images/Democracy-Index-2023-Final-report.pdf)(PDF) from the original on 2024-06-09. [Ġgantija](/wiki/%C4%A0gantija) 2. [Ħaġar Qim](/wiki/%C4%A6a%C4%A1ar\_Qim) 3. [Mnajdra](/wiki/Mnajdra) 4. [Ta' Hagrat](/wiki/Ta%27\_%C4%A6a%C4%A1rat\_Temples) 5. [Skorba](/wiki/Skorba\_Temples) 6. [Tarxien](/wiki/Tarxien\_Temples)  [Criteria](/wiki/World\_Heritage\_Site#Selection\_criteria)   [132ter](https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/132ter)  Inscription   1992, 2015  Area   167 ha (0.64 sq mi)  [Borġ in-Nadur](/wiki/Bor%C4%A1\_in-Nadur) [Buġibba](/wiki/Bu%C4%A1ibba\_Temple) \*\*[Ġgantija](/wiki/%C4%A0gantija)\*\* \*\*[Ħaġar Qim](/wiki/%C4%A6a%C4%A1ar\_Qim)\*\* [Kordin](/wiki/Kordin\_Temples) \*\*[Mnajdra](/wiki/Mnajdra)\*\* [Santa Verna](/wiki/Santa\_Verna) \*\*[Skorba](/wiki/Skorba\_Temples)\*\* \*\*[Ta' Ħaġrat](/wiki/Ta%27\_%C4%A6a%C4%A1rat\_Temples)\*\* [Tal-Qadi](/wiki/Tal-Qadi\_Temple) [Tas-Silġ](/wiki/Tas-Sil%C4%A1) \*\*[Tarxien](/wiki/Tarxien\_Temples)\*\* [Xrobb l-Għaġin](/wiki/Xrobb\_l-G%C4%A7a%C4%A1in\_Temple) [class=notpageimage The \*\*Megalithic Temples of Malta\*\* ([Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_language): \*It-Tempji Megalitiċi ta' Malta\*) are several prehistoric temples, some of which are [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO)[World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World\_Heritage\_Site),[[1]](#cite\_note-1) built during three distinct periods approximately between 3600 BC and 2500 BC on the island country of [Malta](/wiki/Malta).[[2]](#cite\_note-2) They had been claimed as the oldest free-standing structures on Earth until the discovery of [Göbekli Tepe](/wiki/G%C3%B6bekli\_Tepe) in Turkey.[[3]](#cite\_note-3)[[4]](#cite\_note-4) Archaeologists believe that these [megalithic](/wiki/Megalith) complexes are the result of local innovations in a process of [cultural evolution](/wiki/Cultural\_evolution).[[5]](#cite\_note-5)[[6]](#cite\_note-6) This led to the building of several temples of the [Ġgantija](/wiki/%C4%A0gantija) phase (3600–3000 BC), culminating in the large [Tarxien temple complex](/wiki/Tarxien\_Temples), which remained in use until 2500 BC.

After this date, the temple-building culture disappeared.[[7]](#cite\_note-7)[[8]](#cite\_note-8) The Ġgantija temples were listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1980.[[9]](#cite\_note-Unesco-9) In 1992, the UNESCO Committee further extended the existing listing to include five other megalithic temple sites. These are [Ħaġar Qim](/wiki/%C4%A6a%C4%A1ar\_Qim) (in [Qrendi](/wiki/Qrendi)), [Mnajdra](/wiki/Mnajdra) (in Qrendi), [Ta' Ħaġrat Temples](/wiki/Ta%27\_%C4%A6a%C4%A1rat\_Temples) (in [Mġarr](/wiki/M%C4%A1arr)), [Skorba Temples](/wiki/Skorba\_Temples) (in [Żebbiegħ](/w/index.php?title=%C5%BBebbieg%C4%A7&action=edit&redlink=1)) and Tarxien Temples (in [Tarxien](/wiki/Tarxien)).[[9]](#cite\_note-Unesco-9) Nowadays, the sites are managed by [Heritage Malta](/wiki/Heritage\_Malta), while ownership of the surrounding lands varies from site to site.[[10]](#cite\_note-10)[[11]](#cite\_note-11) Apart from these, there are other megalithic temples in Malta which are not included in the UNESCO World Heritage list. ## Etymology [(/w/index.php?title=Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=1)] Many of the names used to refer to the different sites carry a link with the stones used for their building. The [Maltese word](/wiki/Maltese\_language) for boulders, 'ħaġar', is common to \*Ta' Ħaġrat\* and \*[Ħaġar Qim](/wiki/%C4%A6a%C4%A1ar\_Qim)\*.

While the former uses the word in conjunction with the marker of possession, the latter adds the word \*'Qim'\*, which is either a form of the Maltese word for 'worship', or an [archaic](/wiki/Archaism) form of the word meaning 'standing'.\*[[12]](#cite\_note-12)\* Maltese [folklore](/wiki/Folklore) describes [giants](/wiki/Giant\_(mythology)) as having built the temples, which led to the name Ġgantija, meaning 'Giants' tower'.[[13]](#cite\_note-13) The Maltese linguist [Joseph Aquilina](/wiki/Joseph\_Aquilina) believed that Mnajdra ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic\_language): منيدرة) was the diminutive of 'mandra' ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic\_language): مندرة), meaning a plot of ground planted with cultivated trees (the same usage is colloquial in [Egyptian Arabic](/wiki/Egyptian\_Arabic) today); a less likely derivation is from the [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic\_language) root 'manzara ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic\_language): منظرة), meaning 'a place with commanding views.'[[14]](#cite\_note-14) The Tarxien temples owe their name to the locality where they were found (from \*Tirix\*, meaning a large stone), as were the remains excavated at Skorba. ## History [(/w/index.php?title=Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=2)] Main articles: [History of Malta](/wiki/History\_of\_Malta) and [Timeline of Maltese history](/wiki/Timeline\_of\_Maltese\_history) ### Dates [(/w/index.php?title=Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=3)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Skorba1.jpg/250px-Skorba1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Skorba1.jpg) Charcoal found on site at Skorba was crucial in dating the Maltese Temple phases. The [temples](/wiki/Temples) were the result of several phases of construction from 5000 to 2200 [BC](/wiki/Anno\_Domini). There is evidence of human activity in the islands since the [Early Neolithic Period](/wiki/Neolithic#Europe) (c.

5900 BC), attested by pottery [sherds](/wiki/Sherd), evidence of fires, and bones.[\*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed)\*] The dating and understanding of the various phases of activity in the temples is not easy. The main problem found is that the sites themselves are evolutionary in nature, in that each successive temple brought with it further refinement to architectural development. Furthermore, in some cases, later Bronze Age peoples built their own sites over the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic) temples, thus adding an element of confusion to early researchers who did not have modern dating technology. [Sir Temi Żammit](/wiki/Themistocles\_Zammit), an eminent Maltese archaeologist of the late nineteenth century, had dated the Neolithic temples to 2800 BC and the Tarxien [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze\_Age) culture to 2000 BC.[[15]](#cite\_note-15) These dates were considered "considerably too high" by scholars,[[16]](#cite\_note-16) who proposed a reduction of half a millennium each.[[17]](#cite\_note-17) However, [radiocarbon testing](/wiki/Radiocarbon\_dating) favoured Żammit's dating.[[18]](#cite\_note-18)[[19]](#cite\_note-19) A theory that the temple art was connected with an [Aegean](/wiki/Aegean\_civilization)-derived culture collapsed with this proof of the temples' elder origins.[[20]](#cite\_note-20) ### Temple phases [(/w/index.php?title=Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=4)]     Phase   [Għar Dalam](/wiki/G%C4%A7ar\_Dalam\_phase)   4500–4400 BC  [Red Skorba](/w/index.php?title=Red\_Skorba\_phase&action=edit&redlink=1)   [Żebbuġ](/w/index.php?title=%C5%BBebbu%C4%A1\_phase&action=edit&redlink=1)   3800–3600 BC  [Ġgantija](/w/index.php?title=%C4%A0gantija\_phase&action=edit&redlink=1)   3300–3000 BC  [Tarxien](/wiki/Tarxien\_phase)   [Tarxien Cemetery](/wiki/Tarxien\_Cemetery\_phase)   1500–700 BC  [Baħrija](/w/index.php?title=Ba%C4%A7rija\_phase&action=edit&redlink=1)  The development of the chronological phases, based on recalibrated [radiocarbon dating](/wiki/Radiocarbon\_dating), has split the period up to the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze\_Age) in Malta into a number of phases.

The first evidence of human habitation in the Neolithic occurred in the Għar Dalam phase, in c. 5000 BC. The Temple period, from c. 4100 BC to roughly 2500 BC, produced the most notable monumental remains. This period is split into five phases;[\*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed)\*][[21]](#cite\_note-21) however, the first two of these left mostly pottery shards. The next three phases, starting from the Ġgantija phase, begins in c. 3600 BC, and the last, the Tarxien phase, ends in c. 2500 BC. #### Ġgantija phase (3600–3200 BC) [(/w/index.php?title=Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=5)] The [Ġgantija phase](/w/index.php?title=%C4%A0gantija\_phase&action=edit&redlink=1) is named after the [Ġgantija](/wiki/%C4%A0gantija) site in [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo). It represents an important development in the cultural evolution of Neolithic humans on the islands. To this date belong the earliest datable temples and the first two, if not three, of the stages of development in their ground plan: the lobed or kidney-shaped plan found in [Mġarr](/wiki/M%C4%A1arr) east, the trefoil plan evident in Skorba, Kordin and various minor sites, and the five-apsed plan Ġgantija South, Tarxien East.[[22]](#cite\_note-22) #### Saflieni phase (3300–3000 BC) [(/w/index.php?title=Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=6)] The [Saflieni phase](/wiki/Saflieni\_phase) constitutes a transitional phase between two major periods of development.[[23]](#cite\_note-23) Its name derives from the site of the [Hypogeum of Ħal-Saflieni](/wiki/%C4%A6al-Saflieni\_Hypogeum).

This period carried forward the same characteristics of the Ġgantija pottery shapes, but it also introduces new biconical bowls.[[24]](#cite\_note-24) #### Tarxien phase (3150–2500 BC) [(/w/index.php?title=Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=7)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Malta\_Hal\_Tarxien\_BW\_2011-10-04\_12-42-32.JPG/330px-Malta\_Hal\_Tarxien\_BW\_2011-10-04\_12-42-32.JPG)](/wiki/File:Malta\_Hal\_Tarxien\_BW\_2011-10-04\_12-42-32.JPG) An altar in the [Tarxien temple complex](/wiki/Tarxien\_Temples) The [Tarxien phase](/wiki/Tarxien\_phase) marks the peak of the temple civilisation. This phase is named after the temple-complex at Tarxien, a couple of kilometres inland from the [Grand Harbour](/wiki/Grand\_Harbour). To it belong the last two stages in the development of the temple plan. The western temple at Ġgantija represents, along with other units in Tarxien, Ħaġar Qim and L-Imnajdra, the penultimate stage in development, that is, the introduction of a shallow niche instead of an apse at the far end of the temple.

The final stage is testified in only one temple, the central unit at Tarxien, with its three symmetrical pairs of apses.[[25]](#cite\_note-25) The Temple culture reached its climax in this period, both in terms of the craftsmanship of pottery, as well as in sculptural decoration, both free-standing and in relief.[[26]](#cite\_note-26) Spiral reliefs resembling those at Tarxien once adorned the Ġgantija temples, but have faded to a level where they are only clearly recognisable in a series of drawings made by the artist [Charles Frederick de Brocktorff](/wiki/Charles\_Frederick\_de\_Brocktorff) in 1829, immediately after the temples' excavation.[[27]](#cite\_note-27) The Tarxien phase is characterised by a rich variety of [pottery](/wiki/Pottery) forms and decorative techniques. Most shapes tend to be angular, with almost no handles or lugs. The [clay](/wiki/Clay) tends to be well prepared and fired very hard, while the surface of the scratched ware is also highly polished. This scratched decoration remains standard, but it becomes more elaborate and elegant, the most popular motif being a kind of [volute](/wiki/Volute).[[28]](#cite\_note-28) ## Architecture and construction [(/w/index.php?title=Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=8)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Kordin\_III\_Temple\_Malta.png/250px-Kordin\_III\_Temple\_Malta.png)](/wiki/File:Kordin\_III\_Temple\_Malta.png) Part of the Kordin III Temple site, with a two-apse design [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Temple\_layout.png/250px-Temple\_layout.png)](/wiki/File:Temple\_layout.png) A diagram of the standard temple layout written in [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_language) The Maltese temple complexes were built in different locations, and over a wide span of years; while each individual site has its unique characteristics, they all share a common architecture.

The approach to the temples lies on an oval [forecourt](/wiki/Chamber\_tomb\_forecourt), levelled by terracing if the terrain is sloping. The forecourt is bounded on one side by the temples' own [façades](/wiki/Facade), which faces south or south-east. The monuments' façades and internal walls are made up of [orthostats](/wiki/Orthostat), a row of large stone slabs laid on end.[[29]](#cite\_note-29) The centre of the façades is usually interrupted by an entrance doorway forming a \*[trilithon](/wiki/Trilithon)\*, a pair of orthostats surmounted by a massive [lintel](/wiki/Lintel) slab.[[30]](#cite\_note-autogenerated58-30)[[31]](#cite\_note-31) Further trilithons form a passage, which is always paved in stone. This in turn opens onto an open space, which then gives way to the next element, a pair of D-shaped chambers, usually referred to as '[apses](/wiki/Apse)', opening on both sides of the passage. The space between the apses' walls and the external boundary wall is usually filled with loose stones and earth, sometimes containing cultural debris including [pottery](/wiki/Pottery) shards.[[32]](#cite\_note-autogenerated71-32) The main variation in the temples lies in the number of apses found; this may vary to three, four, five or six.

If three, they open directly from the central court in a [trefoil](/wiki/Trefoil) fashion.[[33]](#cite\_note-33) In cases of more complex temples, a second axial passage is built, using the same trilithon construction, leading from the first set of apses into another later pair, and either a fifth central or a [niche](/wiki/Niche\_(architecture)) giving the four or five apsial form. In one case, at the Tarxien central temple, the fifth apse or niche is replaced by a further passage, leading to a final pair of apses, making six in all.[[34]](#cite\_note-34) With the standard temple plan, found in some thirty temples across the islands, there is a certain amount of variation both in the number of apses, and in the overall length—ranging from 6.5m in the Mnajdra east temple to 23m in the six-apsed Tarxien central temple. The external walls were usually built of [coralline limestone](/wiki/Coralline\_limestone), which is harder than the globigerina limestone used in the internal sections of the temples.[[32]](#cite\_note-autogenerated71-32)[[35]](#cite\_note-35) The softer globigerina was used for decorative elements within the temples, usually carvings.

These features are usually sculpted in relief, and they show a variety of designs linked to vegetative or animal [symbolism](/wiki/Symbol).[[36]](#cite\_note-36) These usually depict running [spiral motifs](/w/index.php?title=Spiral\_motif&action=edit&redlink=1), trees and plants as well as a selection of animals.[[37]](#cite\_note-37) Although in their present form the temples are unroofed, a series of unproven theories regarding possible ceiling and roof structures have been debated for several years.[[38]](#cite\_note-38)[[39]](#cite\_note-39)[[40]](#cite\_note-40) ## UNESCO sites [(/w/index.php?title=Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=9)] ### Ġgantija [(/w/index.php?title=Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=10)] Main article: [Ġgantija](/wiki/%C4%A0gantija) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/%C4%A0gantija\_2014-003.jpg/250px-%C4%A0gantija\_2014-003.jpg)](/wiki/File:%C4%A0gantija\_2014-003.jpg) The megalithic remains at Ġgantija The Ġgantija temples stand at the end of the [Xagħra](/wiki/Xag%C4%A7ra)[plateau](/wiki/Plateau), facing towards the south-east. Its presence was known for a very long time, and even before any excavations were carried out a largely correct plan of its layout was drawn by [Jean-Pierre Houël](/wiki/Jean-Pierre\_Hou%C3%ABl) in the late eighteenth century.[[41]](#cite\_note-41) In 1827, the site was cleared of debris—the soil and remains being lost without proper examination.[[42]](#cite\_note-zammayr1-42) The loss resulting from this clearance was partially compensated by the German artist Brochtorff, who painted the site within a year or two from the removal of the debris. This is the only practical record of the clearance.[[42]](#cite\_note-zammayr1-42) A boundary wall encloses the temples.

The southerly one is the elder, and is better preserved.[[43]](#cite\_note-43) The plan of the temple incorporates five large apses, with traces of the plaster that once covered the irregular wall still clinging between the blocks.[[44]](#cite\_note-44) ### Ta' Ħaġrat [(/w/index.php?title=Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=11)] Main article: [Ta' Ħaġrat Temples](/wiki/Ta%27\_%C4%A6a%C4%A1rat\_Temples) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Ta%27Hagrat2.jpg/250px-Ta%27Hagrat2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ta%27Hagrat2.jpg) Ta' Ħaġrat The Ta' Ħaġrat temple in [Mġarr](/wiki/M%C4%A1arr) is on the eastern outskirts of the village, roughly one kilometer from the [Ta' Skorba temples](/wiki/Skorba\_Temples).[[45]](#cite\_note-45) The remains consist of a double temple, made up of two adjacent complexes, both in the shape of a trefoil. The two parts are both less regularly planned and smaller in size than many of the other neolithic temples in Malta, and no blocks are decorated.[[46]](#cite\_note-46) Sir Temi Żammit excavated the site in 1925–27. A village on the site that pre-dates the temples by centuries has provided plentiful examples of what is now known as \*Mġarr phase\* pottery.[[47]](#cite\_note-47) ### Skorba [(/w/index.php?title=Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=12)] Main article: [Skorba Temples](/wiki/Skorba\_Temples) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Sudika\_Mgarr\_Skorba\_temple.jpg/250px-Sudika\_Mgarr\_Skorba\_temple.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sudika\_Mgarr\_Skorba\_temple.jpg) Skorba The importance of this site lies less in the remains than in the information garnered from their excavations.[[48]](#cite\_note-48) This monument has a typical three-apsed shape of the Ġgantija phase, of which the greater part of the first two apses and the whole of the façade have been destroyed to ground level.

What remains are the stone paving of the entrance passage, with its perforations, the \*torba\* floors,[[49]](#cite\_note-49) and a large upright slab of coralline limestone.[[50]](#cite\_note-50) The north wall is in better shape; originally the entrance opened on a court, but the doorway was later closed off in the Tarxien phase, with altars set in the corners formed by the closure.[[51]](#cite\_note-51) East of this temple, a second monument was added in the Tarxien phase, with four apses and a central niche.[[52]](#cite\_note-52) Before the temples were built, the area had supported a village over a period of roughly twelve centuries. The oldest structure is the eleven-metre-long straight wall to the west of the temples' first entrance.[[53]](#cite\_note-TrumpSkorba-53) The deposit against it contained material from the first known human occupation of the island, the Għar Dalam phase.

Among the domestic deposits found in this material, which included charcoal and carbonised grain, there were several fragments of daub, accidentally baked.[[54]](#cite\_note-54) The charcoal fragments were then radiocarbon dated, and their age analysis stood at 4850 BC.[[53]](#cite\_note-TrumpSkorba-53) ### Ħaġar Qim [(/w/index.php?title=Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=13)] Main article: [Ħaġar Qim](/wiki/%C4%A6a%C4%A1ar\_Qim) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Hagar\_qim\_temples\_2.jpg/250px-Hagar\_qim\_temples\_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hagar\_qim\_temples\_2.jpg) The forecourt of Ħaġar Qim temple [Ħaġar Qim](/wiki/%C4%A6a%C4%A1ar\_Qim) stands on a ridge some two kilometers away from the villages of [Qrendi](/wiki/Qrendi) and [Siġġiewi](/wiki/Si%C4%A1%C4%A1iewi).[[55]](#cite\_note-55) Its builders used the soft [globigerina](/wiki/Globigerina) limestone that caps the ridge to construct the temple.[[56]](#cite\_note-56) One can clearly see the effects of this choice in the outer southern wall, where the great orthostats are exposed to the sea-winds. Here the temple has suffered from severe weathering and surface flaking over the centuries.[[57]](#cite\_note-57) The temple's façade is typical, with a trilithon entrance, a bench and orthostats.

It has a wide forecourt with a retaining wall, through which a passage runs through the middle of the building.[[58]](#cite\_note-58) This entrance passage and first court follow the common, though considerably modified, Maltese megalithic design.[[59]](#cite\_note-59) A separate entrance gives access to four enclosures, which are independent of each other and replace the north-westerly apse.[[60]](#cite\_note-60) ### Mnajdra [(/w/index.php?title=Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=14)] Main article: [Mnajdra](/wiki/Mnajdra) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Malta\_16\_Mnajdra.jpg/250px-Malta\_16\_Mnajdra.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_16\_Mnajdra.jpg) A trilithon at [Mnajdra](/wiki/Mnajdra) L-Imnajdra temples lies in a hollow 500 metres from Ħaġar Qim.[[61]](#cite\_note-61) It is another complex site in its own right, and it is centred on a near circular forecourt. Three adjacent temples overlook it from one side, while a terrace from the other separates it from a steep slope that runs down to the sea.[[62]](#cite\_note-62) The first buildings on the right are small irregular chambers, similar to the enclosures in Ħaġar Qim.[[63]](#cite\_note-63) Then there is a small trefoil temple, dating from the Ġgantija phase, with pitted decorations. Its unusual triple entrance was copied on a larger scale in the second temple.[[30]](#cite\_note-autogenerated58-30) The middle temple was actually the last to be built, inserted between the others in the Tarxien phase, after 3100 BC.[[64]](#cite\_note-64) It has four apses and a niche.

The third temple, built early in the Tarxien phase and so second in date, opens on the court at a lower level.[[65]](#cite\_note-65) It has a markedly concave façade, with a bench, orthostats and trilithon entrance. The southern temple is oriented astronomically aligned with the rising sun during [solstices](/wiki/Solstice) and [equinoxes](/wiki/Equinox); during the summer solstice the first rays of sunlight light up the edge of a decorated megalith between the first apses, while during the [winter solstice](/wiki/Winter\_solstice) the same effect occurs on a megalith in the opposite apse.[[66]](#cite\_note-66) During the equinox, the rays of the rising sun pass straight through the principal doorway to reach the innermost central niche.[[67]](#cite\_note-67) ### Tarxien [(/w/index.php?title=Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=15)] Main article: [Tarxien Temples](/wiki/Tarxien\_Temples) The Tarxien temple complex is found some 400 metres to the east of the [Hypogeum of Ħal-Saflieni](/wiki/Hypogeum\_of\_%C4%A6al-Saflieni).[[68]](#cite\_note-68) The three temples found here were seriously excavated in the early twentieth century by Temi Żammit.[[69]](#cite\_note-69) Unlike the other sites, this temple is bounded on all sides by modern urban development; however, this does not detract from its value.

One enters into the first great forecourt of the southern temple, marked by its rounded façade and a cistern, which is attributed to the temple.[[70]](#cite\_note-70) The earliest temple to the north-east was built between 3600 and 3200 BC; it consisted of two parallel sets of semi-circular apses, with a passage in the middle.[[71]](#cite\_note-71) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Malta\_Hal\_Tarxien\_BW\_2011-10-04\_12-41-17.JPG/250px-Malta\_Hal\_Tarxien\_BW\_2011-10-04\_12-41-17.JPG)](/wiki/File:Malta\_Hal\_Tarxien\_BW\_2011-10-04\_12-41-17.JPG) A carved relief at Tarxien temples The south and east temples were built in the Tarxien phase, between 3150 and 2500 BC. The second one has three parallel semi-circular apses, connected by a large passage; the third one has two parallel sets of apses with a passage in a direction parallel to that of the first temple. The first temple is solidly built with large stones, of which some are roughly dressed.[[72]](#cite\_note-72) The walls are laid with great accuracy, and are very imposing in their simplicity.[[73]](#cite\_note-73) The second temple is more elaborately constructed, the walls being finished with greater care, some of the standing slabs being decorated with flat raised spirals.[[74]](#cite\_note-74) In one of the chambers, two bulls and a sow are cut in low relief across one of the walls.[[75]](#cite\_note-75) The third temple has a carelessly-built frame, but most of its standing stones are richly decorated with carved patterns.

## Other sites [(/w/index.php?title=Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=16)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Borg\_in\_Nadur.jpg/250px-Borg\_in\_Nadur.jpg)](/wiki/File:Borg\_in\_Nadur.jpg) Borġ in-Nadur [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Bugibba\_hotel\_Dolmen\_Malta\_7.jpg/250px-Bugibba\_hotel\_Dolmen\_Malta\_7.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bugibba\_hotel\_Dolmen\_Malta\_7.jpg) Buġibba [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Malta\_-\_Naxxar\_-\_Triq\_l-Imdawra\_-\_Tal-Qadi\_Temple\_09\_ies.jpg/250px-Malta\_-\_Naxxar\_-\_Triq\_l-Imdawra\_-\_Tal-Qadi\_Temple\_09\_ies.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_-\_Naxxar\_-\_Triq\_l-Imdawra\_-\_Tal-Qadi\_Temple\_09\_ies.jpg) Tal-Qadi [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Malta\_-\_St.\_Paul%27s\_Bay\_-\_Xemxija\_Heritage\_Trail\_-\_Xemxija\_Temple\_2\_01\_ies.jpg/250px-Malta\_-\_St.\_Paul%27s\_Bay\_-\_Xemxija\_Heritage\_Trail\_-\_Xemxija\_Temple\_2\_01\_ies.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_-\_St.\_Paul%27s\_Bay\_-\_Xemxija\_Heritage\_Trail\_-\_Xemxija\_Temple\_2\_01\_ies.jpg) Xemxija Malta has various other megalithic temples and related sites apart from those included in the UNESCO World Heritage list.[[76]](#cite\_note-76) These include: \* [Borġ l-Imramma](/wiki/Bor%C4%A1\_l-Imramma)[[77]](#cite\_note-77) \* [Borġ in-Nadur](/wiki/Bor%C4%A1\_in-Nadur)[[78]](#cite\_note-MPT-78) \* [Buġibba](/wiki/Bu%C4%A1ibba\_Temple)[[79]](#cite\_note-79) \* [Debdieba](/wiki/Debdieba)[[80]](#cite\_note-80) \* [Kordin](/wiki/Kordin\_Temples) + [Kordin I](/wiki/Kordin\_Temples#Kordin\_I)[[81]](#cite\_note-81) + [Kordin II](/wiki/Kordin\_Temples#Kordin\_II)[[82]](#cite\_note-82) + [Kordin III](/wiki/Kordin\_Temples#Kordin\_III)[[83]](#cite\_note-83) \* [Ħal-Ġinwi Temples](/wiki/%C4%A6al-%C4%A0inwi\_Temples)[[84]](#cite\_note-84) \* [Qortin l-Imdawwar](/w/index.php?title=Qortin\_l-Imdawwar&action=edit&redlink=1)[[85]](#cite\_note-85) \* [Santa Verna](/wiki/Santa\_Verna)[[86]](#cite\_note-86) \* [Ta' Marżiena](/w/index.php?title=Ta%27\_Mar%C5%BCiena&action=edit&redlink=1)[[87]](#cite\_note-87) \* [Ta' Raddiena](/w/index.php?title=Ta%27\_Raddiena&action=edit&redlink=1)[[88]](#cite\_note-88) \* [Tal-Qadi](/wiki/Tal-Qadi\_Temple)[[89]](#cite\_note-89) \* [Tas-Silġ](/wiki/Tas-Sil%C4%A1)[[90]](#cite\_note-90) \* [Xemxija](/w/index.php?title=Xemxija\_Temple&action=edit&redlink=1)[[91]](#cite\_note-91) + Xemxija I + Xemxija II \* [Xrobb l-Għaġin](/wiki/Xrobb\_l-G%C4%A7a%C4%A1in\_Temple)[[92]](#cite\_note-92) Some of these sites were excavated but afterwards reburied, such as the Debdieba site. Others, such as Kordin I and II, were destroyed. The other temples usually consist of only a few scattered megaliths or remains, but some such as Buġibba Temple (which is now in the grounds of a hotel) are in better condition. A few sites, such as Ta' Marżiena, have never been excavated. Tas-Silġ contains few megalithic remains, but many more Bronze Age and later remains since the site was used until at least the ninth century AD. Apart from these, cart ruts were found at [Misraħ Għar il-Kbir](/wiki/Misra%C4%A7\_G%C4%A7ar\_il-Kbir), but these may or may not date from the temple period.

A submerged site known as [Ġebel ġol-Baħar](/wiki/%C4%A0ebel\_%C4%A1ol-Ba%C4%A7ar) possibly exists off the coast of Malta, but it is not proven to be a megalithic temple. ## See also [(/w/index.php?title=Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=17)] \* [List of World Heritage Sites in Southern Europe](/wiki/List\_of\_World\_Heritage\_Sites\_in\_Southern\_Europe) \* [List of oldest known surviving buildings](/wiki/List\_of\_oldest\_known\_surviving\_buildings) \* [Megalithic architectural elements](/wiki/Megalithic\_architectural\_elements) \* [Neolithic architecture](/wiki/Neolithic\_architecture) \* [Tombs of Malta](/wiki/Tombs\_of\_Malta) \* [Xagħra Stone Circle](/wiki/Xag%C4%A7ra\_Stone\_Circle) \* [List of largest monoliths](/wiki/List\_of\_largest\_monoliths) ## References [(/w/index.php?title=Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=18)] 1. \*\*[^](#cite\_ref-1)\*\*["21 World Heritage Sites you have probably never heard of"](https://www.telegraph.co.uk/travel/travelnews/10999939/21-World-Heritage-Sites-you-have-probably-never-heard-of.html). From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia (Redirected from [Maltese architecture](/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_architecture&redirect=no)) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Sudika\_Mnajdra\_porthole.jpg/250px-Sudika\_Mnajdra\_porthole.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sudika\_Mnajdra\_porthole.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Kapella\_Santa\_Cilja.jpeg/250px-Kapella\_Santa\_Cilja.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Kapella\_Santa\_Cilja.jpeg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Parish-church-attard.jpg/250px-Parish-church-attard.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parish-church-attard.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Castille\_Palace\_01.jpg/250px-Castille\_Palace\_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Castille\_Palace\_01.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Valletta\_St\_Johns\_square\_Malta\_2014\_2.jpg/250px-Valletta\_St\_Johns\_square\_Malta\_2014\_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Valletta\_St\_Johns\_square\_Malta\_2014\_2.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Sliema\_Malta\_Colored-Balconies-01.jpg/250px-Sliema\_Malta\_Colored-Balconies-01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sliema\_Malta\_Colored-Balconies-01.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Balluta\_B.jpg/250px-Balluta\_B.jpg)](/wiki/File:Balluta\_B.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Piet%C3%A0\_Malta\_Buildings\_19.jpg/250px-Piet%C3%A0\_Malta\_Buildings\_19.jpg)](/wiki/File:Piet%C3%A0\_Malta\_Buildings\_19.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Manikata.jpg/250px-Manikata.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manikata.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Ta%27\_Xbiex%2C\_Malta.JPG/250px-Ta%27\_Xbiex%2C\_Malta.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ta%27\_Xbiex,\_Malta.JPG) Examples of Maltese architecture from different periods: the prehistoric [Mnajdra](/wiki/Mnajdra) Temples, the medieval [Santa Cecilia Chapel](/wiki/Santa\_Cecilia\_Chapel), the Renaissance [parish church of Attard](/wiki/Parish\_Church\_of\_St.\_Mary,\_Attard), the Baroque [Auberge de Castille](/wiki/Auberge\_de\_Castille), the neoclassical [Main Guard](/wiki/Main\_Guard\_(Valletta)), traditional townhouses in [Sliema](/wiki/Sliema), the Art Nouveau [Balluta Buildings](/wiki/Balluta\_Buildings), the modernist [Rediffusion House](/wiki/Rediffusion\_House), the critical regionalist [Manikata church](/wiki/Parish\_Church\_of\_St\_Joseph,\_Manikata) and contemporary apartments in [Ta' Xbiex](/wiki/Ta%27\_Xbiex) \*\*Maltese architecture\*\* has its origins in prehistory, and some of the oldest free-standing structures on Earth – a series of [megalithic temples](/wiki/Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta) – can be found on [Malta](/wiki/Malta).[[1]](#cite\_note-1) The islands were colonized by the [Phoenicians](/wiki/Phoenicia) and later the [Romans](/wiki/Ancient\_Rome), who established the cities of [Melite](/wiki/Melite\_(ancient\_city)) and [Gaulos](/wiki/Victoria,\_Gozo). Although these were substantial settlements and are known to have had numerous temples, churches and palaces, few remains have survived apart from some architectural fragments.[[2]](#cite\_note-galea-2) After the [fall of the Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Fall\_of\_the\_Western\_Roman\_Empire), Malta became part of the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine\_Empire), before falling to the Arabs in 870. Virtually no examples of Byzantine or Arab architecture have survived, although the Arabs left a significant influence on Maltese vernacular architecture which remained popular in subsequent centuries.

Malta became part of the [County](/wiki/County\_of\_Sicily) and later the [Kingdom of Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom\_of\_Sicily) in 1091, and [Norman architecture](/wiki/Norman\_architecture) and other European styles were introduced to the island. Relatively few examples of medieval architecture have survived, including a few buildings in [Mdina](/wiki/Mdina) and the [Cittadella](/wiki/Cittadella\_(Gozo)), as well as several chapels in the Maltese countryside.[[2]](#cite\_note-galea-2) Maltese architecture flourished while the islands were [under the rule](/wiki/History\_of\_Malta\_under\_the\_Order\_of\_Saint\_John) of the [Order of St. John](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller) from 1530 to 1798. The Hospitallers introduced [Renaissance architecture](/wiki/Renaissance\_architecture) to Malta in the mid-16th century, with the [Baroque style](/wiki/Maltese\_Baroque\_architecture) becoming popular about a century later. The two and a half centuries of Hospitaller rule saw the establishment of new settlements (most notably the capital [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta)) and the construction of many churches, palaces and public buildings.

The Order also built [bastioned](/wiki/Star\_fort)[fortifications](/wiki/Fortifications\_of\_Malta#Hospitaller\_fortifications\_(1530–1798)) around the main cities, apart from a series of coastal and inland defences.[[3]](#cite\_note-galea2-3) After Malta became part of the [British Empire](/wiki/British\_Empire) in 1800, [Neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassical\_architecture) and [Neo-Gothic architecture](/wiki/Gothic\_Revival\_architecture) were introduced, and they were the predominant styles of the 19th century.[[3]](#cite\_note-galea2-3) Several styles left an influence on Maltese architecture in the first half of the 20th century, including [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art\_Nouveau), [Art Deco](/wiki/Art\_Deco), [Italian futurism](/wiki/Futurist\_architecture), [rationalism](/wiki/Rationalism\_(architecture)) and [modernism](/wiki/Modernist\_architecture).[[4]](#cite\_note-4) Malta experienced a building boom after [World War II](/wiki/World\_War\_II), which increased after independence in 1964.[[5]](#cite\_note-5) The modernist style remained popular, but new styles such as [critical regionalism](/wiki/Critical\_regionalism) were also introduced. ## Prehistoric architecture [(/w/index.php?title=Architecture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=1)] Main article: [Megalithic Temples of Malta](/wiki/Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/%C4%A0gantija\_2014-007.jpg/250px-%C4%A0gantija\_2014-007.jpg)](/wiki/File:%C4%A0gantija\_2014-007.jpg) [Ġgantija](/wiki/%C4%A0gantija) The [Ġgantija](/wiki/%C4%A0gantija) temples (two sites) were listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1980.[[6]](#cite\_note-Unesco-6) In 1992, the UNESCO Committee further extended the existing listing to include five other megalithic temple sites. These are [Ħaġar Qim](/wiki/%C4%A6a%C4%A1ar\_Qim) (in [Qrendi](/wiki/Qrendi)), [Mnajdra](/wiki/Mnajdra) (in Qrendi), [Ta' Ħaġrat Temples](/wiki/Ta%27\_%C4%A6a%C4%A1rat\_Temples) (in [Mġarr](/wiki/M%C4%A1arr)), [Skorba Temples](/wiki/Skorba\_Temples) (in [Żebbiegħ](/w/index.php?title=%C5%BBebbieg%C4%A7&action=edit&redlink=1)) and Tarxien Temples (in [Tarxien](/wiki/Tarxien)).[[6]](#cite\_note-Unesco-6) Nowadays, the sites are managed by [Heritage Malta](/wiki/Heritage\_Malta), while ownership of the surrounding lands varies from site to site.[[7]](#cite\_note-7)[[8]](#cite\_note-8) Apart from these, there are other megalithic temples in Malta which are not included in the UNESCO World Heritage list.

## Roman architecture [(/w/index.php?title=Architecture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=2)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Sudika\_Rabat\_Domus\_Romana.jpg/250px-Sudika\_Rabat\_Domus\_Romana.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sudika\_Rabat\_Domus\_Romana.jpg) The mosaic of the [peristyle](/wiki/Peristyle) of [Rabat](/wiki/Rabat,\_Malta)'s [Domvs Romana](/wiki/Domvs\_Romana) The [Domvs Romana](/wiki/Domvs\_Romana) ([Latin](/wiki/Latin) for "[Roman](/wiki/Ancient\_Romans) House"), is a ruined Roman-era house located on the boundary between [Mdina](/wiki/Mdina) and [Rabat](/wiki/Rabat,\_Malta), [Malta](/wiki/Malta). It was built in the 1st century BC as an aristocratic town house (\*[domus](/wiki/Domus)\*) within the Roman city of [Melite](/wiki/Melite\_(ancient\_city)). ## Arab architecture [(/w/index.php?title=Architecture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=3)]   ---   \*\*This section is empty.\*\* You can help by [adding to it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Architecture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=). \*(December 2019)\*    ---   \* [Giovanni Attard](/wiki/Giovanni\_Attard) (c. 1570–1636) \* [Giovanni Barbara](/wiki/Giovanni\_Barbara) (1642–1728) \* [Andrea Belli](/wiki/Andrea\_Belli) (1703–1772) \* [Giuseppe Bonavia](/wiki/Giuseppe\_Bonavia) (1821–1885) \* [Giuseppe Bonici](/wiki/Giuseppe\_Bonici) (1707–1779) \* [Antonio Cachia](/wiki/Antonio\_Cachia) (1739–1813) \* [Domenico Cachia](/wiki/Domenico\_Cachia) (c. 1690–1761) \* [Michele Cachia](/wiki/Michele\_Cachia) (1760–1839) \* [Girolamo Cassar](/wiki/Girolamo\_Cassar) (c. 1520–c. 1592) \* [Vittorio Cassar](/wiki/Vittorio\_Cassar) (c. 1550–c. 1609) \* [Cesar Castellani](/wiki/Cesar\_Castellani) (died 1905) \* [Ġużè Damato](/wiki/%C4%A0u%C5%BC%C3%A8\_Damato) (1886–1963) \* [Tommaso Dingli](/wiki/Tommaso\_Dingli) (1591–1666) \* [Salvatore Ellul](/wiki/Salvatore\_Ellul) (1891–1961) \* [Richard England](/wiki/Richard\_England\_(architect)) (born 1937) \* [Antonio Falzon](/wiki/Antonio\_Falzon) (16th century) \* [Lorenzo Gafà](/wiki/Lorenzo\_Gaf%C3%A0) (1639–1703) \* [Emanuele Luigi Galizia](/wiki/Emanuele\_Luigi\_Galizia) (1830–1907) \* [Carlo Gimach](/wiki/Carlo\_Gimach) (1651–1730) \* [Giorgio Grognet de Vassé](/wiki/Giorgio\_Grognet\_de\_Vass%C3%A9) (1774–1862) \* [Joseph G. From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia              After [independence](/wiki/History\_of\_Malta) from the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United\_Kingdom) in 1964, [Malta](/wiki/Malta) followed a policy of close co-operation with [NATO](/wiki/NATO) countries. Since 1971, the country sought relations with the rest of the world, including communist countries in Eastern Europe and the non-aligned countries. After substantially increased financial contributions from several NATO countries (including the United States), the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal\_Navy) remained in the [Malta Dockyard](/wiki/Malta\_Dockyard) until 1979. Following their departure, Malta charted a new course of neutrality and became an active member of the [Non-Aligned Movement](/wiki/Non-Aligned\_Movement). The country [joined the Non-Aligned Movement in 1973](/wiki/Malta\_and\_the\_Non-Aligned\_Movement) as the third [European](/wiki/Europe) member state after [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus\_and\_the\_Non-Aligned\_Movement) and [SFR Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia\_and\_the\_Non-Aligned\_Movement).[[1]](#cite\_note-Bishku-1) Malta is an active participant in the [United Nations](/wiki/United\_Nations), the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth\_of\_Nations), the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council\_of\_Europe), [OSCE](/wiki/Organization\_for\_Security\_and\_Co-operation\_in\_Europe), and various other international organisations. In these forums, Malta has frequently expressed its concern for the peace and economic development of the [Mediterranean region](/wiki/Mediterranean\_region). On May 1, 2004, Malta withdrew from the Non-Aligned Movement and became a [full member](/wiki/Member\_state\_of\_the\_European\_Union) of the [European Union](/wiki/European\_Union), with which it had an association agreement since 1971. It was one of ten [new members which joined on that date](/wiki/2004\_enlargement\_of\_the\_European\_Union). The [Ministry for Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Ministry\_of\_Foreign\_Affairs\_(Malta)), at [Palazzo Parisio](/wiki/Palazzo\_Parisio\_(Valletta)), oversees the direction of Maltese foreign policy. The country has close relations with most sovereign countries, with an emphasis on increased trade and foreign direct investment.

[(/w/index.php?title=Foreign\_relations\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=3)]  Formal Relations Began   ---   See [2004 enlargement of the European Union](/wiki/2004\_enlargement\_of\_the\_European\_Union) Malta joined the [European Union](/wiki/European\_Union) as a full member on 1 May 2004.

[NATO](/wiki/NATO)  ### Africa [(/w/index.php?title=Foreign\_relations\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=4)]  Formal Relations Began   ---   4 November 1965   21 October 1976   27 February 1974   11 April 2006   15 June 1965   20 December 1967   21 July 2004  ### Americas [(/w/index.php?title=Foreign\_relations\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=5)]  Formal Relations Began   ---   28 May 1975   23 June 1975   21 December 1964   11 December 1989   12 March 1976   29 October 1975   10 December 2004   24 September 2009   21 September 1964  ### Asia [(/w/index.php?title=Foreign\_relations\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=6)]  Formal Relations Began   ---   7 June 1993   31 January 1972   26 February 1993   10 March 1965   \* Iran is accredited to Malta from its embassy in Rome, Italy. \* Malta is represented in Iran through a non-resident ambassador based in [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta) (in the Foreign Ministry). [Israel](/wiki/Israel)  \* Malta has an embassy in [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel\_Aviv).[[21]](#cite\_note-israelEmb-21) \* Both countries are full members of the [Union for the Mediterranean](/wiki/Union\_for\_the\_Mediterranean). \* See also [Maltese Jews](/wiki/Maltese\_Jews). \* In December 2015, Israel singled Malta and five other EU countries for sanctions because they supported a decision that goods made in Jewish communities in the West Bank and the Golan Heights should be specially marked.[[151]](#cite\_note-151)  [Kyrgyzstan](/wiki/Kyrgyzstan)  \* Both countries established diplomatic relations on February 19, 1993.[[152]](#cite\_note-152) \* Both countries are full members of the [Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe](/wiki/Organization\_for\_Security\_and\_Co-operation\_in\_Europe). [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan)  \* Malta is represented in Pakistan through its embassy in Beijing (China) and an honorary consulate in [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi).

Pakistan is represented in Malta through its embassy in [Tripoli](/wiki/Tripoli,\_Libya) ([Libya](/wiki/Libya)) and an honorary consulate in [Marsa](/wiki/Marsa\_(city)). Both countries are full members of the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth\_of\_Nations). [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi\_Arabia)  \* Malta has an embassy in [Riyadh](/wiki/Riyadh) and an honorary consulate in [Jeddah](/wiki/Jeddah).[[153]](#cite\_note-153) \* Saudi Arabia is represented through its embassy in Rome (Italy).[[154]](#cite\_note-154)  [South Korea](/wiki/South\_Korea)  The establishment of diplomatic relations between the Republic of Korea and Malta began on April 2, 1965. \* On the occasion of the 50th anniversary of diplomatic relations] in 2015 President [Park Geun-hye](/wiki/Park\_Geun-hye) exchanged celebratory messages with Maltese President [Marie Louise Coleiro Preca](/wiki/Marie\_Louise\_Coleiro\_Preca) \* Bilateral Trade in 2014 + Exports 1,014,000,000 US dollars + Imports 514,000,000 US dollars \* Bilateral Investments(2014): + South Korea's investment in Malta was 31,490,000 US dollars + Malta's investment in South Korea was 5,883,000,000 US dollars[[156]](#cite\_note-156)  [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan)  \* Both countries established diplomatic relations on September 25, 2007.[[157]](#cite\_note-157) \* Both countries are full members of the [Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe](/wiki/Organization\_for\_Security\_and\_Co-operation\_in\_Europe). [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey)  See [Malta–Turkey relations](/wiki/Malta%E2%80%93Turkey\_relations) \* Malta has an embassy in [Ankara](/wiki/Ankara) and a consulate-general in [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul). \* Turkey has an embassy in Valletta. \* Both countries are full members of the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council\_of\_Europe), the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World\_Trade\_Organization) and the [Union for the Mediterranean](/wiki/Union\_for\_the\_Mediterranean). [Russia](/wiki/Russia)  See [Malta–Russia relations](/wiki/Malta%E2%80%93Russia\_relations) \* Malta has an [embassy in Moscow](/wiki/Embassy\_of\_Malta\_in\_Moscow) and an honorary consulate in [Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Saint\_Petersburg).[[195]](#cite\_note-195) \* Russia has an embassy in [San Ġwann](/wiki/San\_%C4%A0wann).[[196]](#cite\_note-196) \* [Coordination Board of Russian Compatriots in Malta](http://www.russian-malta.com)[Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20201006115949/http://russian-malta.com/) October 6, 2020, at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback\_Machine)  [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia)  See [Malta–Serbia relations](/wiki/Malta%E2%80%93Serbia\_relations) \* Malta is represented in Serbia through a non-resident ambassador based in [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta) (in the Foreign Ministry).[[197]](#cite\_note-197) \* Serbia is represented in Malta through its embassy in Rome (Italy) and through an honorary consulate in [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta).[[198]](#cite\_note-198) \* Malta is an EU [member](/wiki/Member\_state\_of\_the\_European\_Union) and Serbia is an EU [candidate](/wiki/Accession\_of\_Serbia\_to\_the\_European\_Union). [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia)  See [Malta–Slovakia relations](/wiki/Malta%E2%80%93Slovakia\_relations) \* Malta is represented in Slovakia through a non-resident ambassador based in [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta) (in the Foreign Ministry).[[199]](#cite\_note-199) \* Slovakia is represented in Malta through its embassy in Rome (Italy) and an honorary consulate in [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta).[[200]](#cite\_note-200)[[201]](#cite\_note-201) \* Both countries are full members of the [European Union](/wiki/European\_Union). \* The foreign relations between Malta and Slovakia are not very intensive. Foreign policy of Malta is concentrated rather on cooperation with [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria), [Tunis](/wiki/Tunis), [Libya](/wiki/Libya) and [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt).

There is, however, 41 years lasting cooperation between those two countries.[[202]](#cite\_note-autogenerated1-202) \* In 2001 Slovak prime minister [Mikuláš Dzurinda](/wiki/Mikul%C3%A1%C5%A1\_Dzurinda) visited Malta at the invitation of Maltese premier [Edward Fenech Adami](/wiki/Edward\_Fenech\_Adami), and negotiated the integration of both countries into European Union and economic cooperation between Malta and Slovakia.[[203]](#cite\_note-203) \* In 2008 Slovakian agency SARIA signed a treaty of reciprocal assistance related to third-world countries, with [Malta Enterprise](/wiki/Malta\_Enterprise).[[202]](#cite\_note-autogenerated1-202) \* Slovak prime minister Robert Fico visited Maltese capital [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta) in 2008.[[204]](#cite\_note-204)  [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia)  See [Malta–Slovenia relations](/wiki/Malta%E2%80%93Slovenia\_relations) \* Malta has an honorary consulate in [Ljubljana](/wiki/Ljubljana).[[205]](#cite\_note-205) \* Slovenia is represented through its embassy in Rome, Italy and through an honorary consulate in [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta).[[206]](#cite\_note-206) \* Both countries are members of the [European Union](/wiki/European\_Union) and the [Union for the Mediterranean](/wiki/Union\_for\_the\_Mediterranean). [Spain](/wiki/Spain)  See [Malta–Spain relations](/wiki/Malta%E2%80%93Spain\_relations) \* Malta has an embassy in [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid) and 5 honorary consulates (in [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona), [Palma de Mallorca](/wiki/Palma\_de\_Mallorca), [Santander](/wiki/Santander,\_Cantabria), [Seville](/wiki/Seville) and [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia,\_Spain)).[[207]](#cite\_note-207) \* Spain has an embassy in [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta).[[208]](#cite\_note-208) \* Both countries are full members of the [European Union](/wiki/European\_Union) and of the [Union for the Mediterranean](/wiki/Union\_for\_the\_Mediterranean).

[Sweden](/wiki/Sweden)  \* Malta is represented in Sweden through its embassy in [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen) ([Denmark](/wiki/Denmark)) and through 2 honorary consulates (in [Malmö](/wiki/Malm%C3%B6) and [Sundsvall](/wiki/Sundsvall)).[[209]](#cite\_note-209) \* Sweden is represented in Malta through a non-resident ambassador (based in the Foreign Ministry in [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm)) and through an honorary consulate in [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta).[[210]](#cite\_note-210) \* Both countries are full members of the [European Union](/wiki/European\_Union). [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine)  See [Malta–Ukraine relations](/wiki/Malta%E2%80%93Ukraine\_relations) \* The Maltese embassy in Moscow (Russia) is also accredited as a non-resident embassy to Ukraine. \* Ukraine is represented in Malta through its embassy in Rome (Italy). [United Kingdom](/wiki/United\_Kingdom)  See [Malta–United Kingdom relations](/wiki/Malta%E2%80%93United\_Kingdom\_relations) Malta established [diplomatic relations with the United Kingdom](/wiki/Foreign\_relations\_of\_the\_United\_Kingdom) on 21 September 1964.[[211]](#cite\_note-211)[\*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability)\*] \* Malta maintains a [high commission](/wiki/High\_Commission\_of\_Malta,\_London) in [London](/wiki/London).[[212]](#cite\_note-212) \* The United Kingdom is accredited to Malta through its [high commission](/wiki/British\_High\_Commission) in Valletta.[[213]](#cite\_note-213) The UK governed [Malta](/wiki/Crown\_Colony\_of\_Malta) from 1800 until 1964, when it achieved full independence. Both countries share common membership of the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth\_of\_Nations), the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council\_of\_Europe), the [International Criminal Court](/wiki/International\_Criminal\_Court), the [OSCE](/wiki/Organization\_for\_Security\_and\_Co-operation\_in\_Europe), and the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World\_Trade\_Organization). Bilaterally the two countries have a Bilateral Cooperation Framework,[[214]](#cite\_note-214) and a Double Taxation Convention.[[215]](#cite\_note-215)  Country  Notes  ---  ---  [Australia](/wiki/Australia)  see [Australia–Malta relations](/wiki/Australia%E2%80%93Malta\_relations) Both countries have full embassy level diplomatic relations since 1967. Australia has a High Commission in [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta).

Malta has a High Commission in [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra),[[3]](#cite\_note-australiaHC-3) 2 Consulates-General (in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne) and Sydney[[3]](#cite\_note-australiaHC-3)), and 4 honorary consulates (in [Adelaide](/wiki/Adelaide), [Ascot Vale](/wiki/Ascot\_Vale), [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne) and [Perth](/wiki/Perth)).[\*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed)\*] Maltese president [Eddie Fenech Adami](/wiki/Eddie\_Fenech\_Adami) embarked on a state visit to Australia in February 2009, where he met with Governor-General [Quentin Bryce](/wiki/Quentin\_Bryce) and Australian Prime Minister [Kevin Rudd](/wiki/Kevin\_Rudd). During an official dinner in honour of the Maltese President, Kevin Rudd declared "Australia would not be as complete without Malta".[[216]](#cite\_note-216) In the same month, Maltese Deputy Prime Minister and Foreign Affairs Minister [Tonio Borg](/wiki/Tonio\_Borg) visited Australia for discussions with Australian foreign minister [Stephen Smith](/wiki/Stephen\_Smith\_(Australian\_politician)), where the commercial relationship between the two countries were discussed, in particular the signing of a contract between a [Perth](/wiki/Perth)-based shipbuilding company and the [Maltese armed forces](/wiki/Armed\_Forces\_of\_Malta) for the construction and delivery of four inshore patrol craft.[[217]](#cite\_note-217)  Foreign Affairs.
