For other uses, see [Valletta (disambiguation)](/wiki/Valletta\_(disambiguation)). Capital city and local council in Port Region, Malta           [Malta](/wiki/Malta)  [Region](/wiki/Regions\_of\_Malta)   [Southern Harbour District](/wiki/Southern\_Harbour\_District)  Established   18 March 1571  Founded by   [Floriana](/wiki/Floriana)  Government  • [Mayor](/wiki/List\_of\_mayors\_of\_places\_in\_Malta)    0.61 km2 (0.24 sq mi)  • Urban   56 m (184 ft)  Population (Jan. 2019)  • [Capital city](/wiki/Capital\_city) and [local council](/wiki/Local\_councils\_of\_Malta)   8,500/km2 (22,000/sq mi)  • [Urban](/wiki/Urban\_area)   Belti (m), Beltija (f), Beltin (pl)  [Time zone](/wiki/Time\_zone)   [UTC+2](/wiki/UTC%2B2) ([CEST](/wiki/Central\_European\_Summer\_Time))  [Postal code](/wiki/Postal\_codes\_in\_Malta)   356  [ISO 3166 code](/wiki/ISO\_3166)   [St. Paul, the Apostle](/wiki/Paul\_the\_Apostle)[St. Dominic](/wiki/Saint\_Dominic)[Our Lady of Mount Carmel](/wiki/Our\_Lady\_of\_Mount\_Carmel)[St. Augustine](/wiki/Augustine\_of\_Hippo)  [Day of festa](/wiki/Public\_holidays\_in\_Malta#Traditional\_Feasts)   [Official website](http://www.cityofvalletta.org/)   [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/World\_Heritage\_Site)  Official name   Cultural: i, vi  Reference   1980 (4th [Session](/wiki/World\_Heritage\_Committee))  Area  \*\*Valletta\*\* ([/vəˈlɛtə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English)[ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q1860\_(eng)-Vealhurl-Valletta.wav);[[3]](#cite\_note-3)[Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_language): \*il-Belt Valletta\*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal\_translation)'the city [of] Valletta', pronounced[[ɪlˈbɛltvɐˈlːɛtːɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Maltese)), also known as \*\*Città Umilissima\*\*[[4]](#cite\_note-4) (transl. the Humblest City), is the [capital city](/wiki/Capital\_city) of [Malta](/wiki/Malta) and one of its 68 [council areas](/wiki/Local\_councils\_of\_Malta). Located between the [Grand Harbour](/wiki/Grand\_Harbour) to the east and [Marsamxett Harbour](/wiki/Marsamxett\_Harbour) to the west, its population as of 2021 was 5,157.[[5]](#cite\_note-5) As Malta's capital city, it is a commercial centre for shopping, bars, dining, and café life.

It is also the southernmost capital of [Europe](/wiki/Europe),[[6]](#cite\_note-6)[[note 1]](#cite\_note-Cyprus-7) and, at just 0.61 square kilometres (0.24 sq mi), it is the [European Union](/wiki/European\_Union)'s smallest capital city.[[7]](#cite\_note-8)[[8]](#cite\_note-9) Valletta's 16th-century buildings were constructed by the [Knights Hospitaller](/wiki/Hospitaller\_Malta). The city was named after the Frenchman [Jean Parisot de Valette](/wiki/Jean\_Parisot\_de\_Valette), who succeeded in defending the island against an Ottoman invasion during the [Great Siege of Malta](/wiki/Great\_Siege\_of\_Malta). The city is [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque\_architecture) in character, with elements of [Mannerist](/wiki/Mannerist\_architecture#Mannerist\_architecture), [Neo-Classical](/wiki/Neoclassical\_architecture) and [Modern architecture](/wiki/Modern\_architecture), though the [Second World War](/wiki/Second\_World\_War) left major scars on the city, particularly the destruction of the [Royal Opera House](/wiki/Royal\_Opera\_House,\_Valletta). The city was officially recognised as a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World\_Heritage\_Site) by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO) in 1980.[[9]](#cite\_note-unesco-10) The city has 320 monuments, all within an area of 0.55 square kilometres (0.21 sq mi), making it one of the most concentrated historic areas in the world.[[9]](#cite\_note-unesco-10)[[10]](#cite\_note-11) Sometimes called an "open-air museum",[[11]](#cite\_note-12) Valletta was chosen as the [European Capital of Culture](/wiki/European\_Capital\_of\_Culture) for 2018. Valletta was also listed as the sunniest city in Europe in 2016.[[12]](#cite\_note-13)[[13]](#cite\_note-14) The city is noted for [its fortifications](/wiki/Fortifications\_of\_Valletta), consisting of [bastions](/wiki/Bastion), [curtains](/wiki/Curtain\_wall\_(fortification)) and [cavaliers](/wiki/Cavalier\_(fortification)), along with the beauty of its [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque) palaces, gardens and churches.

## History [(/w/index.php?title=Valletta&action=edit&section=1)] See also: [Mount Sciberras](/wiki/Mount\_Sciberras) and [Sciberras Peninsula](/wiki/Sciberras\_Peninsula) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Malta\_-\_Valletta\_-\_Triq\_il-Merkanti\_12\_ies.jpg/250px-Malta\_-\_Valletta\_-\_Triq\_il-Merkanti\_12\_ies.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_-\_Valletta\_-\_Triq\_il-Merkanti\_12\_ies.jpg) Former mural at \*[Is-Suq tal-Belt](/wiki/Is-Suq\_tal-Belt)\* illustrating the city's construction The peninsula was previously called \*Xagħret Mewwija\* (Mu' awiya – Meuia; named during the Arab period[[14]](#cite\_note-15))[[15]](#cite\_note-16)[[16]](#cite\_note-17) or Ħal Newwija.[[17]](#cite\_note-18) Mewwija refers to a sheltered place.[[18]](#cite\_note-19) Some authors state that the extreme end of the peninsula was known as Xebb ir-Ras (Sheb point), of which name origins from the lighthouse on site.[[19]](#cite\_note-20)[[20]](#cite\_note-BRILL-21) A family which surely owned land became known as \*Sceberras\*, now a Maltese surname as Sciberras.[[21]](#cite\_note-22) At one point the entire peninsula became known as Sceberras. Historical affiliations > [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Flag\_of\_the\_Order\_of\_St.\_John\_%28various%29.svg/40px-Flag\_of\_the\_Order\_of\_St.\_John\_%28various%29.svg.png)](/wiki/Order\_of\_Knights\_of\_the\_Hospital\_of\_Saint\_John\_of\_Jerusalem)[Hospitaller Malta](/wiki/Hospitaller\_Malta) 1566–1798[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Flag\_of\_France.svg/40px-Flag\_of\_France.svg.png)](/wiki/France)[French Republic](/wiki/French\_occupation\_of\_Malta) 1798–1800[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/ae/Flag\_of\_the\_United\_Kingdom.svg/40px-Flag\_of\_the\_United\_Kingdom.svg.png)](/wiki/United\_Kingdom)[Protectorate of Malta](/wiki/Malta\_Protectorate) 1800–1813[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Flag\_of\_Malta\_%281943%E2%80%931964%29.svg/40px-Flag\_of\_Malta\_%281943%E2%80%931964%29.svg.png)](/wiki/Malta)[Crown Colony of Malta](/wiki/Crown\_Colony\_of\_Malta) 1813–1964[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Flag\_of\_Malta.svg/40px-Flag\_of\_Malta.svg.png)](/wiki/Malta)[State of Malta](/wiki/State\_of\_Malta) 1964–1974[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Flag\_of\_Malta.svg/40px-Flag\_of\_Malta.svg.png)](/wiki/Malta)[Republic of Malta](/wiki/Malta) 1974–present Recent scholarly studies have however shown that the Xeberras phrase is of Punic origin and means 'the headland' and 'the middle peninsula' as it actually is.[[22]](#cite\_note-Article-23) ### Order of Saint John [(/w/index.php?title=Valletta&action=edit&section=2)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/DetalleSiegeMalta.jpg/250px-DetalleSiegeMalta.jpg)](/wiki/File:DetalleSiegeMalta.jpg) The Ottoman army bombs the Knights' Three Cities from the peninsula of Sciberras during the 1565 [Great Siege](/wiki/Great\_Siege\_of\_Malta). [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Malte%2C\_La\_Valette%2C\_co-cath%C3%A9drale\_St\_Jean.jpg/250px-Malte%2C\_La\_Valette%2C\_co-cath%C3%A9drale\_St\_Jean.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malte,\_La\_Valette,\_co-cath%C3%A9drale\_St\_Jean.jpg) The nave of [Saint John's Co-Cathedral](/wiki/Saint\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Grandmaster%27s\_palace%2C\_Valletta.jpg/250px-Grandmaster%27s\_palace%2C\_Valletta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grandmaster%27s\_palace,\_Valletta.jpg) [Grandmaster's Palace](/wiki/Grandmaster%27s\_Palace\_(Valletta)) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/GrandHarbourValletta1801.jpg/250px-GrandHarbourValletta1801.jpg)](/wiki/File:GrandHarbourValletta1801.jpg) Valletta and the [Grand Harbour](/wiki/Grand\_Harbour)c.

1801 The building of a city on the Sciberras Peninsula had been proposed by the [Order of Saint John](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller) as early as 1524.[[23]](#cite\_note-about-24) Back then, the only building on the peninsula was a small watchtower[[24]](#cite\_note-25) dedicated to [Erasmus of Formia](/wiki/Erasmus\_of\_Formia) (Saint Elmo), which had been built in 1488.[[25]](#cite\_note-26) In 1552, the [Aragonese](/wiki/Crown\_of\_Aragon) watchtower was demolished and the larger [Fort Saint Elmo](/wiki/Fort\_Saint\_Elmo) was built in its place.[[26]](#cite\_note-cityhistory-27) In the [Great Siege](/wiki/Great\_Siege\_of\_Malta) of 1565, Fort Saint Elmo fell to the [Ottomans](/wiki/Ottoman\_Empire), but the Order eventually won the siege with the help of Sicilian reinforcements. The victorious Grand Master, [Jean de Valette](/wiki/Jean\_Parisot\_de\_Valette), immediately set out to build a new fortified city on the Sciberras Peninsula to fortify the Order's position in Malta and bind the Knights to the island. The city took his name and was called \*La Valletta\*.[[27]](#cite\_note-28) The Grand Master asked the European kings and princes for help, receiving a lot of assistance because of the increased fame of the Order after its victory in the Great Siege. [Pope Pius V](/wiki/Pope\_Pius\_V) sent his military architect, [Francesco Laparelli](/wiki/Francesco\_Laparelli), to design the new city, while [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip\_II\_of\_Spain) sent substantial monetary aid. The [foundation stone](/wiki/Cornerstone) of the city was laid by Grand Master de Valette on 28 March 1566.

He placed the first stone in what later became [Our Lady of Victories Church](/wiki/Our\_Lady\_of\_Victories\_Church,\_Valletta).[[28]](#cite\_note-journal-29) In his book \*Dell'Istoria della Sacra Religione et Illustrissima Militia di San Giovanni Gierosolimitano\* (English: The History of the Sacred Religion and Illustrious Militia of St John of Jerusalem), written between 1594 and 1602, [Giacomo Bosio](/wiki/Giacomo\_Bosio) writes that, when the cornerstone of Valletta was placed, a group of [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_people) elders said: "\*Iegi zimen en fel wardia col sceber raba iesue uquie\*" (Which in modern Maltese reads, "\*Jiġi żmien li fil-Wardija [l-Għolja Sciberras] kull xiber raba' jiswa uqija\*", and in English, "There will come a time when every piece of land on Sciberras Hill will be worth its weight in gold").[[29]](#cite\_note-30) De Valette died from a stroke on 21 August 1568 at age 74 and never saw the completion of his city. Originally interred in the church of Our Lady of the Victories, his remains now rest in [St. John's Co-Cathedral](/wiki/St.\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral) among the tombs of other Grand Masters of the [Knights of Malta](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller).[[28]](#cite\_note-journal-29) [Francesco Laparelli](/wiki/Francesco\_Laparelli) was the city's principal designer and his plan departed from medieval Maltese architecture, which exhibited irregular winding streets and alleys. He designed the new city on a rectangular [grid plan](/wiki/Grid\_plan), and without any \*collacchio\* (an area restricted for important buildings).

The streets were designed to be wide and straight, beginning centrally from the [City Gate](/wiki/City\_Gate\_(Valletta)) and ending at Fort Saint Elmo (which was rebuilt) overlooking the Mediterranean; certain bastions were built 47 metres (154 ft) high. His assistant was the Maltese architect [Girolamo Cassar](/wiki/Girolamo\_Cassar), who later oversaw the construction of the city himself after Laparelli's death in 1570.[[28]](#cite\_note-journal-29) The \*[Ufficio delle Case](/w/index.php?title=Ufficio\_delle\_Case&action=edit&redlink=1)\* regulated the building of the city as a [planning authority](/wiki/Planning\_authority).[[30]](#cite\_note-31) The city of Valletta was mostly completed by the early 1570s, and it became the capital on 18 March 1571 when Grand Master [Pierre de Monte](/wiki/Pierre\_de\_Monte) moved from his seat at [Fort St Angelo](/wiki/Fort\_St\_Angelo) in [Birgu](/wiki/Birgu) to the [Grandmaster's Palace in Valletta](/wiki/Grandmaster%27s\_Palace\_in\_Valletta).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Joseph\_Mallord\_William\_Turner\_-\_Malta.jpg/250px-Joseph\_Mallord\_William\_Turner\_-\_Malta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joseph\_Mallord\_William\_Turner\_-\_Malta.jpg) [Turner](/wiki/J.\_M.\_W.\_Turner)'s depiction of the [Grand Harbour](/wiki/Grand\_Harbour), [National Museum of Fine Arts](/wiki/National\_Museum\_of\_Fine\_Arts\_(Malta)) Seven Auberges were built for the [Order's Langues](/wiki/Langue\_(Knights\_Hospitaller)), and these were complete by the 1580s.[[31]](#cite\_note-32)[[32]](#cite\_note-33) An eighth Auberge, [Auberge de Bavière](/wiki/Auberge\_de\_Bavi%C3%A8re), was later added in the 18th century.[[33]](#cite\_note-melitensiawth-phw-34) In [Antoine de Paule](/wiki/Antoine\_de\_Paule)'s reign, it was decided to build more fortifications to protect Valletta, and these were named the [Floriana Lines](/wiki/Floriana\_Lines) after the architect who designed them, [Pietro Paolo Floriani](/wiki/Pietro\_Paolo\_Floriani) of [Macerata](/wiki/Macerata).[[34]](#cite\_note-35) During [António Manoel de Vilhena](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio\_Manoel\_de\_Vilhena)'s reign, a town began to form between the walls of Valletta and the Floriana Lines, and this evolved from a suburb of Valletta to [Floriana](/wiki/Floriana), a town in its own right.[[35]](#cite\_note-armstrong-36) In 1634, a [gunpowder factory explosion](/wiki/1634\_Valletta\_explosion) killed 22 people in Valletta.[[36]](#cite\_note-37) In 1749, Muslim slaves [plotted](/wiki/Conspiracy\_of\_the\_Slaves) to kill Grandmaster [Pinto](/wiki/Manuel\_Pinto\_da\_Fonseca) and take over Valletta, but the revolt was suppressed before it even started because their plans leaked out to the Order.[[37]](#cite\_note-38) Later in his reign, Pinto embellished the city with [Baroque architecture](/wiki/Baroque\_architecture), and many important buildings such as [Auberge de Castille](/wiki/Auberge\_de\_Castille) were remodeled or completely rebuilt in the new architectural style.[[38]](#cite\_note-39) In 1775, during the reign of [Ximenes](/wiki/Francisco\_Ximenes\_de\_Texada), an unsuccessful revolt known as the [Rising of the Priests](/wiki/Rising\_of\_the\_Priests) occurred in which Fort Saint Elmo and [Saint James Cavalier](/wiki/Saint\_James\_Cavalier) were captured by rebels, but the revolt was eventually suppressed.[[39]](#cite\_note-40) ### French occupation and British rule [(/w/index.php?title=Valletta&action=edit&section=3)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Malta\_GC.\_Valletta-1967\_%288240967236%29.jpg/250px-Malta\_GC.\_Valletta-1967\_%288240967236%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_GC.\_Valletta-1967\_(8240967236).jpg) Early morning in 1967 on the notorious Strait Street known to generations of British Servicemen (especially to sailors on shore leave) as "The Gut".

Bars and bordellos abounded, and brawls were common, but its popularity never waned. In 1798, the [French invaded the island](/wiki/French\_occupation\_of\_Malta) and expelled the Order.[[40]](#cite\_note-41) After the Maltese rebelled, French troops continued to occupy Valletta and the surrounding harbour area, until they capitulated to the British in September 1800. In the early 19th century, the British Civil Commissioner, [Henry Pigot](/wiki/Henry\_Pigot), agreed to demolish the majority of the city's fortifications.[[41]](#cite\_note-42) The demolition was again proposed in the 1870s and 1880s, but it was never carried out and the fortifications have survived largely intact.[[23]](#cite\_note-about-24) Eventually, building projects in Valletta resumed under British rule. These projects included widening gates, demolishing and rebuilding structures, widening newer houses over the years, and installing civic projects. The [Malta Railway](/wiki/Malta\_Railway), which linked Valletta to [Mdina](/wiki/Mdina), was officially opened in 1883.[[42]](#cite\_note-43) It was closed down in 1931 after [buses](/wiki/Malta\_bus) became a popular means of transport. In 1939, Valletta was abandoned as the headquarters of the [Royal Navy Mediterranean Fleet](/wiki/Mediterranean\_Fleet) due to its proximity to Italy, and the city became a flash point during the subsequent two-year long [Siege of Malta](/wiki/Siege\_of\_Malta\_(World\_War\_II)).[[43]](#cite\_note-Jacobs2016-44)[German](/wiki/Nazi\_Germany) and [Italian](/wiki/Kingdom\_of\_Italy) air raids throughout the [Second World War](/wiki/Second\_World\_War) caused much destruction in Valletta and the rest of the harbor area.

The [Royal Opera House](/wiki/Royal\_Opera\_House,\_Valletta), constructed at the city entrance in the 19th century, was one of the buildings lost to the raids.[[26]](#cite\_note-cityhistory-27) \* Piano](/wiki/Renzo\_Piano)'s [Parliament House](/wiki/Parliament\_House\_(Malta)) ### Local government [(/w/index.php?title=Valletta&action=edit&section=6)] The City of Valletta, Malta's Capital, was founded in 1566 by Grand Master of the Knights of St.

John. During the period of the Knights of Malta, Valletta had its own Municipal Administrative Council, the Università, administered out of what was known the Municipio or the Banca Giuratale. The Administration attracted notable Maltese personalities. The Universitas of Valletta ran from 1577 to 1818 when the British Colonial Governor, Thomas Maithland suppressed it, taking full control of all administrative affairs of Malta. 175 year later, local administration was re-established in Malta.[[49]](#cite\_note-50)[\*[circular reference](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Wikipedia\_and\_sources\_that\_mirror\_or\_use\_it)\*] The Valletta Local Council was established by the Local Councils Act of 1993, along with the other [local councils of Malta](/wiki/Local\_councils\_of\_Malta).[[50]](#cite\_note-51) The first election was held on 20 November 1993. Other elections were held in 1996, 1999, 2002, 2005, 2008, 2013,[[51]](#cite\_note-52) 2017.[[52]](#cite\_note-53) The present local council was elected in 2019.[[53]](#cite\_note-54) The local council is housed in a building in South Street.

The following people have served as Mayors of Valletta:[[54]](#cite\_note-55) \* Hector Bruno (1993–1999) ([PN](/wiki/Nationalist\_Party\_(Malta))) \* [Paul Borg Olivier](/wiki/Paul\_Borg\_Olivier) (1999–2008) ([PN](/wiki/Nationalist\_Party\_(Malta))) \* [Alexiei Dingli](/wiki/Alexiei\_Dingli) (2008–2019) ([PN](/wiki/Nationalist\_Party\_(Malta)))[[55]](#cite\_note-56) \* Christian Micallef (2019) ([PN](/wiki/Nationalist\_Party\_(Malta)))[[56]](#cite\_note-57) \* Alfred Zammit (2019–) ([PL](/wiki/Labour\_Party\_(Malta)))[[57]](#cite\_note-58) ### National government [(/w/index.php?title=Valletta&action=edit&section=7)] Valletta is the capital city of Malta,[[58]](#cite\_note-59) and is the country's administrative and commercial hub.[[59]](#cite\_note-60) The [Parliament of Malta](/wiki/Parliament\_of\_Malta) has been housed at the [Parliament House](/wiki/Parliament\_House\_(Malta)) near the city's entrance since 2015: it was previously housed at the [Grandmaster's Palace](/wiki/Grandmaster%27s\_Palace\_in\_Valletta) in the city centre.[[60]](#cite\_note-61) The latter palace still houses the Office of the [President of Malta](/wiki/President\_of\_Malta),[[61]](#cite\_note-president-62) while the [Auberge de Castille](/wiki/Auberge\_de\_Castille) houses the Office of the [Prime Minister of Malta](/wiki/Prime\_Minister\_of\_Malta). The [courthouse](/wiki/Courts\_of\_Justice\_building\_(Valletta)) and many government departments are also located in Valletta.[[62]](#cite\_note-nicpmi-63) ## Geography [(/w/index.php?title=Valletta&action=edit&section=8)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Prelucrare\_3D\_pentru\_La\_Valletta\_Harbour.jpg/250px-Prelucrare\_3D\_pentru\_La\_Valletta\_Harbour.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prelucrare\_3D\_pentru\_La\_Valletta\_Harbour.jpg) Valletta between its two harbours The Valletta [peninsula](/wiki/Peninsula) has two natural harbours, [Marsamxett](/wiki/Marsamxett\_Harbour) and the [Grand Harbour](/wiki/Grand\_Harbour).[[45]](#cite\_note-olivergatt-46) The Grand Harbour is Malta's major port, with unloading quays at nearby [Marsa](/wiki/Marsa,\_Malta).

A cruise-liner terminal is located along the old seawall of the [Valletta Waterfront](/wiki/Valletta\_Waterfront) that [Portuguese](/wiki/Portugal) Grandmaster [Manuel Pinto da Fonseca](/wiki/Manuel\_Pinto\_da\_Fonseca) built.[[63]](#cite\_note-64) ### Climate [(/w/index.php?title=Valletta&action=edit&section=9)] Main article: [Climate of Malta](/wiki/Climate\_of\_Malta) Valletta features a hot-summer variant [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean\_climate) ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen\_climate\_classification)\*Csa\*) with very mild, wet winters and long, hot, dry summers, with an average annual temperature above 23 °C (73 °F) during the day and 16 °C (61 °F) at night. Valletta experiences a lack of precipitation during the summer months with most of the annual precipitation occurring during the winter months, and despite the summer dryness, [humidity](/wiki/Humidity) and [dew points](/wiki/Dew\_point) remain high because of the city's coastal location. Winter temperatures are moderated by the surrounding sea and as a result, the city has very mild winters and a long seasonal lag. Air frosts and snowfall are essentially non-existent, with the last measurable snow falling during the [winter of 1963](/wiki/Winter\_of\_1962%E2%80%931963\_in\_the\_United\_Kingdom). The official climate recording station in Malta is at [Luqa Airport](/wiki/Malta\_International\_Airport), which is a few miles inland from Valletta. Average high temperatures range from around 16 °C (61 °F) in January to about 32 °C (90 °F) in August, while average low temperatures range from around 10 °C (50 °F) in January to 23 °C (73 °F) in August.

Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information.\*(December 2014)\*   ---   Rank  2014  2016  2018  ---  ---  ---  ---  1  487,714  559,987  640,570  2  262,631  315,223  390,607  3  143,053  156,786  226,966  4  125,511  144,804  213,299  5  42,285  55,023  99,046  6  N/A  70,563  96,362  7  31,399  41,759  70,191  8  44,697  52,642  57,355  9  22,402  26,454  47,170  10  31,797  40,504  45,572  Total foreign tourists  1,783,366  2,273,837  ### Historical trends [(/w/index.php?title=Tourism\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=8)] Tourism in Malta began to grow beginning in the mid-1960s. During the 1970s, Maltese tourism grew significantly, with numbers growing from 170,800 in 1970 to 705,500 in 1981. From 1981, the figures dropped to approximately 500,000 visitors per year until the late 1980s, when an upward trend began again. In the mid-1990s, figures were as high as 1.2 million tourists per year.[[23]](#cite\_note-sportstourism-23) The volatility of the Maltese tourism market in the past has been largely due to trends in the preferences of tourists from the United Kingdom, who comprise Malta's largest tourism market.

Although Malta's uniform use of English, its traditional ties to the United Kingdom and low-cost travel options have made it an attractive option to British tourists, changing preferences of these tourists can impact Malta's tourism income quite significantly. For example, the increasing preference of British tourists for Spanish destinations during the 1980s was reflected in a drop in Malta's tourism numbers during that period.[[23]](#cite\_note-sportstourism-23) Tourist Arrivals to Malta (2001–2024)[[24]](#cite\_note-24)  Tourist Arrivals  ---   1,146,262  2002   1,089,548  2004   1,170,816  2006   1,243,506  2008   1,182,490  2010   1,415,018  2012   1,582,153  2014   1,783,366  2016   2,273,837  2018   2,753,239  2020   968,136  2022   2,981,476  2024  ## Market issues [(/w/index.php?title=Tourism\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=9)] Malta's tourism industry faces a number of issues affecting it now and threatening to affect it in the future. One of the clearest issues facing Malta's tourism industry is overcrowding as a result of the island nation's relatively small size, in terms of both area and population. Malta is a nation of just under 450,000 people,[[25]](#cite\_note-25) yet its infrastructure is required to support 2.6 million tourists every year. Malta's water works, roads, waste management systems and beaches are stretched to capacity in the summer months of July and August of every year, when tourism numbers are at their peak.

This is a challenge facing Maltese tourism operators as it means that they cannot simply pursue the kinds of 'mass tourism' marketing measures taken by operators in other Mediterranean destinations with more space and resources to pursue them. In addition, Maltese tourism operators must balance increased tourism numbers with the needs of the 'native population', as when resources are stretched thin by tourists during the summer season, there are negative impacts on Maltese residents also.[[26]](#cite\_note-26)[[27]](#cite\_note-27) ## See also [(/w/index.php?title=Tourism\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=10)] \* [List of museums in Malta](/wiki/List\_of\_museums\_in\_Malta) ## References [(/w/index.php?title=Tourism\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=11)] 1. \*\*[^](#cite\_ref-NSO\_1-0)\*\*["Tourism Satellite Accounts 2010"](https://nso.gov.mt/wp-content/uploads/TSA\_2010.pdf)(PDF). \*nso.gov.mt\*. [Coordinates](/wiki/Geographic\_coordinate\_system): ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/WMA\_button2b.png/20px-WMA\_button2b.png)[36°03′N14°15′E﻿ / ﻿36.050°N 14.250°E﻿ / 36.050; 14.250](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Gozo&params=36\_03\_N\_14\_15\_E\_region:MT\_type:isle) From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Island of the Maltese archipelago in the Mediterranean Sea For the administrative region, see [Gozo Region](/wiki/Gozo\_Region). For the island's short lived independence, see [Gozo (independent state)](/wiki/Gozo\_(independent\_state)). Gozo       South of [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily), [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean\_Sea)  Coordinates   [Maltese islands](/wiki/Geography\_of\_Malta#Islands)  Area   13.34 km (8.289 mi)  Width     [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,\_Gozo)[[a]](#cite\_note-1) (pop. 7,242[[1]](#cite\_note-nso-census-2021-2))  Minister for Gozo    Gozitans  Population   557/km2 (1443/sq mi)  Languages   [Maltese people](/wiki/Maltese\_people)     ---   #### Tunnel [(/w/index.php?title=Gozo&action=edit&section=7)] Main article: [Malta–Gozo Tunnel](/wiki/Malta%E2%80%93Gozo\_Tunnel) Several proposals have been made to construct a road link between Malta and Gozo. In 1972[[28]](#cite\_note-Sansone-31) the newly elected Labour Party administration carried out a feasibility study that concluded building a bridge between the two islands was possible, but would have negative environmental effects. A tunnel was also considered, but found to be too expensive at the time.[[31]](#cite\_note-Said-34) An online poll by \*[The Times of Malta](/wiki/The\_Times\_of\_Malta)\* in 2006 found that 55% of respondents supported a road link.[[32]](#cite\_note-35) In June 2013 a "mega Chinese state-owned company" [China Communications Construction Corporation Limited](/wiki/China\_Communications\_Construction\_Company) will finance a €4 million study to assess the feasibility of a bridge between Malta and Gozo.

"Depending on the feasibility of the tunnel and bridge projects, popular consultation will take place giving particular weight to what Gozitans have to say."[[33]](#cite\_note-36) "Gozo Minister [Anton Refalo](/wiki/Anton\_Refalo) alluded to the possibility of calling a referendum to determine whether Gozitans prefer a tunnel or a bridge to connect Malta and Gozo".[[34]](#cite\_note-timesofmalta.com-37) The study found out that the bridge would take four years to build and construction would cost €1 billion. Apart from this, operation and maintenance costs are estimated to cost up to €4 million every year; China Communications Construction Corporation Limited proposed to build the bridge by 2020.[[35]](#cite\_note-38)[[36]](#cite\_note-39) The idea of building a bridge is opposed by environmentalists and NGOs such as [Din l-Art Ħelwa](/wiki/Din\_l-Art\_%C4%A6elwa) and Flimkien għal Ambjent Aħjar.[[37]](#cite\_note-40)[[38]](#cite\_note-41) An 11 km underground sub-seabed tunnel has been proposed in a report on the viability of a Gozo-Malta tunnel link by the [Gozo Business Chamber](/w/index.php?title=Gozo\_Business\_Chamber&action=edit&redlink=1) (GBC) together with [Transport Malta](/wiki/Transport\_Malta) (TM).

The Gozo Business Chamber is organising a presentation by economist Gordon Cordina, of the detailed report about the feasibility of a 'Gozo-Malta Subsea Tunnel.[[39]](#cite\_note-42) In 2015, members from the GBC together with representatives from TM also visited Norway, where they spoke with experts, and toured several underwater tunnels.[[40]](#cite\_note-43) In December 2015, a group of students joined forces to create 'Front Favur il-Mina' to support a permanently-connected, tunnel project between Malta and its sister island. Several MPs endorsed the group.[[41]](#cite\_note-44) A catamaran service (fast ferry) to Gozo, ferry trips from the Grand Harbour and ultimately a tunnel linking Malta with the sister island are the main proposals pushed forward by the pressure group.[[42]](#cite\_note-45) Addressing a conference in Gozo organised by the pro-tunnel movement, [Joseph Muscat](/wiki/Joseph\_Muscat) said "The government intends to move forward with a project linking the two islands." Furthermore, former opposition leader [Simon Busuttil](/wiki/Simon\_Busuttil) confirmed during the same conference, that the [Nationalist Party](/wiki/Nationalist\_Party\_(Malta)) was in favour of the project and willing to cooperate with the government.[[43]](#cite\_note-46) The [University of Malta](/wiki/University\_of\_Malta) carried out geological and geophysical investigations in connection with a proposed sub-sea tunnel between Malta and Gozo following an agreement with Transport Malta. Scientific investigations included both desktop and field studies, passive seismic measurements, bathymetric mapping, and a seismic study.

The University of Malta conducted a marine baseline study which incorporated a geological model of [topography](/wiki/Topography), [stratigraphy](/wiki/Stratigraphy), structure, geological, geophysical and tectonic properties of the study area.[[44]](#cite\_note-47) During this phase of the study, scientists deployed a 300-metre-long cable with a series of specialised receptors. An 'air gun' released bursts of compressed air every few metres. The compressed air was reflected to the receptors. Different geological layers reflect different frequencies. Scientists could thus determine the geological formations that lie beneath the surface and determine possible cracks.

Following such tests, more tests will be carried out; these will consist in the drilling of a series of boreholes, up to 200 metres below the sea bed, to determine the rock strata below the surface.[[45]](#cite\_note-48) #### Catamaran [(/w/index.php?title=Gozo&action=edit&section=8)] "Expressions of interest have been issued for a fast [catamaran](/wiki/Catamaran) service between Gozo and Valletta as well as between Gozo and [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily)." In June 2013, the services would be aimed for use by both tourists and the Maltese and would involve public service obligations.[[34]](#cite\_note-timesofmalta.com-37) #### Air [(/w/index.php?title=Gozo&action=edit&section=9)] An airstrip for fixed-wing aircraft on Gozo was proposed in the 1990s, but rejected for environmental reasons.[[31]](#cite\_note-Said-34) In June 2013 the government announced a plan "to issue a call for [expressions of interest](/wiki/Expression\_of\_interest) for the operation of a scheduled air service between Malta and Gozo".[[46]](#cite\_note-49) ## Transportation within Gozo [(/w/index.php?title=Gozo&action=edit&section=10)]   ---   This article \*\*is written like a [travel guide](/wiki/Wikipedia:What\_Wikipedia\_is\_not#Wikipedia\_is\_not\_a\_manual,\_guidebook,\_textbook,\_or\_scientific\_journal)\*\*. According to the Arab chronicler and geographer al-Ḥimyarī (d. c.727/1326–7), Malta remained practically uninhabited after the Muslim conquest, until it was colonised by Muslims from Sicily in 440–1/1048–9. Joseph Brincat interprets al-Ḥimyarī's account of the 441/1049 Arab colonisation to suggest, among other things, that the island remained essentially uninhabited for 180 years (Brincat, 9–14), somewhat confirming Ibn Ḥawqal's assertion that Malta was populated only by wild donkeys and numerous sheep, and that it "produces honey" (Ibn Hawqal, 1:198)." 22. \*\*[^](#cite\_ref-22)\*\*[Blouet 2007](#CITEREFBlouet2007), p. 41 23. \*\*[^](#cite\_ref-23)\*\*[Brincat 1995](#CITEREFBrincat1995), p. 12 24. \*\*[^](#cite\_ref-24)\*\*Dalli, Charles (2005).

14.33083﻿ (Ġebel tal-Ħalfa)](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Geography\_of\_Malta&params=36.02917\_N\_14.33083\_E\_&title=%C4%A0ebel+tal-%C4%A6alfa)[[4]](#cite\_note-Geo-4) Situated near Gozo. In the island there is a 4-to-5-metre (13 to 16 ft) deep water rock pool just 2 metres (6 ft 7 in) above sea level, possibly man made due to its perfectly round shape. There is also an underground fresh water system which emerges from a small cave. Most of the plants occurs on the western part of the island.

[Crucianella rupestris](/wiki/Crucianella\_rupestris), [Arthrocnemum macrostachyum](/wiki/Arthrocnemum\_macrostachyum), [Lygeum spartum](/wiki/Lygeum\_spartum), [Convolvulus oleifolius](/wiki/Convolvulus\_oleifolius), [Thymbra capitata](/wiki/Thymbra\_capitata), [Teucrium fruticans](/wiki/Teucrium\_fruticans), [Allium melitense](/wiki/Allium\_melitense), [Anacamptis urvilleana](/w/index.php?title=Anacamptis\_urvilleana&action=edit&redlink=1), [Bromus madritensis](/wiki/Bromus\_madritensis), [Capparis spinosa](/wiki/Capparis\_spinosa), [Echium parviflorum](/wiki/Echium\_parviflorum), [Pallenis spinosa](/wiki/Pallenis\_spinosa), [Plantago lagopus](/wiki/Plantago\_lagopus), [Trachynia distachya](/w/index.php?title=Trachynia\_distachya&action=edit&redlink=1), [Urginea pancration](/w/index.php?title=Urginea\_pancration&action=edit&redlink=1).[[5]](#cite\_note-S&S-5)   Situated on the southeast of Comino. Flora consisted of 22 individuals of [Inula crithmoides](/wiki/Inula\_crithmoides) (2010).[[5]](#cite\_note-S&S-5)  Lantern Point Rock\*(Ġebla Tal-Ponta Rqiqa)\*   Large [Blue Lagoon Rock](/w/index.php?title=Blue\_Lagoon\_Rock&action=edit&redlink=1)   Small [Blue Lagoon Rocks](/w/index.php?title=Blue\_Lagoon\_Rock&action=edit&redlink=1)   Devil's End Rock\*(Il-Ġebla tax-Xifer l-Infern)\*[[5]](#cite\_note-S&S-5)   Għallis Rocks[[5]](#cite\_note-S&S-5)   Taċ-Ċawl Rock\*(Il-Ġebla taċ-Ċawl)\*   Situated near main island. It is 9 to 12 metres (30 to 39 ft) high at its highest point. [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/94/Symbol\_support\_vote.svg/20px-Symbol\_support\_vote.svg.png)](/wiki/Wikipedia:Good\_articles\*) From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Uni-20malta.jpg/250px-Uni-20malta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uni-20malta.jpg) Campus of the [University of Malta](/wiki/University\_of\_Malta) \*\*Education in Malta\*\* is [compulsory](/wiki/Compulsory\_education) through age sixteen and is offered through three different providers: the [state](/wiki/State\_school), the [church](/wiki/Church\_school), and the [private sector](/wiki/Private\_school). The state is responsible for promoting education and instruction and ensuring universal access to education for all Maltese citizens the existence of a system of schools and institutions accessible to all Maltese citizens. The objectives of education in Malta include intellectual and moral development and the preparation of every citizen to contribute productively to the national economy. The arrival of a number of religious orders in the Middle Ages brought religious-based education to the island for wealthy families. The arrival of the [Knights Hospitaller](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller) saw the establishment of the [University of Malta](/wiki/University\_of\_Malta), around which a number of primary, secondary and post-secondary institutions were established. Education in Malta has been universally available at the primary level since the ejection of the Knights Hospitaller by the French in 1798, when state-funded elementary schooling was established. In 1878, English replaced Italian as the primary language of instruction, and education was made compulsory in 1946 in response to a number of children not attending school due to poverty between World Wars One and Two. The age at which education became compulsory was lowered to five years in 1988.

Malta's educational system is divided into four stages: pre-primary (ages 3–5), primary (ages 5–11), secondary (ages 11–16) and tertiary. Pre-primary education is optional but fully funded by the state. In their last ten years of primary education, students are placed on tracks based on educational attainment, and at the age of eleven, students sit an [eleven plus examination](/wiki/Eleven\_plus\_exam) to eliminate a student's secondary schooling direction. Success in the eleven plus exam places a student in a junior lyceum - a prestigious secondary school - while mediocre performance or not sitting the examination places a student in a less competitive secondary school. Secondary Education Certificate (SEC) examinations are taken at age 16, and matriculation examinations are taken at age 18 to determine university entrance eligibility. In 2008, 26,711 primary students, 25,793 secondary students, 5,719 post-secondary students, 9,472 tertiary students and 6,268 vocational students were enrolled in educational courses in Malta. Approximately 30 per cent of Malta's primary and secondary school students are enrolled in private schools, most of which are operated by the Catholic Church. Malta's highest post-secondary institution is the [University of Malta](/wiki/University\_of\_Malta), which has operated since 1552. ## History [(/w/index.php?title=Education\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=1)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Sacra\_Infermeria\_hospital\_medical\_school\_Malta.jpg/250px-Sacra\_Infermeria\_hospital\_medical\_school\_Malta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sacra\_Infermeria\_hospital\_medical\_school\_Malta.jpg) The historic Sacra Infermeria hospital, original site of the medieval University of Malta's medical school, now operates as the Mediterranean Conference Center.

The period of Arab rule between 870 and 1090 brought what would become the [Maltese language](/wiki/Maltese\_language). The arrival of the [Franciscans](/wiki/Franciscan) in 1350, the [Carmelites](/wiki/Carmelite) in 1418, the [Dominican Order](/wiki/Dominican\_Order) in 1450, the [Augustinians](/wiki/Augustinians) in 1460 and the [Friars Minor](/wiki/Friars\_Minor) in 1492 brought religious-based education to the island. Members of these groups were asked to serve as private tutors for the children of wealthy parents, and later moved to set up classes for instruction in [Italian](/wiki/Italian\_language), [Latin](/wiki/Latin) and numeracy. In 1592, the Collegium Melitense (what was to become the [University of Malta](/wiki/University\_of\_Malta)) was established by the [Society of Jesus](/wiki/Society\_of\_Jesus) as a result of a direct order from [Pope Clement VIII](/wiki/Pope\_Clement\_VIII), and around this institution a number of others flourished, including a grammar school, a preparatory school and institutions for the study of [cartography](/wiki/Cartography), [naval architecture](/wiki/Naval\_architecture) and navigation. In addition to public options, it was possible to hire private tutors in a number of different areas, including accounting, philosophy, navigation and languages.

During this period, however, education for those without wealth was non-existent.[[1]](#cite\_note-evolution-1) During the 16th century, philosophy, theology, grammar and the humanities were taught at the Collegium, and following Europe's temporary recovery from the [Black Death](/wiki/Black\_Death) in 1675, the [Grand Master](/wiki/Grand\_Master\_(order)) of the [Knights Hospitaller](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller) appointed a new lecturer in anatomy and surgery at Sacra Infermeria, essentially establishing the University of Malta's medical school.[[2]](#cite\_note-unihist-2) The Roman Catholic Church, however, was against educating the lower classes and focused on those who afforded to pay. This resulted in mass illiteracy by the end of the Order of St John and took until the second half of the 19th century, under British rule, to have a sprint towards further inclusion.[[3]](#cite\_note-3) The ejection of the [Knights Hospitaller](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller) by the [French Republic](/wiki/French\_Republic) in 1798 brought a reformation of the educational system with it due to the disparity between the traditional ideals of the Knights and the revolutionary ideals of the French. Private schooling was banned, [elementary schooling](/wiki/Elementary\_school) was introduced and funded by the state, and adult education was introduced.

The gradual introduction of [universal education](/wiki/Universal\_education) for primary school students was an ideal born before and during the [French Revolution](/wiki/French\_Revolution), while private schools were abolished due to their traditional association with the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic\_Church), an institution rejected by those involved in the Revolution.[[4]](#cite\_note-4) In addition, [Napoleon I](/wiki/Napoleon\_I) abolished the University of Malta just five days after his ejection of the Knights, although this decision was reversed after the British ejected the French the following year.[[2]](#cite\_note-unihist-2) In 1799, Malta fell under the jurisdiction of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United\_Kingdom), and attempts were made to adjust Malta's institutions towards a British style.[[5]](#cite\_note-5) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/MLAupperBarakkaGarden.JPG/250px-MLAupperBarakkaGarden.JPG)](/wiki/File:MLAupperBarakkaGarden.JPG) This 1903 sculpture in [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta)'s Upper Barrakka Gardens, "Les Gavroches" by Maltese artist Antonio Sciortino, depicts the poverty children experienced in Malta in the early 20th century. Under Government Ponsonby, in 1833, the Catholic Church in Malta was staunchly against public education; the only effort done by the [priesthood](/wiki/Priesthood) is to demand people not to accept education or be condemned by [excommunication](/wiki/Excommunication), together with other religious consequences.[[6]](#cite\_note-6)[[7]](#cite\_note-7) Focus on religious teaching, at the exclusion of important matters, left over 97% of the country illiterate. The role of [Canon](/wiki/Canon\_(priest)) Fortunato Panzavecchia as Director of Schools proved him to be unworthy and stubborn of progress. He even fabricated irrelevant religious-related documents about supposed improvements.

The British observed that the Director was more interested in the failure of educating the common people and was unfit for purpose.[[8]](#cite\_note-8) In 1847, elementary schooling was changed to the British model, schools were opened in every village and education was made free of charge. In 1878, the mode of language education delivery changed following the publication of two reports on the matter. Italian language education was replaced by instruction in the [English language](/wiki/English\_language), and the use of [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_language) was encouraged in the primary years. However, English was the language in which education was delivered at all stages of education following primary school. Debate continued until the 1930s over which language should be used in Maltese education - Italian or English.[[1]](#cite\_note-evolution-1) During [World War I](/wiki/World\_War\_I), the [interwar years](/wiki/Interwar\_years) and [World War II](/wiki/World\_War\_II), widespread poverty in Malta prevented the children of the lower classes from pursuing basic education in favor of staying home to assist parents with agricultural work and other income earning activities.

To counteract this phenomenon, on 1 February 1946 education was declared [compulsory](/wiki/Compulsory\_education) till the age of 14.[[9]](#cite\_note-9) In 1964, Malta became an independent nation, and in 1988 a new Education Act was passed, lowering the compulsory education age to five years, decentralising educational decision making and establishing a right for any person to apply for a licence to open a school.

The Faculty of Arts, Sciences and Theology at the University of Malta was also reinstated.[[1]](#cite\_note-evolution-1) ## Statistics [(/w/index.php?title=Education\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=2)] In 2008, 26,711 primary students, 25,793 secondary students, 5,719 post-secondary students, 9,472 tertiary students and 6,268 vocational students were enrolled in educational courses in Malta.[[10]](#cite\_note-maltainfigures10-10) Approximately 30 per cent of students of school age attend private schools, most of which are operated by the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic\_Church).[[11]](#cite\_note-councilofeurope-11) In 2009, 34.9 per cent of the population between 18 and 24 years of age had not completed secondary school,[[12]](#cite\_note-maltainfigures11-12) while in 2008 92 per cent of the population was considered [literate](/wiki/Literacy).[[13]](#cite\_note-unicefstats-13) Malta ranks equal eleventh in the world on the [national IQ scale](/wiki/IQ\_and\_Global\_Inequality).[[14]](#cite\_note-nationaliq-14) The [compulsory education](/wiki/Compulsory\_education) period in Malta has been between five and 16 years of age since the introduction of the \*Education Act 1988\*.[[1]](#cite\_note-evolution-1) This is an increase from up to 12 years of age in 1924 (in this year, school \*attendance\* became compulsory for those already enrolled - the act of enrollment itself was not compulsory) and 14 years of age in 1946, when compulsory school attendance was introduced for all students, enrolled or otherwise.

Compulsory schooling has always applied to both males and females during its existence.[[15]](#cite\_note-admin-15) ## Structure [(/w/index.php?title=Education\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=3)] ### School education [(/w/index.php?title=Education\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=4)] Schooling is compulsory from the age of five,[[1]](#cite\_note-evolution-1) although free-of-charge [kindergarten](/wiki/Kindergarten) is available to all students beginning at age three.[[16]](#cite\_note-16) Approximately 94 per cent of three- and four-year-olds attend kindergarten. A number of private operators exist, but free state-run institutions are also popular. State-run Maltese kindergartens are required to have one staff member present for every ten children present, while this ratio decreases to 1:20 for independently run institutions. During the kindergarten years, the focus is on play, not formal education. From kindergarten, students transition to primary school, which is compulsory from the age of five. Since 1980, all state-run primary schools have been [co-educational](/wiki/Co-educational), with many independent schools following suit. However, church-run primary schools are usually single-sex. Classes usually have a limit of thirty students, and a minimum teacher to student ratio has been set at 1:19 in all schools, whether they be state-run, church-run or independent.[[17]](#cite\_note-systems-17) Formal end-of-year examinations commence starting in Year 4 to dictate class streaming arrangements for the following year. Students are examined in English, Maltese, mathematics, religion and social studies.

Despite the fact that [eleven-plus examinations](/wiki/Eleven\_plus\_exam) highlight inequality and failures, and are recognized as ineffective educational tools by experts,[[18]](#cite\_note-18)[[19]](#cite\_note-19)[[20]](#cite\_note-20) Malta still implements such exams.[[21]](#cite\_note-differentiated-21) In Year 6, when most pupils have turned eleven years of age, an eleven-plus exam is given to students to determine which students attend which secondary schools. Students are tested in English language, Maltese language, mathematics, social studies and religion; the latter is optional for those not adhering to the faith taught in schools. While 73 per cent of eleven-year-old students sat the eleven-plus exam in 2001, only about 54 percent pass it each year. Students achieving success in the eleven-plus exam go on to attend prestigious 'junior lyceums' during their secondary years, whilst those who do not attend 'area secondary schools'. This is similar to the operation of the [Tripartite System](/wiki/Tripartite\_System) in the United Kingdom following [World War II](/wiki/World\_War\_II).[[17]](#cite\_note-systems-17) State-run junior lyceums and area secondary schools are single-sex. Junior lyceums are divided into two grade stages. The first stage is an introductory stage, and runs for the first two years of secondary schooling. Students study essentially the same subjects with very limited curriculum options in order to provide a good grounding for future studies.

Following the two-year introductory cycle, a three-year specialist stage occurs during which students study a common core curriculum as well as a number of elective subjects. This orientation is similar at area secondary schools, although the introductory period is longer, at three years in duration, and the final specialist period is shorter, at two years. The staff-student ratio is set at 1:11, and the school leaving age is 16.[[17]](#cite\_note-systems-17) Examinations in Maltese secondary schools are taken at both age 16 and 18, with the latter being a final optional set of matriculation examinations. The examinations taken at age 16 are the Secondary Education Certificate (SEC) exams, which have operated since 1994, before which the [GCE Ordinary Level](/wiki/GCE\_Ordinary\_Level) was used as a secondary certificate examination. The examinations taken at 18, those enabling entry into university, are the Matriculation Certificate examinations, based on the [International Baccalaureate](/wiki/International\_Baccalaureate).

These examinations replaced the [GCE Advanced Levels](/wiki/GCE\_Advanced\_Level).[[21]](#cite\_note-differentiated-21) ### Day-to-day operation [(/w/index.php?title=Education\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=5)] A number of students travel to school using the nation's [bus network](/wiki/Transport\_in\_Malta), which is free for state school students to use.[[22]](#cite\_note-22) Schools in Malta generally begin their school day at 8:30am and finish at 2:40pm.[[23]](#cite\_note-secularhs-23) Students receive three days of holidays in November 15 days of holidays over Christmas, two days of holidays in March and eight days of holidays in April, in addition to [public holidays](/wiki/Public\_holiday).[[24]](#cite\_note-24) Maltese school students receive the highest number of days off of all school students in Europe, with 90 days during the 2010–11 academic year being non-school days. This is 14 days more than the European average of 76.[[25]](#cite\_note-25) Virtually all Maltese state schools run [tuck shops](/wiki/Tuck\_shop), while some secondary schools are home to [cafeterias](/wiki/Cafeteria).[[26]](#cite\_note-malta?!-26) State schools provide textbooks free of charge to their students, while private schools generally require their students to pay for their textbooks, enabling the latter to change their textbooks and textbook editions more frequently. All primary schools have at least four computers, one printer, a large monitor and a teacher's laptop computer in their classrooms, while many secondary schools have specialised rooms necessary to teach subjects such as Home Economics and Food Technology.

An average of 22.5 students are enrolled per primary school class, with the average rising to 24.3 in secondary school classes. Primary school teachers are expected to dedicate five hours per week to each of [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_language), [English](/wiki/English\_language) and mathematics, one hour per week to science, two hours per weeks to arts and crafts, two hours per week to social studies, one hour and 15 minutes per week to social studies, and two and a half hours per week to physical education and religion. State school syllabi at the secondary level are set by the local examination bodies.[[26]](#cite\_note-malta?!-26) ### Tertiary education [(/w/index.php?title=Education\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=6)] Under the \*Education Act of 1988\*, the [Government of Malta](/wiki/Government\_of\_Malta) is obligated to provide free university education to eligible residents with sufficient qualifications for entry. However, due to funding constraints, the [University of Malta](/wiki/University\_of\_Malta) offers some courses only every other year in order to maximise course offerings with optimum funding usage.[[17]](#cite\_note-systems-17) 10,000 students currently study at the university. Most [Bachelor degree](/wiki/Bachelor\_degree) courses run for three years.[[27]](#cite\_note-universityofmalta-27) The [College of Remote and Offshore Medicine](/w/index.php?title=College\_of\_Remote\_and\_Offshore\_Medicine&action=edit&redlink=1) offers undergraduate and postgraduate degrees in the field of remote and [Wilderness medicine](/wiki/Wilderness\_medicine) in Malta.

## Educational management [(/w/index.php?title=Education\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=7)] ### Administration [(/w/index.php?title=Education\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=8)] State-run education in Malta is administered at the national level of government, with the Minister for Education empowered to make orders, or 'Legal Notices', relating to the administration of education throughout the country. The Minister for Education is appointed by the [President](/wiki/President\_of\_Malta) on advice of the [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime\_Minister\_of\_Malta) and is responsible for a number of education-related government functions (such as the operation of schools and libraries) as well as tertiary education in the country. The Ministry of Education, the national government department responsible for education in Malta, is headed by a Permanent Secretary. The Education Division of the Ministry is responsible for setting a number of annual nationwide examinations, managing human resources in Malta's educational system, selecting school textbook lists, allocating students to schools based on area, managing the Ministry's educational finances and promoting a number of cultural, sporting and social activities. Malta's Ministry of Education is currently being decentralised with the aim of having schools managed at the local level.[[15]](#cite\_note-admin-15) With the exception of the currently occurring [educational management](/wiki/Educational\_management) decentralisation process, regional administration of education in Malta is limited. However, the island of [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo) does maintain a form of regional educational administration through the Ministry for Gozo.

Although the Ministry for Education manages education throughout Malta, the Ministry for Gozo is responsible for the remuneration of the island's teaching staff. Local authorities such as Malta's numerous Local Councils have no official ties to Malta's educational system but often donate to local schools and maintain preschool building structures.[[15]](#cite\_note-admin-15) ### Teacher development [(/w/index.php?title=Education\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=9)] Primary and secondary school teachers begin their teacher education at the [University of Malta](/wiki/University\_of\_Malta) by gaining a Bachelor of Education (Honours), which typically takes four years of study to achieve. To gain entry into the university to study primary or secondary education, students must not only meet the university's general entry requirements but also a number of other special course requirements. University students seeking to enter the teaching profession may also become qualified by gaining a [postgraduate certificate in education](/wiki/Postgraduate\_certificate\_in\_education). Two years of professional teaching practice is also generally required.

The Minister for Education may also grant a temporary warrant to teach, valid for one year, to any person the Minister believes has the ability to teach in the country.[[15]](#cite\_note-admin-15) In 2019, teachers entering the workforce are paid a minimum of €23,936 per year, with the most experienced teachers being paid €27,340 per year after 18 years of service.[[28]](#cite\_note-28) Heads of School received a minimum of €27,707.00 per year.[[29]](#cite\_note-29) In 2005–06, there were 8,217 teaching staff in Malta, of which 7,988 worked at state schools (Including the University of Malta, [MCAST](/wiki/Malta\_College\_of\_Arts,\_Science\_and\_Technology) and [Ġ. F. Abela Junior College](/wiki/%C4%A0.\_F.\_Abela\_Junior\_College)).[[15]](#cite\_note-admin-15) ## Further reading [(/w/index.php?title=Education\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=10)] \* Cedefop (2017).  Dec  5  6,151  ## In popular culture [(/w/index.php?title=Siege\_of\_Malta\_(World\_War\_II)&action=edit&section=30)] ### In film [(/w/index.php?title=Siege\_of\_Malta\_(World\_War\_II)&action=edit&section=31)] \* The [war propaganda](/wiki/Propaganda) short film \*[Malta G.C.](/w/index.php?title=Malta\_G.C.&action=edit&redlink=1)\* was commissioned in 1942 by the British government to popularise the endurance of the Maltese people and the [awarding of the George Cross](/wiki/Award\_of\_the\_George\_Cross\_to\_Malta).

It features real footage of the bombings and their effects.[[190]](#cite\_note-190) \* The 1953 British film \*[Malta Story](/wiki/Malta\_Story)\* is the fictional story of a photo reconnaissance RAF pilot in Malta during the siege. It features real footage and re-enactments with authentic aircraft. ### In literature [(/w/index.php?title=Siege\_of\_Malta\_(World\_War\_II)&action=edit&section=32)] In his novel \*The Kappillan of Malta\* (1973) [Nicholas Monsarrat](/wiki/Nicholas\_Monsarrat) gives an account of the siege of Malta from June 1940 to August 1942 as experienced by the fictional Catholic priest Father Salvatore.[[191]](#cite\_note-191) The novel is interspersed with brief episodes from other periods of Maltese history. [Thomas Pynchon](/wiki/Thomas\_Pynchon)'s 1963 debut novel \*[V.](/wiki/V.)\* features a chapter devoted to life during the siege of Malta. In his 2016 novel \*Everyone Brave is Forgiven,\*[Chris Cleave](/wiki/Chris\_Cleave) presents the misery and horror of the siege through the eyes of British officers whose experiences are loosely based on those of his grandfather David Hill, who served in the Royal Artillery. ## See also [(/w/index.php?title=Siege\_of\_Malta\_(World\_War\_II)&action=edit&section=33)] \* [Malta Command](/wiki/Malta\_Command) \* [Military history of Gibraltar during World War II](/wiki/Military\_history\_of\_Gibraltar\_during\_World\_War\_II) \* [Military history of the British Commonwealth in the Second World War](/wiki/Military\_history\_of\_the\_British\_Commonwealth\_in\_the\_Second\_World\_War) \* [Adrian Warburton](/wiki/Adrian\_Warburton) \* [George Beurling](/wiki/George\_Beurling) \* [List of British military equipment of World War II](/wiki/List\_of\_British\_military\_equipment\_of\_World\_War\_II) \* [List of Italian Army equipment in World War II](/wiki/List\_of\_Italian\_Army\_equipment\_in\_World\_War\_II) \* [List of German military equipment of World War II](/wiki/List\_of\_German\_military\_equipment\_of\_World\_War\_II) ## Notes [(/w/index.php?title=Siege\_of\_Malta\_(World\_War\_II)&action=edit&section=34)] 1.
