Island country in Southern Europe This article is about the country. For other uses, see [Malta (disambiguation)](/wiki/Malta\_(disambiguation)). [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta)[35°54′N14°31′E﻿ / ﻿35.900°N 14.517°E﻿ / 35.900; 14.517](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Malta&params=35\_54\_N\_14\_31\_E\_type:city)  Official languages   \* [Maltese Sign Language](/wiki/Maltese\_Sign\_Language)[[2]](#cite\_note-2) \* [Italian](/wiki/Maltese\_Italian)  [Ethnic groups](/wiki/Ethnic\_group) (2021[[3]](#cite\_note-3))   \* + 88.5% [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity) - 82.6% [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic\_Church\_in\_Malta) ([official](/wiki/State\_Religion)) - 5.9% other [Christian](/wiki/List\_of\_Christian\_denominations) \* 5.1% [no religion](/wiki/Irreligion) \* 3.9% [Islam](/wiki/Islam\_in\_Malta) \* 1.4% [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism\_in\_Malta) \* 1.1% other  [Demonym](/wiki/Demonym)   [Unitary parliamentary republic](/wiki/Unitary\_parliamentary\_republic)   • [President](/wiki/President\_of\_Malta)   [Robert Abela](/wiki/Robert\_Abela)  Legislature     21 September 1964  • Republic    316[[6]](#cite\_note-Zammit-6) km2 (122 sq mi) ([187th](/wiki/List\_of\_countries\_and\_dependencies\_by\_area))  • Water (%)    519,562[[7]](#cite\_note-NSO-7)  • Density   2024 estimate  • Total   $67,682[[8]](#cite\_note-IMFWEO.MT-8) ([20th](/wiki/List\_of\_countries\_by\_GDP\_(PPP)\_per\_capita))  [GDP](/wiki/Gross\_domestic\_product)(nominal)   $22.737 billion[[8]](#cite\_note-IMFWEO.MT-8) ([118th](/wiki/List\_of\_countries\_by\_GDP\_(nominal)))  • Per capita   31.4[[9]](#cite\_note-eurogini-9)medium inequality  [HDI](/wiki/Human\_Development\_Index)(2023)   [Euro](/wiki/Euro) ([€](/wiki/Euro\_sign)) ([EUR](/wiki/ISO\_4217))  Time zone   [UTC](/wiki/Coordinated\_Universal\_Time)+2 ([CEST](/wiki/Central\_European\_Summer\_Time))  [Calling code](/wiki/Telephone\_numbers\_in\_Malta)   [MT](/wiki/ISO\_3166-2:MT)  [Internet TLD](/wiki/Country\_code\_top-level\_domain)   \*\*Malta\*\*,[[a]](#cite\_note-13) officially the \*\*Republic of Malta\*\*,[[b]](#cite\_note-14) is an [island country](/wiki/Island\_country) in [Southern Europe](/wiki/Southern\_Europe) located in the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean\_Sea), between [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily) and [North Africa](/wiki/North\_Africa). It consists of an archipelago 80 km (50 mi) south of [Italy](/wiki/Italy), 284 km (176 mi) east of [Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisia),[[13]](#cite\_note-ashby-15) and 333 km (207 mi) north of [Libya](/wiki/Libya).[[14]](#cite\_note-16)[[15]](#cite\_note-17) The two official languages are [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_language) and [English](/wiki/English\_language) but Maltese is recognised as the national language.[[16]](#cite\_note-18) The country's capital is [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta), which is the smallest capital city in the [European Union](/wiki/European\_Union) (EU) by both area and population.

With a population of about 574,250[[17]](#cite\_note-19) spread over an area of 316 km2 (122 sq mi),[[6]](#cite\_note-Zammit-6) Malta is the world's [tenth-smallest country by area](/wiki/List\_of\_countries\_and\_dependencies\_by\_area)[[18]](#cite\_note-20)[[19]](#cite\_note-21) and the [ninth-most densely populated](/wiki/List\_of\_countries\_and\_dependencies\_by\_population\_density). Various sources consider the country to consist of a single urban region,[[20]](#cite\_note-WUP-22)[[21]](#cite\_note-ESPON-EUC-23) for which reason it is often described as a [city-state](/wiki/City-state).[[22]](#cite\_note-GMB\_Publishing-24)[[23]](#cite\_note-creativemalta-25)[[24]](#cite\_note-doi-26) Malta has been inhabited since at least 6500 BC, during the [Mesolithic](/wiki/Mesolithic).[[25]](#cite\_note-:0-27) Its location in the centre of the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean\_Sea) has historically given it great [geostrategic](/wiki/Geostrategy) importance, with a succession of powers having ruled the islands and shaped its culture and society.[[26]](#cite\_note-28) These include the [Phoenicians](/wiki/Phoenicia), [Carthaginians](/wiki/Ancient\_Carthage), [Greeks](/wiki/Ancient\_Greece), and [Romans](/wiki/Ancient\_Rome) in [antiquity](/wiki/Ancient\_Mediterranean\_Sea); the [Arabs](/wiki/Arabs), [Normans](/wiki/Normans), and [Aragonese](/wiki/Crown\_of\_Aragon) during the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle\_Ages); and the [Knights Hospitaller](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller), [French](/wiki/French\_people), and [British](/wiki/British\_people) in the [modern era](/wiki/Modern\_era).[[27]](#cite\_note-29)[[28]](#cite\_note-30) Malta came under [British rule](/wiki/Crown\_colony) in the early 19th century and served as the headquarters for the British [Mediterranean Fleet](/wiki/Mediterranean\_Fleet). It was [besieged by the Axis powers during World War II](/wiki/Siege\_of\_Malta\_(World\_War\_II)) and was an important Allied base for North Africa and the Mediterranean.[[29]](#cite\_note-31)[[30]](#cite\_note-32) Malta achieved independence in 1964,[[31]](#cite\_note-33) and established its current parliamentary republic in 1974.

It has been a member state of the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth\_of\_Nations) and the [United Nations](/wiki/United\_Nations) since independence; it joined the European Union in 2004 and the [eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone) monetary union in 2008.[[32]](#cite\_note-34) Malta's long history of foreign rule and its proximity to both Europe and North Africa have influenced its art, music, cuisine, and architecture. Malta has close historical and cultural ties to Italy and especially Sicily; and while Maltese and English are its official languages, between 62 and 66 percent of [Maltese people](/wiki/Maltese\_people) speak or have significant knowledge of the [Italian language](/wiki/Italian\_language), which had official status from 1530 to 1934.[[33]](#cite\_note-Eurobarometer-35)[[34]](#cite\_note-skills2022-36) Malta was an [early centre of Christianity](/wiki/Early\_Christianity), and [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic\_Church\_in\_Malta) is the state religion, although the country's [constitution](/wiki/Constitution\_of\_Malta) guarantees freedom of conscience and religious worship.[[35]](#cite\_note-constitution-37)[[36]](#cite\_note-cia-38) Malta is a developed country with an advanced, [high-income economy](/wiki/Economy\_of\_Malta). It is heavily reliant on tourism, attracting both travellers and a growing expatriate community with its warm climate, numerous recreational areas, and architectural and historical monuments, including three [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO) World Heritage Sites: [Ħal Saflieni Hypogeum](/wiki/%C4%A6al\_Saflieni\_Hypogeum),[[37]](#cite\_note-39) Valletta,[[38]](#cite\_note-40) and seven [megalithic temples](/wiki/Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta), which are some of the oldest free-standing structures in the world.[[39]](#cite\_note-41)[[40]](#cite\_note-42)[[41]](#cite\_note-43)[[42]](#cite\_note-b1-44) ## Name [(/w/index.php?title=Malta&action=edit&section=1)] The [English](/wiki/English\_language) name \*[Malta](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Malta)\* derives from [Italian](/wiki/Italian\_language) and [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_language)\*Malta\*, from medieval [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic\_language)\*Māliṭā\* ([مَالِطَا](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%D9%85%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%B7%D8%A7)), from classical [Latin](/wiki/Latin\_language)\*[Melita](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Melita)\*,[[43]](#cite\_note-45) from [Latinised](/wiki/Latinization\_of\_names) or [Doric](/wiki/Doric\_Greek\_language) forms[[44]](#cite\_note-46) of the [ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient\_Greek\_language)\*Melítē\* ([Μελίτη](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CE%9C%CE%B5%CE%BB%CE%AF%CF%84%CE%B7)) of uncertain origin.

The name \*Melítē\*—shared by the [Croatian](/wiki/Croatia)[island](/wiki/Islands\_of\_Croatia)[Mljet](/wiki/Mljet) in antiquity—literally means "place of honey" or "sweetness", derived from the combining form of \*méli\* ([μέλι](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CE%BC%CE%AD%CE%BB%CE%B9), "honey" or any similarly sweet thing)[[45]](#cite\_note-47) and the [suffix](/wiki/Suffix)\*-ē\* ([-η](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-%CE%B7)). The ancient Greeks may have given the island this name after Malta's [endemic subspecies](/wiki/Maltese\_honey\_bee) of [bees](/wiki/Bee).[[46]](#cite\_note-malticross-48) Alternatively, other scholars argue for derivation of the Greek name from an original [Phoenician](/wiki/Phoenician\_language) or [Punic](/wiki/Punic\_language)\*Maleth\* ([𐤌𐤋𐤈](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%F0%90%A4%8C%F0%90%A4%8B%F0%90%A4%88), mlṭ), meaning "haven"[[47]](#cite\_note-49), "refuge"[[48]](#cite\_note-50) or "port"[[49]](#cite\_note-51) in reference to the [Grand Harbour](/wiki/Grand\_Harbour) and its primary settlement at [Cospicua](/wiki/Cospicua) following the sea level rise that separated the [Maltese islands](/wiki/Maltese\_islands) and flooded its original coastal settlements in the 10th centuryBC.[[50]](#cite\_note-vel-52) The name was then applied to all of [Malta](/wiki/Malta\_Island) by the Greeks and to [its ancient capital](/wiki/Melite\_(ancient\_city)) at [Mdina](/wiki/Mdina) by the Romans.[[50]](#cite\_note-vel-52) \*Malta\* and its [demonym](/wiki/Demonym)\*[Maltese](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Maltese)\* are attested in English from the late 16th century.[[51]](#cite\_note-53)[English Bible translations](/wiki/List\_of\_English\_Bible\_translations) including the 1611 [King James Version](/wiki/King\_James\_Version) long used the [Vulgate Latin](/wiki/Vulgate\_Latin) form \*Melita\*, although the 1525 [Tyndale Bible](/wiki/Tyndale\_Bible) used the transliteration \*Melite\* instead. \*Malta\* is widely used in more recent versions.[[52]](#cite\_note-54) ## History [(/w/index.php?title=Malta&action=edit&section=2)] Main article: [History of Malta](/wiki/History\_of\_Malta) ### Prehistory [(/w/index.php?title=Malta&action=edit&section=3)] See also: [Megalithic Temples of Malta](/wiki/Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta), [Ħal Saflieni Hypogeum](/wiki/%C4%A6al\_Saflieni\_Hypogeum), and [Għar Dalam](/wiki/G%C4%A7ar\_Dalam) Malta has been inhabited from at least c. 6500 BC, with the arrival of [Mesolithic](/wiki/Mesolithic) hunter-gatherers likely originating from Sicily.

Discoveries at [Latnija Cave](/wiki/Latnija\_Cave) led by the Maltese archaeologist Eleanor Scerri included the remains of hearths, stone tools and an abundant and diverse range of animal bones. These included indigenous red deer that are now extinct, fish and marine mammals, as well as abundant edible marine [gastropods](/wiki/Gastropods). To arrive on Malta, these hunter-gatherers had to cross around 100 km of open water, documenting the longest yet-known sea crossing by hunter-gatherers in the Mediterranean.[[25]](#cite\_note-:0-27) The extinction of the [dwarf hippos](/wiki/Hippopotamus\_melitensis), [giant swans](/wiki/Cygnus\_falconeri) and [dwarf elephants](/wiki/Palaeoloxodon\_falconeri) has historically been linked to the earliest arrival of humans on Malta.[[53]](#cite\_note-55) However this seems unlikely since recent work suggests these animals went extinct many thousands of years before the arrival of the first people,[[54]](#cite\_note-56) and no such animals were found in association with the earliest known Mesolithic hunter-gatherers.[[25]](#cite\_note-:0-27) The claim for an earlier occupation of the island by [Neanderthals](/wiki/Neanderthal) is widely rejected.[[25]](#cite\_note-:0-27)Supplementary Information[Neolithic Farmers](/wiki/Early\_European\_Farmers), also originating from Sicily, are thought to have arrived on the islands by around 5400 BC.[[25]](#cite\_note-:0-27) Prehistoric farming settlements dating to the [Early Neolithic](/wiki/Early\_Neolithic) include the [Għar Dalam](/wiki/G%C4%A7ar\_Dalam) cave site in the uppermost layers associated with domesticated animals.[[55]](#cite\_note-Skeates2010-57) The Neolithic population on Malta grew [cereals](/wiki/Cereals), raised livestock and, in common with other ancient Mediterranean cultures, worshipped a [fertility figure](/wiki/Mother\_goddess).[[56]](#cite\_note-58) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Ggantija\_Temples%2C\_Xaghra%2C\_Gozo.jpg/250px-Ggantija\_Temples%2C\_Xaghra%2C\_Gozo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ggantija\_Temples,\_Xaghra,\_Gozo.jpg) [Ġgantija](/wiki/%C4%A0gantija)[megalithic](/wiki/Megalith) temple complex A culture of [megalithic](/wiki/Megalith) temple builders then either supplanted or arose from this early period.

Around 3500 BC, these people built some of the oldest existing free-standing structures in the world in the form of the megalithic [Ġgantija](/wiki/%C4%A0gantija) temples on [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo);[[57]](#cite\_note-otsf-59) other early temples include those at [Ħaġar Qim](/wiki/%C4%A6a%C4%A1ar\_Qim) and [Mnajdra](/wiki/Mnajdra).[[42]](#cite\_note-b1-44)[[58]](#cite\_note-60)[[59]](#cite\_note-61) The temples have distinctive architecture, typically a complex [trefoil](/wiki/Trefoil) design, and were used from 4000 to 2500 BC. Tentative information suggests that [animal sacrifices](/wiki/Animal\_sacrifice) were made to the goddess of fertility, whose statue is now in the National Museum of Archaeology in Valletta.[[60]](#cite\_note-62) Another archaeological feature of the Maltese Islands often attributed to these ancient builders is equidistant uniform grooves dubbed "cart tracks" or "cart ruts", which can be found in several locations throughout the islands, with the most prominent being those found in [Misraħ Għar il-Kbir ("Clapham Junction")](/wiki/Misra%C4%A7\_G%C4%A7ar\_il-Kbir). These may have been caused by wooden-wheeled carts eroding soft limestone.[[61]](#cite\_note-63)[[62]](#cite\_note-64) The culture apparently disappeared from the islands around 2500 BC, possibly due to famine or disease. After 2500 BC, the Maltese Islands were depopulated for several decades until an influx of [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze\_Age) immigrants, a culture that [cremated](/wiki/Cremation) its dead and introduced smaller megalithic structures called [dolmens](/wiki/Dolmen).[[63]](#cite\_note-65) They are claimed to belong to a population certainly different from that which built the previous megalithic temples.

It is presumed the population arrived from [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily) because of the similarity of Maltese dolmens to some small constructions found there.[[64]](#cite\_note-Piccolo-dolmens-66) ### Phoenicians, Carthaginians, and Romans [(/w/index.php?title=Malta&action=edit&section=4)] See also: [Magna Graecia](/wiki/Magna\_Graecia), [Phoenicia](/wiki/Phoenicia), [Cippi of Melqart](/wiki/Cippi\_of\_Melqart), [Ancient Rome](/wiki/Ancient\_Rome), [Sicilia (Roman province)](/wiki/Sicilia\_(Roman\_province)), and [Byzantine Malta](/wiki/Byzantine\_Malta) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Justinian555AD.png/250px-Justinian555AD.png)](/wiki/File:Justinian555AD.png) The lands which comprise modern-day Malta were a part of the Byzantine Empire (the empire in 555 under [Justinian the Great](/wiki/Justinian\_the\_Great), at its greatest extent since the fall of the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western\_Roman\_Empire) (its [vassals](/wiki/Vassal\_state) in pink)).

[Phoenician](/wiki/Phoenicia) traders[[65]](#cite\_note-ndmh-67)[colonised](/wiki/Phoenician\_colonization) the islands under the name Ann (𐤀𐤍𐤍‎, ʾNN)[[66]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTECulican1992-68)[[67]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEFiligheddu2006-69)[[50]](#cite\_note-vel-52) sometime after 1000 BC[[13]](#cite\_note-ashby-15) as a stop on their trade routes from the eastern [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean\_Sea) to [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall).[[68]](#cite\_note-70) Their seat of government was apparently at [Mdina](/wiki/Mdina), which shared the island's name;[[66]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTECulican1992-68)[[67]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEFiligheddu2006-69) the primary port was at [Cospicua](/wiki/Cospicua) on the [Grand Harbour](/wiki/Grand\_Harbour), which they called Maleth.[[50]](#cite\_note-vel-52) After the [fall of Phoenicia](/wiki/Siege\_of\_Tyre\_(332\_BC)) in 332 BC, the area came under the control of [Carthage](/wiki/Carthage).[[13]](#cite\_note-ashby-15)[[69]](#cite\_note-maltihist-71) During this time, the people on Malta mainly cultivated [olives](/wiki/Olive) and [carob](/wiki/Ceratonia\_siliqua) and produced textiles.[[69]](#cite\_note-maltihist-71) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Roman\_Malta.jpg/250px-Roman\_Malta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Roman\_Malta.jpg) [Roman](/wiki/Ancient\_Rome) mosaic from the [Domvs Romana](/wiki/Domvs\_Romana) During the [First Punic War](/wiki/First\_Punic\_War), the island was conquered after harsh fighting by [Marcus Atilius Regulus](/wiki/Marcus\_Atilius\_Regulus).[[70]](#cite\_note-ei-72) After the failure of his expedition, the island fell back in the hands of Carthage, only to be [conquered again](/wiki/Capture\_of\_Malta\_(218\_BC)) during the [Second Punic War](/wiki/Second\_Punic\_War) in 218 BC by the [Roman consul](/wiki/Roman\_consul)[Tiberius Sempronius Longus](/wiki/Tiberius\_Sempronius\_Longus\_(consul\_218\_BC)).[[70]](#cite\_note-ei-72) Malta became a \*[Foederata Civitas](/wiki/Civitas)\*, a designation that meant it was exempt from paying [tribute](/wiki/Tribute) or the rule of [Roman law](/wiki/Roman\_law), and fell within the jurisdiction of the [province](/wiki/Roman\_province) of [Sicily](/wiki/Sicilia\_(Roman\_province)).[[46]](#cite\_note-malticross-48) Its capital at [Mdina](/wiki/Mdina) was renamed [Melita](/wiki/Melita\_(ancient\_city)) after the Greek and Roman name for the island.

Punic influence, however, remained vibrant on the islands with [Cippi of Melqart](/wiki/Cippi\_of\_Melqart), pivotal in deciphering the [Punic language](/wiki/Punic\_language), dedicated in the second century BC.[[71]](#cite\_note-73)[[72]](#cite\_note-74) Local Roman coinage, which ceased in the first century BC,[[73]](#cite\_note-cassar56–57-75) indicates the slow pace of the island's Romanisation: the last locally minted coins still bear inscriptions in [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient\_Greek) and Punic motifs, showing the resistance of the Greek and Punic cultures.[[74]](#cite\_note-76) In the second century, Emperor [Hadrian](/wiki/Hadrian) upgraded the status of Malta to a [municipium](/wiki/Municipium) or free town: the island's local affairs were administered by four [duumvirs](/wiki/Duumviri) and a municipal senate, while a Roman [procurator](/wiki/Procurator\_(Roman)) living in [Mdina](/wiki/Mdina) represented the [proconsul](/wiki/Proconsul) of Sicily.[[70]](#cite\_note-ei-72) In AD 58,[Paul the Apostle](/wiki/Paul\_the\_Apostle) and [Luke the Evangelist](/wiki/Luke\_the\_Evangelist) were shipwrecked on the islands.[[70]](#cite\_note-ei-72) Paul remained for three months, preaching the [Christian faith](/wiki/Early\_Christianity).[[70]](#cite\_note-ei-72) The island is mentioned at the [Acts of the Apostles](/wiki/Acts\_of\_the\_Apostles) as Melitene ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient\_Greek\_language): Μελιτήνη).[[75]](#cite\_note-77) In 395, when the [Roman Empire was divided for the last time](/wiki/Division\_of\_the\_Roman\_Empire) at the death of [Theodosius I](/wiki/Theodosius\_I), Malta, following Sicily, fell under the control of the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western\_Roman\_Empire).[[76]](#cite\_note-78) During the [Migration Period](/wiki/Migration\_Period) as the [Western Roman Empire declined](/wiki/Fall\_of\_the\_Western\_Roman\_Empire), Malta was conquered or occupied a number of times.[[73]](#cite\_note-cassar56–57-75) From 454 to 464 the islands were subdued by the [Vandals](/wiki/Vandals), and after 464 by the [Ostrogoths](/wiki/Ostrogoths).[[70]](#cite\_note-ei-72) In 533, [Belisarius](/wiki/Belisarius), on his way to conquer the [Vandal Kingdom](/wiki/Vandal\_Kingdom) in North Africa, reunited the islands under Imperial ([Eastern](/wiki/Byzantine\_Empire)) rule.[[70]](#cite\_note-ei-72) Little is known about the [Byzantine rule in Malta](/wiki/Byzantine\_Malta): the island depended on the [theme of Sicily](/wiki/Sicily\_(theme)) and had Greek governors and a small Greek garrison.[[70]](#cite\_note-ei-72) While the bulk of population continued to be constituted by the old, [Latinized](/wiki/Latin\_Church) dwellers, during this period its religious allegiance oscillated between the Pope and the [Patriarch of Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical\_Patriarchate\_of\_Constantinople).[[70]](#cite\_note-ei-72) The Byzantine rule introduced [Greek](/wiki/Greeks) families to the Maltese collective.[[77]](#cite\_note-79) Malta remained under the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine\_Empire) until 870, when it was conquered by the [Arabs](/wiki/Arabs).[[70]](#cite\_note-ei-72)[[78]](#cite\_note-80) ### Arab period and the Middle Ages [(/w/index.php?title=Malta&action=edit&section=5)] See also: [Arab–Byzantine wars](/wiki/Arab%E2%80%93Byzantine\_wars) and [Islam in Malta](/wiki/Islam\_in\_Malta) Malta became involved in the [Arab–Byzantine wars](/wiki/Arab%E2%80%93Byzantine\_wars), and the conquest of Malta is closely linked with [that of Sicily](/wiki/Muslim\_conquest\_of\_Sicily) that began in 827 after [Admiral Euphemius](/wiki/Euphemius\_(Sicily))' betrayal of his fellow Byzantines, requesting that the [Aghlabids](/wiki/Aghlabids) invade the island.[[79]](#cite\_note-stan-81) The [Muslim](/wiki/Muslims) chronicler and geographer [al-Himyari](/wiki/Kitab\_al-Rawd\_al-Mitar) recounts that in 870, following [a violent struggle](/wiki/Siege\_of\_Melite\_(870)) against the defending Byzantines, the Arab invaders, first led by Halaf al-Hadim, and later by Sawada ibn Muhammad,[[80]](#cite\_note-Travel\_Malta-82) pillaged the island, destroying the most important buildings, and leaving it practically uninhabited until it was recolonised by the Arabs from Sicily in 1048–1049.[[80]](#cite\_note-Travel\_Malta-82) It is uncertain whether this new settlement resulted from demographic expansion in Sicily, a higher [standard of living](/wiki/Standard\_of\_living) in Sicily (in which case the recolonisation may have taken place a few decades earlier), or a civil war that broke out among the Arab rulers of Sicily in 1038.[[81]](#cite\_note-83) The [Arab Agricultural Revolution](/wiki/Arab\_Agricultural\_Revolution) introduced new irrigation, cotton, and some fruits.

The [Siculo-Arabic](/wiki/Siculo-Arabic) language was adopted on the island from Sicily; it eventually evolved into the [Maltese language](/wiki/Maltese\_language).[[82]](#cite\_note-84) ### Norman conquest [(/w/index.php?title=Malta&action=edit&section=6)] Main article: [Norman invasion of Malta](/wiki/Norman\_invasion\_of\_Malta) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Roger\_I\_of\_Sicily\_%28Troina%29.jpg/250px-Roger\_I\_of\_Sicily\_%28Troina%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Roger\_I\_of\_Sicily\_(Troina).jpg) [Roger I of Sicily](/wiki/Roger\_I\_of\_Sicily) returned Malta to Christian rule. The [Normans](/wiki/Normans) attacked Malta in 1091, as part of their [conquest of Sicily](/wiki/Norman\_conquest\_of\_Sicily).[[83]](#cite\_note-85) The Norman leader, [Roger I of Sicily](/wiki/Roger\_I\_of\_Sicily), was welcomed by Christian captives,[[46]](#cite\_note-malticross-48) though contrary to myth he did not tear off a portion of his checkered red-and-white banner and present it to the [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_people) in gratitude for having fought on his behalf, forming the basis of the modern [flag of Malta](/wiki/Flag\_of\_Malta).[[46]](#cite\_note-malticross-48)[[84]](#cite\_note-86) Malta became part of the newly formed [Kingdom of Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom\_of\_Sicily), which also covered the island of Sicily and the southern [Italian Peninsula](/wiki/Italian\_Peninsula).[[46]](#cite\_note-malticross-48) The [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic\_Church) was reinstated as the state religion, with Malta under the [See of Palermo](/wiki/See\_of\_Palermo), and some [Norman architecture](/wiki/Norman\_architecture) sprang up around Malta, especially in its ancient capital [Mdina](/wiki/Mdina).[[46]](#cite\_note-malticross-48)[King Tancred](/wiki/King\_Tancred) made Malta a [fief](/wiki/Fief) of the kingdom and installed a [Count of Malta](/wiki/Count\_of\_Malta) in 1192. As the islands were much desired due to their strategic importance, it was during this time that the men of Malta were militarised to fend off attempted conquest; early Counts were skilled [Genoese](/wiki/Republic\_of\_Genoa)[privateers](/wiki/Privateer).[[46]](#cite\_note-malticross-48) The kingdom passed on to the [Hohenstaufen](/wiki/Hohenstaufen) dynasty from 1194 until 1266.

As [Emperor Frederick II](/wiki/Emperor\_Frederick\_II) began to reorganise his Sicilian kingdom, Western culture and religion started to exert their influence more intensely.[[85]](#cite\_note-87) Malta was declared a county and a [marquisate](/wiki/Marquess), but its trade was totally ruined. For a long time it remained solely a fortified [garrison](/wiki/Garrison).[[86]](#cite\_note-88) A mass expulsion of Arabs occurred in 1224, and the entire Christian male population of [Celano](/wiki/Celano) in Abruzzo was deported to Malta in the same year.[[46]](#cite\_note-malticross-48) In 1249 [Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Frederick\_II,\_Holy\_Roman\_Emperor), decreed that all remaining Muslims be expelled from Malta[[87]](#cite\_note-89) or compelled to convert.[[88]](#cite\_note-90)[[89]](#cite\_note-91) For a brief period, the kingdom passed to the [Capetian House of Anjou](/wiki/Capetian\_House\_of\_Anjou),[[90]](#cite\_note-92) but high taxes made the dynasty unpopular in Malta, due in part to [Charles of Anjou](/wiki/Charles\_I\_of\_Naples)'s war against the Republic of Genoa, and the island of [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo) was sacked in 1275.[[46]](#cite\_note-malticross-48) ### Crown of Aragon and the Knights of Malta [(/w/index.php?title=Malta&action=edit&section=7)] See also: [County of Sicily](/wiki/County\_of\_Sicily), [Kingdom of Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom\_of\_Sicily), [Crown of Aragon](/wiki/Crown\_of\_Aragon), [Hospitaller Malta](/wiki/Hospitaller\_Malta), [Great Siege of Malta](/wiki/Great\_Siege\_of\_Malta), and [António Manoel de Vilhena](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio\_Manoel\_de\_Vilhena) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Bandiera\_del\_Regno\_di\_Sicilia\_4.svg/250px-Bandiera\_del\_Regno\_di\_Sicilia\_4.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bandiera\_del\_Regno\_di\_Sicilia\_4.svg) Flag of the Aragonese [Kingdom of Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom\_of\_Sicily) Malta was ruled by the [House of Barcelona](/wiki/House\_of\_Barcelona), the ruling dynasty of the [Crown of Aragon](/wiki/Crown\_of\_Aragon), from 1282 to 1409,[[91]](#cite\_note-culturalheritage.gov.mt-93) with the Aragonese aiding the Maltese insurgents in the [Sicilian Vespers](/wiki/Sicilian\_Vespers) in [the naval battle](/wiki/Battle\_of\_Malta\_(1283)) in [Grand Harbour](/wiki/Grand\_Harbour) in 1283.[[92]](#cite\_note-94) Relatives of the [kings of Aragon](/wiki/Kings\_of\_Aragon) ruled the island until 1409 when it formally passed to the Crown of Aragon.

Early on in the Aragonese ascendancy, the sons of the monarchs received the title [Count of Malta](/wiki/Count\_of\_Malta). During this time much of the local nobility was created. By 1397, however, the bearing of the comital title reverted to a feudal basis, with two families fighting over the distinction. This led King [Martin I of Sicily](/wiki/Martin\_I\_of\_Sicily) to abolish the title. The dispute over the title returned when the title was reinstated a few years later and the Maltese, led by the local nobility, rose up against Count [Gonsalvo Monroy](/wiki/Gonsalvo\_Monroy).[[46]](#cite\_note-malticross-48) Although they opposed the Count, the Maltese voiced their loyalty to the [Sicilian Crown](/wiki/List\_of\_monarchs\_of\_Sicily), which so impressed [King Alfonso V](/wiki/Alfonso\_V\_of\_Aragon) that he did not punish the people for their rebellion. Instead, he promised never to grant the title to a third party and incorporated it back into the crown. The city of [Mdina](/wiki/Mdina) was given the title of \*Città Notabile\*.[[46]](#cite\_note-malticross-48) In September 1429, [Hafsid](/wiki/Hafsid\_dynasty) Berbers [attempted to capture Malta](/wiki/Siege\_of\_Malta\_(1429)) but were repelled by the Maltese. The invaders pillaged the countryside and took about 3,000 inhabitants as [slaves](/wiki/Slavery\_in\_Malta).[[93]](#cite\_note-95) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Malta\_-\_Mdina\_-\_Pjazza\_San\_Pawl\_%2B\_St.\_Paul%27s\_Cathedral\_ex\_01\_ies.jpg/250px-Malta\_-\_Mdina\_-\_Pjazza\_San\_Pawl\_%2B\_St.\_Paul%27s\_Cathedral\_ex\_01\_ies.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_-\_Mdina\_-\_Pjazza\_San\_Pawl\_%2B\_St.\_Paul%27s\_Cathedral\_ex\_01\_ies.jpg) [St.

Paul's Cathedral, Mdina](/wiki/St.\_Paul%27s\_Cathedral,\_Mdina) built in the [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque\_architecture) style On 23 March 1530,[[94]](#cite\_note-96)[Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Charles\_V,\_Holy\_Roman\_Emperor), gave the islands to the [Knights Hospitaller](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller) under the leadership of Frenchman [Philippe Villiers de L'Isle-Adam](/wiki/Philippe\_Villiers\_de\_L%27Isle-Adam),[[95]](#cite\_note-97)[[96]](#cite\_note-autogenerated2-98) in perpetual lease for which they had to pay an annual [tribute of a single Maltese Falcon](/wiki/Tribute\_of\_the\_Maltese\_Falcon).[[97]](#cite\_note-autogenerated6-99)[[98]](#cite\_note-odonnel-100)[[99]](#cite\_note-perez-101)[[100]](#cite\_note-elpais-102)[[101]](#cite\_note-tc1-103)[[102]](#cite\_note-tc2-104)[[103]](#cite\_note-abc-105) These knights, a military religious order also known as the Order of St John and later as the Knights of Malta, had been driven out of [Rhodes](/wiki/Rhodes) by the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman\_Empire) in 1522.[[104]](#cite\_note-106) [The Knights Hospitaller](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller) ruled Malta and Gozo between 1530 and 1798.[[105]](#cite\_note-Devrim.\_2008-107) During this period, the strategic and military importance of the island grew greatly as the small yet efficient fleet of the [Order of Saint John](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller) launched their attacks from this new base targeting the shipping lanes of the Ottoman territories around the Mediterranean Sea.[[105]](#cite\_note-Devrim.\_2008-107)[[106]](#cite\_note-108) In 1551, the population of the island of [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo) (around 5,000 people) were [enslaved](/wiki/Barbary\_slave\_trade) by [Barbary pirates](/wiki/Barbary\_pirates) and taken to the [Barbary Coast](/wiki/Barbary\_Coast) in North Africa.[[107]](#cite\_note-109) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Matteo\_Perez\_d%27\_Aleccio\_%281547-1616%29\_-\_The\_Siege\_of\_Malta%2C\_Attack\_on\_the\_Post\_of\_the\_Castilian\_Knights%2C\_21\_August\_1565\_-\_BHC0257\_-\_Royal\_Museums\_Greenwich.jpg/250px-Matteo\_Perez\_d%27\_Aleccio\_%281547-1616%29\_-\_The\_Siege\_of\_Malta%2C\_Attack\_on\_the\_Post\_of\_the\_Castilian\_Knights%2C\_21\_August\_1565\_-\_BHC0257\_-\_Royal\_Museums\_Greenwich.jpg)](/wiki/File:Matteo\_Perez\_d%27\_Aleccio\_(1547-1616)\_-\_The\_Siege\_of\_Malta,\_Attack\_on\_the\_Post\_of\_the\_Castilian\_Knights,\_21\_August\_1565\_-\_BHC0257\_-\_Royal\_Museums\_Greenwich.jpg) Ottoman attack on the post of the [Castilian knights](/wiki/Langue\_(Knights\_Hospitaller)) on 21 August 1565 The knights, led by Frenchman [Jean Parisot de Valette](/wiki/Jean\_Parisot\_de\_Valette), withstood the [Great Siege of Malta](/wiki/Great\_Siege\_of\_Malta) by the Ottomans in 1565.[[96]](#cite\_note-autogenerated2-98) The knights, with the help of Portuguese, Spanish and Maltese forces, repelled the attack.[[108]](#cite\_note-110)[[109]](#cite\_note-111) After the siege they decided to increase Malta's [fortifications](/wiki/Fortification), particularly in the inner-harbour area, where the new city of [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta), named in honour of Valette, was built.

[Coordinates](/wiki/Geographic\_coordinate\_system): ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/WMA\_button2b.png/20px-WMA\_button2b.png)[35°53′52″N14°30′46″E﻿ / ﻿35.89778°N 14.51278°E﻿ / 35.89778; 14.51278](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Saint\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral&params=35\_53\_52\_N\_14\_30\_46\_E\_type:landmark\_region:MT) From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia (Redirected from [St John's Co-Cathedral](/w/index.php?title=St\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral&redirect=no)) Catholic co-cathedral in Malta   ---   This article \*\*is written like a [travel guide](/wiki/Wikipedia:What\_Wikipedia\_is\_not#Wikipedia\_is\_not\_a\_manual,\_guidebook,\_textbook,\_or\_scientific\_journal)\*\*. Please help [rewrite it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Saint\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral&action=edit) in a [neutral](/wiki/Wikipedia:NPOV), [encyclopedic](/wiki/Wikipedia:Encyclopedic\_style) style or move its content to [Wikivoyage](https://en.wikivoyage.org/wiki/).\*(July 2023)\*  St John's Co-Cathedral  ---  Kon-Katidral ta' San Ġwann  Façade of St John's Co-Cathedral in 2020  [[Wikimedia](https://foundation.wikimedia.org/wiki/Maps\_Terms\_of\_Use)    [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta)  Country   [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic\_Church)  [Sui iuris](/wiki/Sui\_iuris) church   [www.stjohnscocathedral.com](http://www.stjohnscocathedral.com/)  History  Former name(s)   [Co-cathedral](/wiki/Co-cathedral)  Founder   [John the Baptist](/wiki/John\_the\_Baptist)  [Consecrated](/wiki/Consecration)    Grade 1  Designated   [Girolamo Cassar](/wiki/Girolamo\_Cassar)  Architectural type   [Mannerist](/wiki/Renaissance\_architecture#Mannerism) (exterior)[Baroque](/wiki/Baroque\_architecture) (interior)  Groundbreaking   1577  Specifications  Length   40 metres (130 ft)  Nave width   [Limestone](/wiki/Limestone)  Administration  [Archdiocese](/wiki/Diocese)    [Charles Scicluna](/wiki/Charles\_Scicluna)  [Rector](/wiki/Rector\_(ecclesiastical))   \*\*St John's Co-Cathedral\*\* ([Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_language): \*Kon-Katidral ta' San Ġwann\*) is a [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic\_Church)[co-cathedral](/wiki/Co-cathedral) in [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta), [Malta](/wiki/Malta), dedicated to Saint [John the Baptist](/wiki/John\_the\_Baptist). It was built by the [Order of St. John](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller) between 1573 and 1578,[[2]](#cite\_note-2) having been commissioned by Grand Master [Jean de la Cassière](/wiki/Jean\_de\_la\_Cassi%C3%A8re) as the \*\*Conventual Church of Saint John\*\* ([Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_language): \*Knisja Konventwali ta' San Ġwann\*).[[3]](#cite\_note-3) The church was designed by the [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_people) architect [Girolamo Cassar](/wiki/Girolamo\_Cassar),[[4]](#cite\_note-4) who designed several of the more prominent buildings in Valletta. In the 17th century, its interior was redecorated in the [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque\_architecture) style by [Mattia Preti](/wiki/Mattia\_Preti) and other artists.

The interior of the church is considered to be one of the finest examples of high Baroque architecture in Europe.[[5]](#cite\_note-academia-5) ## History [(/w/index.php?title=Saint\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral&action=edit&section=1)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Valletta\_St.John%27s\_Co-Cathedral%2C\_1870s.jpg/250px-Valletta\_St.John%27s\_Co-Cathedral%2C\_1870s.jpg)](/wiki/File:Valletta\_St.John%27s\_Co-Cathedral,\_1870s.jpg) The co-cathedral in the 1870s Following the [Great Siege of 1565](/wiki/Great\_Siege\_of\_Malta), St. John's Co-Cathedral was commissioned in 1572 by [Jean de la Cassière](/wiki/Jean\_de\_la\_Cassi%C3%A8re), [Grand Master](/wiki/List\_of\_Princes\_and\_Grand\_Masters\_of\_the\_Sovereign\_Military\_Order\_of\_Malta) of the [Order of St. John](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller).[[6]](#cite\_note-restoration-6)[[7]](#cite\_note-St\_Lawrence\_Collegiate\_Church-7)[[8]](#cite\_note-8) It was initially named, in the Italian common language of the time, as \*Chiesa Conventuale di San Giovanni Battista\*.[[9]](#cite\_note-9) The church was designed by the Maltese architect [Girolamo Cassar](/wiki/Girolamo\_Cassar), who also designed and oversaw the construction of many important buildings in Valletta.[[6]](#cite\_note-restoration-6)[[7]](#cite\_note-St\_Lawrence\_Collegiate\_Church-7) It is held that Cassar went to [Rhodes](/wiki/Rhodes) to bring a plan of an already existing church that was by then converted to a mosque, to use it as a model for the present co-cathedral. However, Cassar still took decisions over the final design and made modifications, and thus became the sole architect of the co-cathedral.[[10]](#cite\_note-10) Once St. John's was completed in 1577, it became the new [conventual](/wiki/Convent) church of the Order instead of [St. Lawrence's Church](/wiki/St.\_Lawrence%27s\_Church,\_Vittoriosa) in the Order's former headquarters [Birgu](/wiki/Birgu).[[7]](#cite\_note-St\_Lawrence\_Collegiate\_Church-7) Construction of the oratory and sacristy began in 1598, during the magistracy of [Martin Garzez](/wiki/Martin\_Garzez), and they were completed by Grand Master [Alof de Wignacourt](/wiki/Alof\_de\_Wignacourt) in 1604.[[11]](#cite\_note-heritage5-6-11) For the first century of its existence, the church's interior was modestly decorated.

However, in the 1660s, Grand Master [Raphael Cotoner](/wiki/Raphael\_Cotoner) ordered the redecoration of the interior so as to rival the churches of [Rome](/wiki/Rome). Calabrian artist [Mattia Preti](/wiki/Mattia\_Preti) was in charge of the embellishment, and effectively completely transformed the interior in the [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque) style.[[12]](#cite\_note-history-12) The annexes on the side of the cathedral were added later and feature the coat of arms of Grand Master [António Manoel de Vilhena](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio\_Manoel\_de\_Vilhena) who reigned from 1722 to 1736.[[11]](#cite\_note-heritage5-6-11) St. John's remained the conventual church of the Order until the latter was expelled from Malta with the [French occupation](/wiki/French\_occupation\_of\_Malta) in 1798.[[12]](#cite\_note-history-12) Over time, the church grew to equal prominence with the archbishop's [cathedral](/wiki/St.\_Paul%27s\_Cathedral,\_Mdina) at [Mdina](/wiki/Mdina). In the 1820s, the Bishop of Malta was allowed to use St John's as an alternative see and it thus formally became a [co-cathedral](/wiki/Co-cathedral).[[13]](#cite\_note-vallettapianofest-13) In 1831, Sir [Walter Scott](/wiki/Walter\_Scott) called the cathedral a "magnificent church, the most striking interior [he had] ever seen."[[13]](#cite\_note-vallettapianofest-13) In the mid-19th century, [Giuseppe Hyzler](/wiki/Giuseppe\_Hyzler), a leader of the [Nazarene movement](/wiki/Nazarene\_movement), removed some of the Baroque art of the cathedral, including the ornate altar in the Chapel of the Langue of France.[[14]](#cite\_note-nicpmi-14) The cathedral's exterior was slightly damaged by aerial bombardment in 1941, during [World War II](/wiki/World\_War\_II), barely escaping total destruction.

The contents of the cathedral had been transferred elsewhere before the bombardment, so no works of art were lost.[[6]](#cite\_note-restoration-6)[[12]](#cite\_note-history-12) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/St.\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral.JPG/250px-St.\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral.JPG)](/wiki/File:St.\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral.JPG) The façade of St. John's Co-Cathedral being restored in 2014 The cathedral was restored between the late 1980s and the early 1990s. In 2001, the St. John's Co-Cathedral Foundation was set up to administer and conserve the cathedral and its museum. The sides of the cathedral were restored between 2008 and 2010, and a complete restoration of the exterior began in July 2014 directed by architect Jean Frendo and eight restorers. Restoration of the central part of the façade was completed in September 2015[[15]](#cite\_note-15) and project completion was expected in 2017.[[16]](#cite\_note-16) Today, the cathedral is one of the most popular tourist attractions in Malta,[[12]](#cite\_note-history-12) and is listed on the [National Inventory of the Cultural Property of the Maltese Islands](/wiki/National\_Inventory\_of\_the\_Cultural\_Property\_of\_the\_Maltese\_Islands).[[14]](#cite\_note-nicpmi-14) ## Exterior [(/w/index.php?title=Saint\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral&action=edit&section=2)] The cathedral's exterior is built in the [Mannerist](/wiki/Renaissance\_architecture#Mannerism) style typical of its architect [Girolamo Cassar](/wiki/Girolamo\_Cassar).

Its façade is rather plain but well-proportioned, being bounded by two large [bell towers](/wiki/Bell\_tower).[[5]](#cite\_note-academia-5) The doorway is flanked by [Doric](/wiki/Doric\_order) columns supporting an open balcony from which the Grand Master used to address the people on important occasions.[[11]](#cite\_note-heritage5-6-11) On the side are also two empty niches.[[17]](#cite\_note-17)[[18]](#cite\_note-john-18) The niches and the columns are a break with the rest of exterior Mannerist architecture.[[19]](#cite\_note-19)[[18]](#cite\_note-john-18) Overall, the exterior is rather austere and reminiscent of a fortress,[[20]](#cite\_note-20) reflecting both Cassar's style as a military engineer as well as the Order's mood in the years following the [Great Siege of Malta](/wiki/Great\_Siege\_of\_Malta) in 1565.[[12]](#cite\_note-history-12)[[6]](#cite\_note-restoration-6) ## Interior [(/w/index.php?title=Saint\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral&action=edit&section=3)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Nave\_of\_St.\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral.jpg/250px-Nave\_of\_St.\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nave\_of\_St.\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral.jpg) Nave [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/St\_Johns\_Co-Cathedral\_Main\_Altar.JPG/250px-St\_Johns\_Co-Cathedral\_Main\_Altar.JPG)](/wiki/File:St\_Johns\_Co-Cathedral\_Main\_Altar.JPG) Main altar [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Ledger\_stones\_of\_St.\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral.jpg/250px-Ledger\_stones\_of\_St.\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ledger\_stones\_of\_St.\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral.jpg) Some of the many marble [ledger stones](/wiki/Ledger\_stone) on the floor of the cathedral The cathedral's interior is extremely ornate, standing in sharp contrast with the façade. The interior was largely decorated by [Mattia Preti](/wiki/Mattia\_Preti), the Calabrian artist and knight, at the height of the [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque) period. Preti designed the intricate carved stone walls and painted the vaulted ceiling and side altars with scenes from the life of [John the Baptist](/wiki/John\_the\_Baptist). The figures painted into the ceiling next to each column initially appear to the viewer as three-dimensional statues, but on closer inspection we see that the artist cleverly created an illusion of three-dimensionality by his use of shadows and placement.

Also noteworthy is the fact that the carving was all undertaken in-place (in-situ) rather than being carved independently and then attached to the walls (stucco). The Maltese limestone from which the cathedral is built lends itself particularly well to such intricate carving. The whole marble floor is an entire series of tombs, housing about 400 Knights and officers of the Order.[[21]](#cite\_note-21) There is also a [crypt](/wiki/Crypt) containing the tombs of [Grand Masters](/wiki/List\_of\_Princes\_and\_Grand\_Masters\_of\_the\_Sovereign\_Military\_Order\_of\_Malta) like [Philippe Villiers de L'Isle-Adam](/wiki/Philippe\_Villiers\_de\_L%27Isle-Adam), [Claude de la Sengle](/wiki/Claude\_de\_la\_Sengle), [Jean Parisot de Valette](/wiki/Jean\_Parisot\_de\_Valette), and [Alof de Wignacourt](/wiki/Alof\_de\_Wignacourt). In 1666, a project for the main altar by Malta's greatest sculptor, [Melchiorre Cafà](/wiki/Melchiorre\_Caf%C3%A0), was approved and begun. Cafà intended a large sculpture group in [bronze](/wiki/Bronze) depicting the \*[Baptism of Christ](/wiki/Baptism\_of\_Christ)\*. Following Cafà's tragic death in 1667 in a foundry accident while tending to this work in Rome, the plans were abandoned. Only in 1703, [Giuseppe Mazzuoli](/wiki/Giuseppe\_Mazzuoli\_(1644%E2%80%931725)), Cafà's only pupil, finished a marble group of the \*Baptism of Christ\* which might have been influenced by his master's undocumented designs but certainly is strongly dependent on a small baptism group by [Alessandro Algardi](/wiki/Alessandro\_Algardi).[[22]](#cite\_note-22) The funerary monument of Grand Master [Marc'Antonio Zondadari](/wiki/Marc%27Antonio\_Zondadari) (died 1722), nephew of [Pope Alexander VII](/wiki/Pope\_Alexander\_VII), is located close to the main entrance.

It was originally meant to be installed in the Chapel of the Langue of Italy, but it was too large so it was placed in the nave.[[23]](#cite\_note-23) ### Chapels [(/w/index.php?title=Saint\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral&action=edit&section=4)] The cathedral contains nine chapels, one dedicated to Our Lady of Philermos and the rest dedicated to the patron saints of each of the Order's eight [langues](/wiki/Langue\_(Knights\_Hospitaller)) (or divisions). The following chapels are located on the south side of the church:[[24]](#cite\_note-24) \* Chapel of [Our Lady of Philermos](/wiki/Our\_Lady\_of\_Philermos), also known as the Chapel of the Blessed Sacrament – originally contained an icon of Our Lady of Philermos, which had been in the possession of the Order since the [Crusades](/wiki/Crusades). The icon was taken to Russia by Grand Master [Ferdinand von Hompesch zu Bolheim](/wiki/Ferdinand\_von\_Hompesch\_zu\_Bolheim) when the Order was expelled from Malta in 1798,[[25]](#cite\_note-25) and it is now found at the [National Museum of Montenegro](/wiki/National\_Museum\_of\_Montenegro).[[26]](#cite\_note-26)[[27]](#cite\_note-morana-27) \* Chapel of the Langue of Auvergne – dedicated to [Saint Sebastian](/wiki/Saint\_Sebastian). Its altarpiece depicts the saint's martyrdom, and dates back to the 17th century. The chapel contains the funerary monument of Grand Master [Annet de Clermont-Gessant](/wiki/Annet\_de\_Clermont-Gessant) (died 1660).[[28]](#cite\_note-28) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Co-Cath\_-\_Chapel.jpg/250px-Co-Cath\_-\_Chapel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Co-Cath\_-\_Chapel.jpg) Chapel of the Langue of Aragon [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Malta-Valletta-StJohnChapel.jpg/250px-Malta-Valletta-StJohnChapel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta-Valletta-StJohnChapel.jpg) Chapel of the Langue of Italy [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Chapel\_of\_the\_Langue\_of\_Germany%2C\_St.\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral%2C\_Valletta%2C\_Malta.jpg/250px-Chapel\_of\_the\_Langue\_of\_Germany%2C\_St.\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral%2C\_Valletta%2C\_Malta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chapel\_of\_the\_Langue\_of\_Germany,\_St.\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral,\_Valletta,\_Malta.jpg) Chapel of the Langue of Germany \* Chapel of the Langue of Aragon – dedicated to [Saint George](/wiki/Saint\_George).

Its altarpiece is \*[Saint George on Horseback](/wiki/Saint\_George\_on\_Horseback)\*, and it is considered to be one of [Mattia Preti](/wiki/Mattia\_Preti)'s masterpieces. Grand Masters [Martin de Redin](/wiki/Martin\_de\_Redin) (died 1660), [Raphael Cotoner](/wiki/Raphael\_Cotoner) (died 1663), [Nicolas Cotoner](/wiki/Nicolas\_Cotoner) (died 1680) and [Ramon Perellos y Roccaful](/wiki/Ramon\_Perellos\_y\_Roccaful) (died 1720) are buried in funerary monuments in this chapel.[[29]](#cite\_note-29) \* Chapel of the Langue of Castile, Leon and Portugal – dedicated to [Saint James](/wiki/James,\_son\_of\_Zebedee). Its altarpiece depicts the saint in an aesthetically pleasing manner, and it is the work of Mattia Preti. Grand Masters [António Manoel de Vilhena](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio\_Manoel\_de\_Vilhena) (died 1736) and [Manuel Pinto da Fonseca](/wiki/Manuel\_Pinto\_da\_Fonseca) (died 1773) are buried in ornate marble funerary monuments in this chapel.[[30]](#cite\_note-30)[[31]](#cite\_note-31) On the north side of the church, one finds the following chapels: \* Chapel of the Anglo-Bavarian Langue, also known as the Chapel of Relics – dedicated to Saint [Charles Borromeo](/wiki/Charles\_Borromeo). Its altarpiece depicts the presentation of the saint to the Virgin Mary, and it is attributed to Beaumont. The chapel originally contained many relics that the Order acquired through the centuries, but these were removed in 1798.[[32]](#cite\_note-32) \* Chapel of the Langue of Provence – dedicated to [Saint Michael the Archangel](/wiki/Michael\_(archangel)).

Its altarpiece depicts the archangel leading God's armies against Satan, and it also contains marble funerary monuments of Grand Masters [Antoine de Paule](/wiki/Antoine\_de\_Paule) (died 1636) and [Giovanni Paolo Lascaris](/wiki/Giovanni\_Paolo\_Lascaris) (died 1657).[[33]](#cite\_note-33) \* Chapel of the Langue of France – dedicated to the [Conversion](/wiki/Conversion\_of\_Paul\_the\_Apostle) of [Saint Paul](/wiki/Paul\_the\_Apostle). Its altarpiece depicts \*The Conversion of St Paul on the Way to Damascus\*, and it is the work of Mattia Preti. The chapel also contains the funerary monuments of Grand Masters [Adrien de Wignacourt](/wiki/Adrien\_de\_Wignacourt) (died 1697) and [Emmanuel de Rohan-Polduc](/wiki/Emmanuel\_de\_Rohan-Polduc) (died 1797), as well as the Marquis de Wignacourt (died 1615) and [Louis Charles, Count of Beaujolais](/wiki/Louis\_Charles,\_Count\_of\_Beaujolais) (died 1808). Parts of the chapel were redecorated in the late 1830s.[[34]](#cite\_note-34) \* Chapel of the Langue of Italy – dedicated to the [Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Immaculate\_Conception) and Saint [Catherine of Alexandria](/wiki/Catherine\_of\_Alexandria). Its altarpiece depicts \*The Mystic Marriage of St Catherine\*, and it is the work of Mattia Preti. The chapel also contains the funerary monument of Grand Master [Gregorio Carafa](/wiki/Gregorio\_Carafa) (died 1690).[[35]](#cite\_note-35) \* Chapel of the Langue of Germany – dedicated to the [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany\_(holiday)) of Christ. The chapel was originally assigned to the langue of England, but was given to the langue of Germany following the [English Reformation](/wiki/English\_Reformation).

Its altarpiece depicts \*The Adoration of the Magi\* by the Maltese painter [Stefano Erardi](/wiki/Stefano\_Erardi).[[36]](#cite\_note-36) ### Notable works of art [(/w/index.php?title=Saint\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral&action=edit&section=5)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Michelangelo\_Caravaggio\_021.jpg/250px-Michelangelo\_Caravaggio\_021.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michelangelo\_Caravaggio\_021.jpg) \*[The Beheading of Saint John the Baptist](/wiki/The\_Beheading\_of\_St\_John\_the\_Baptist\_(Caravaggio))\*, 1608. Oil on canvas, 361 x 520 cm. Oratory of the co-cathedral [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Monument\_to\_Grand\_Master\_Nicolas\_Cotoner%2C\_St.\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral%2C\_Valletta.jpg/250px-Monument\_to\_Grand\_Master\_Nicolas\_Cotoner%2C\_St.\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral%2C\_Valletta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Monument\_to\_Grand\_Master\_Nicolas\_Cotoner,\_St.\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral,\_Valletta.jpg) Monument to Grand Master [Nicolas Cotoner](/wiki/Nicolas\_Cotoner) by [Domenico Guidi](/wiki/Domenico\_Guidi), at the Chapel of Aragon.[[37]](#cite\_note-37) The painting depicting \*[The Beheading of Saint John the Baptist](/wiki/The\_Beheading\_of\_Saint\_John\_the\_Baptist\_(Caravaggio))\* (1608) by [Caravaggio](/wiki/Caravaggio) (1571–1610) is the most famous work in the church. Considered one of Caravaggio's masterpieces, the largest canvas he painted and the only painting signed by the painter, the canvas is displayed in the Oratory for which it was painted. Restored in the late 1990s in Florence, this painting is one of Caravaggio's most impressive uses of the [chiaroscuro](/wiki/Chiaroscuro) style for which he is most famous with a circle of light illuminating the scene of St John's beheading at the request of Salome. The oratory also houses Caravaggio's \*[Saint Jerome Writing](/wiki/Saint\_Jerome\_Writing\_(Caravaggio,\_Valletta))\* (1607–1608). Another impressive feature of the church is the collection of marble [tombstones](/wiki/Tombstones) in the nave in which were buried important knights. The more important knights were placed closer to the front of the church.

These tomb stones, richly decorated with in-laid marble and with the coats of arms of the knight buried below as well as images relevant to that knight, often telling a story of triumph in battle, form a rich visual display in the church. Adjoining to the church is the St John's Co-Cathedral Museum containing art objects. Among the contents of the museum there are the Flemish Tapestries designed by [Peter Paul Rubens](/wiki/Peter\_Paul\_Rubens), which were donated by Grand Master [Ramon Perellos y Roccaful](/wiki/Ramon\_Perellos\_y\_Roccaful),[[38]](#cite\_note-38)[[39]](#cite\_note-39) paintings of Grand Masters [Jean de la Cassière](/wiki/Jean\_de\_la\_Cassi%C3%A8re), [Nicolas Cotoner](/wiki/Nicolas\_Cotoner) and [Manuel Pinto da Fonseca](/wiki/Manuel\_Pinto\_da\_Fonseca), and paintings which were formerly in the side chapels such as \*St. George killing the Dragon\* by [Francesco Potenzano](/wiki/Francesco\_Potenzano). ## Visiting [(/w/index.php?title=Saint\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral&action=edit&section=6)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Malta\_-\_Valletta\_-\_Triq\_ir-Repubblika\_-\_Misrah\_l-Assedju\_l-Kbir\_-\_St.\_John%27s\_01\_ies.jpg/250px-Malta\_-\_Valletta\_-\_Triq\_ir-Repubblika\_-\_Misrah\_l-Assedju\_l-Kbir\_-\_St.\_John%27s\_01\_ies.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_-\_Valletta\_-\_Triq\_ir-Repubblika\_-\_Misrah\_l-Assedju\_l-Kbir\_-\_St.\_John%27s\_01\_ies.jpg) Visitors' entrance to the co-cathedral St. John's Co-Cathedral is located in the centre of Valletta, and it is a short walk away from the bus terminus near [City Gate](/wiki/City\_Gate\_(Valletta)).

The main entrance of the cathedral is in [St John's Square](/wiki/St\_John%27s\_Square), but the visitors' entrance is from Great Siege Square in Republic Street, facing the [Law Courts](/wiki/Courts\_of\_Justice\_building\_(Valletta)).[[40]](#cite\_note-40) Fees are charged for visitors, but those visiting the cathedral for Mass only do not have to pay the entrance fee.[[41]](#cite\_note-visitor-information-41) ## Gallery [(/w/index.php?title=Saint\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral&action=edit&section=7)] \* Unsourced material may be challenged and removed.\*Find sources:\*["Three Cities" Malta](https://www.google.com/search?as\_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Three+Cities%22+Malta) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Three+Cities%22+Malta+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1)\*\*·\*\*[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Three+Cities%22+Malta&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks)\*\*·\*\*[books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Three+Cities%22+Malta+-wikipedia)\*\*·\*\*[scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Three+Cities%22+Malta)\*\*·\*\*[JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Three+Cities%22+Malta&acc=on&wc=on)\*(June 2015)\* \*([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance\_template\_removal))\*  Three Cities of [Malta](/wiki/Malta)  ---  \* [Cospicua](/wiki/Cospicua) \* [Birgu](/wiki/Birgu) \* [Senglea](/wiki/Senglea)  Laconic Name   ---  Birgu[[4]](#cite\_note-4)   Senglea  Bormla[[6]](#cite\_note-6)  ## History [(/w/index.php?title=Three\_Cities\_(Malta)&action=edit&section=1)] Vittoriosa has been settled since the time of the [Phoenicians](/wiki/Phoenicians), but the current city dates back from the time of the [Order of Saint John](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller). Vittoriosa was chosen as the capital city of Malta instead of [Mdina](/wiki/Mdina) upon the arrival of the Order in 1530. After the [1551 attack](/wiki/Invasion\_of\_Gozo\_(1551)), [Senglea](/wiki/Senglea) was built on the peninsula known as \*L'Isola\*. In Vittoriosa, [Fort Saint Angelo](/wiki/Fort\_Saint\_Angelo) was built on the site of the ancient Castrum Maris, and [Fort Saint Michael](/wiki/Fort\_Saint\_Michael) was built on Senglea. The cities were besieged during the [Great Siege of Malta](/wiki/Great\_Siege\_of\_Malta) of 1565, and after the siege was lifted Vittoriosa was given the title of \*Città Vittoriosa\* and Senglea the title of \*Città Invicta\*. In the aftermath of the siege, the city of [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta) was built, and in 1571 the new city became the capital instead of Vittoriosa. The cities' fortifications were strengthened during the reign of Grandmaster [Nicolas Cotoner](/wiki/Nicolas\_Cotoner), and in 1722, Grandmaster [Marc'Antonio Zondadari](/wiki/Marc%27Antonio\_Zondadari) gave the town of [Bormla](/wiki/Bormla) the title of \*Città Cospicua\*.

The Cottonera lines or rather Civitas Cotonera was intended as a city surrounded by fortifications. These impressive fortifications were built according to the plan of Antonio Maurizio Valperga on four of the five hills of Bormla. Originally intended as a much larger defense system which would encompass the Corradino hill and extend over Kalkara to Ricasoli, the final plan intended for the Citta Cottonera to be a city with the fort St. Margerita as a citadel at its centre. The beginning to construction began on 28 August 1670 with great solemnity as follows: 'On that day a general procession of all the regular orders walked from the Parish Church of Burmula, led by the prior of the convent church, who held the famous relic of St. John, glorious protector of the Holy Religion followed by the Grand Master, all the knights and a lot of citizens walked towards the hill, where the bastion of S. Niccolo was planned; here and there, with the usual ceremonies, and blessings, the Grand Master Fra Nicola Cotoner cast various coins bearing his and the city’s mark within the foundations of the wall, as also did the Knights of the Grand Cross while they invoked the Divine help.

The foundation stone was laid with the firing of the guns of all the fortresses.' The six kilometre fortification encircles Bormla with a series of bastions and triumphal baroque gateways. A detail from the cartouche by Albert Clouet of the Civitas Cotonera, shows a central castle or fort; the Fort Santa Margerita surrounded by the present day Cottonera lines with new buildings, palaces and gardens with the Dockyard Creek and most importantly, it shows Bormla forming an essential part of Civitas Cotonera. These areas in Bormla within Clouet's cartouche were and are still spread over 3 areas in present-day Bormla; the lower Santa Margerita area, the Verdala/St. Helen's area and the Dock No. 1/St.Paul's area. Albert Clouet's cartouche excludes the peninsulas of the two neighbouring cities, so it is quite evident that neither Vittoriosa nor Senglea were ever intended to form part of Civitas Cotonera. With the death of Grand Master Cotoner critics of the design discontinued work claiming that it would not withstand a siege. Numerous people of note have affirmed this claim and have even gone as far as to say that the Cottonera lines are not siege proven.

The blockade by the British and the Maltese uprising against the French who had to defend their position by locking themselves within the Cottonera lines for well over a year should be sufficient proof that these claims are spurious. With the discontinuation of work on the Civitas Cotonera, the Fort St. Margarita continued with different redesigns by various Grand Masters to its present appearance as a line of bastions. It is safe to say that by the 1670, the fortifications of Vittoriosa (Vittoriosa) and Senglea were grossly outdated so the building of the Cottonera fortifications during the reign of Grandmaster [Nicolas Cotoner](/wiki/Nicolas\_Cotoner) were intended to protect the harbour and Valletta from the landward side against possible attacks by the Ottomans landing at Marsaxlokk. Bormla[[7]](#cite\_note-7)'s ancient name of Burmula has been defined as Phoenician or Punic which indicates this city's ancient origins. In his book \*Notizie Storiche Sull' Etimologia Dei Nomi\*, Achille Ferris explains that ‘Burmula’ is made up of the words ‘Bur’ and ‘Mula’; ‘Bur’ for ‘place’ and ‘Mula’ for ‘high’; an eminent place. Bormla’s domination in height over the surrounding areas tends to corroborate this version.

In the book \*Della lingua punica presentemente usata da maltesi &c\*, [Giovanni Pietro Francesco Agius de Soldanis](/wiki/Giovanni\_Pietro\_Francesco\_Agius\_de\_Soldanis) refers to the t'Ghuxa area in Bormla and the ancient name of Burmula itself as proof that these have Punic origins. The meaning of these words in themselves indicate that Burmula was regarded as an eminent place that stands out - a conspicuous place hence the term 'Cospicua' is as ancient as the name 'Burmula' because it is a direct translation of the site's Phoenician name. It is said that in 1722 on seeing the Bormla's eminence and double line of fortifications that surround it, Grand Master [Marc'Antonio Zondadari](/wiki/Marc%27Antonio\_Zondadari), honoured the town by proclaiming it as a city, hence the term: \*Città Cospicua\* - The Conspicuous City. During [World War II](/wiki/Siege\_of\_Malta\_(World\_War\_II)), the Three Cities were heavily bombed by the Italians and Germans, and they were rebuilt in the 1950s and 1960s. Many people from the educated and professional classes moved out of the Cottonera area after the war, and the cities were subsequently repopulated by people from the working class. The area subsequently had higher levels of illiteracy, unemployment and people on welfare when compared to the rest of Malta.

Since the 1990s, several regeneration projects have been carried out, including the establishment of the [Malta Maritime Museum](/wiki/Malta\_Maritime\_Museum) in Vittoriosa and the [American University of Malta](/wiki/American\_University\_of\_Malta) in Cospicua.[[8]](#cite\_note-8) ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/WMA\_button2b.png/20px-WMA\_button2b.png) WikiMiniAtlas [35°52′37″N14°31′19″E﻿ / ﻿35.87694°N 14.52194°E﻿ / 35.87694; 14.52194](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Three\_Cities\_(Malta)&params=35\_52\_37\_N\_14\_31\_19\_E\_source:cawiki\_region:MT\_type:landmark) ## See also [(/w/index.php?title=Three\_Cities\_(Malta)&action=edit&section=2)] \* [Three Villages](/wiki/Three\_Villages) ## References [(/w/index.php?title=Three\_Cities\_(Malta)&action=edit&section=3)] 1. \*\*[^](#cite\_ref-1)\*\*Rudolf, Uwe Jens (2018). [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/e/e7/Cscr-featured.svg/20px-Cscr-featured.svg.png)](/wiki/Wikipedia:Featured\_lists) From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Malta\_location\_map.svg/330px-Malta\_location\_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Malta\_location\_map.svg) ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Red\_pog.svg/20px-Red\_pog.svg.png) [Ħal Saflieni Hypogeum](/wiki/Hypogeum\_of\_%C4%A6al-Saflieni) ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Red\_pog.svg/20px-Red\_pog.svg.png) [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta) ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Blue\_000080\_pog.svg/20px-Blue\_000080\_pog.svg.png) [Ġgantija](/wiki/%C4%A0gantija) ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Blue\_000080\_pog.svg/20px-Blue\_000080\_pog.svg.png) [Ħaġar Qim](/wiki/%C4%A6a%C4%A1ar\_Qim) ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Blue\_000080\_pog.svg/20px-Blue\_000080\_pog.svg.png) [Mnajdra](/wiki/Mnajdra) ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Blue\_000080\_pog.svg/20px-Blue\_000080\_pog.svg.png) [Skorba](/wiki/Skorba\_Temples) ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Blue\_000080\_pog.svg/20px-Blue\_000080\_pog.svg.png) [Ta' Ħaġrat](/wiki/Ta%27\_%C4%A6a%C4%A1rat\_Temples) ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Blue\_000080\_pog.svg/20px-Blue\_000080\_pog.svg.png) [Tarxien Temples](/wiki/Tarxien\_Temples) [class=notpageimage Site  Location  UNESCO data   ---  ---  ---  [Ħal Saflieni Hypogeum](/wiki/Hypogeum\_of\_%C4%A6al-Saflieni)  [Paola](/wiki/Paola,\_Malta)  130; iii (cultural)    1980  The city of Valletta was founded in 1566. The [Knights of St John](/wiki/Knights\_of\_St\_John) conceived and planned the city as a single, holistic creation of the late [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance), with a uniform grid plan within fortified [city walls](/wiki/Fortifications\_of\_Valletta). Although experiencing renovations and an extensive damage during [World War II](/wiki/World\_War\_II), a high proportion of the urban fabric has been preserved or restored. Some of the Valletta's 320 monuments include [Saint John's Co-Cathedral](/wiki/Saint\_John%27s\_Co-Cathedral), the [Grandmaster's Palace](/wiki/Grandmaster%27s\_Palace\_(Valletta)), the [Auberge de Castille](/wiki/Auberge\_de\_Castille), the [Auberge de Provence](/wiki/Auberge\_de\_Provence), the [Auberge d'Italie](/wiki/Auberge\_d%27Italie), the [Auberge d'Aragon](/wiki/Auberge\_d%27Aragon), and the churches of [Our Lady of Victory](/wiki/Church\_of\_Our\_Lady\_of\_Victories,\_Valletta), [St. Catherine](/wiki/Church\_of\_Saint\_Catherine\_of\_Italy,\_Valletta) and [il Gesù](/wiki/Franciscan\_Church\_of\_St\_Mary\_of\_Jesus), as well as the 18th century constructions such as the [Auberge de Bavière](/wiki/Auberge\_de\_Bavi%C3%A8re), the [Church of the Shipwreck of St Paul](/wiki/Collegiate\_Parish\_Church\_of\_St\_Paul%27s\_Shipwreck), and the [Manoel Theatre](/wiki/Manoel\_Theatre).[[10]](#cite\_note-10)  [Megalithic Temples of Malta](/wiki/Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta)  [Xagħra](/wiki/Xag%C4%A7ra), [Qrendi](/wiki/Qrendi), [Mġarr](/wiki/M%C4%A1arr), and [Tarxien](/wiki/Tarxien)  132; iv (cultural)  ## Tentative list [(/w/index.php?title=List\_of\_World\_Heritage\_Sites\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=2)] In addition to the sites inscribed on the World Heritage list, member states can maintain a list of tentative sites that they may consider for nomination.

Nominations for the World Heritage list are only accepted if the site has previously been listed on the tentative list.[[11]](#cite\_note-11) As of 2018, Malta had seven such sites on its tentative list, all of which were added in 1998.[[2]](#cite\_note-malta-2)  Image  Year listed  Description  ---  ---  ---    1998  The coastlines in various parts of the Maltese Islands contain cliffs with a rich biodiversity of rare flora and fauna.[[12]](#cite\_note-12)  Qawra/Dwejra  [San Lawrenz](/wiki/San\_Lawrenz), [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo)  vii, viii, ix, x (natural)    1998  A small fortified city at a strategic position on a hill dominates the surrounding countryside. First settlements on the site date back to the Bronze Age. The settlement was expanded in the Medieval era and transformed into a purely military outpost by the [Order of St. John](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller) in the 16th century. Nevertheless, some monuments in the [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque) style were constructed as well, including the [Cathedral Church](/wiki/Cathedral\_of\_the\_Assumption,\_Gozo).[[14]](#cite\_note-14)  [Knights' Fortifications around the Harbours of Malta](/wiki/Fortifications\_of\_Malta#Harbour\_area)  [Birgu](/wiki/Birgu), [Senglea](/wiki/Senglea), [Floriana](/wiki/Floriana), [Cospicua](/wiki/Cospicua), [Kalkara](/wiki/Kalkara), [Gżira](/wiki/G%C5%BCira) and [Sliema](/wiki/Sliema)  i, ii, iv (cultural)    1998  Mdina is a small [fortified](/wiki/Fortifications\_of\_Mdina) city with a mainly medieval character, but also containing significant Baroque buildings, such as [St. Paul's Cathedral](/wiki/St.\_Paul%27s\_Cathedral,\_Mdina).[[16]](#cite\_note-16)  [Maltese Catacomb Complexes](/wiki/Catacombs\_of\_Malta)  various locations on the [main island of Malta](/wiki/Malta\_(island))  i, ii, iii (cultural)    1998  The line of fortifications was built in the 19th century by the British.

Initially the fortifications consisted of three independent forts ([Fort Madalena](/wiki/Fort\_Madalena), [Fort Mosta](/wiki/Fort\_Mosta), and [Fort Binġemma](/wiki/Fort\_Bin%C4%A1emma)), which were linked together by a continuous wall in the 1890s. By 1907, the fortifications had lost their military significance and were abandoned.[[18]](#cite\_note-18)   \* [v](/wiki/Template:Malta\_topics) \* [t](/wiki/Template\_talk:Malta\_topics) \*
