From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Hafenpromenade\_Sliema.jpg/330px-Hafenpromenade\_Sliema.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hafenpromenade\_Sliema.jpg) View of [Gżira](/wiki/G%C5%BCira) showing traffic on Triq ix-Xatt and boats in Marsamxett Harbour The \*\*transport system in [Malta](/wiki/Malta)\*\* is small but extensive, and the islands' domestic system of [public transport](/wiki/Public\_transport) is reliant on [buses](/wiki/Bus) and [taxis](/wiki/Taxi), although there was both a [railway](/wiki/Malta\_Railway) and a [tramway](/wiki/Malta\_Tramways) in the past. Public transport in Malta has been [free of charge](/wiki/Free\_public\_transport) since October 2022 for all residents with a Tallinja Card (a personalised public transport card), for which any resident of Malta can register.[[1]](#cite\_note-European\_Commission-1) Malta's primary international connections are the [Malta International Airport](/wiki/Malta\_International\_Airport) in [Luqa](/wiki/Luqa) and by sea the [Valletta Ferry Terminal](/w/index.php?title=Valletta\_Ferry\_Terminal&action=edit&redlink=1). Malta's primary commercial connection is the [Malta Freeport](/wiki/Malta\_Freeport) (the 3rd largest [transshipment](/wiki/Transshipment)[port](/wiki/Port) in the Mediterranean Sea) in [Birżebbuġa](/wiki/Bir%C5%BCebbu%C4%A1a). Created in 2010 the authority in charge of all Transport services in the country is [Transport Malta](/wiki/Transport\_Malta),[[2]](#cite\_note-2) consumed the services of the Malta Maritime Authority, the Malta Transport Authority and the Director and Directorate of Civil Aviation.[[3]](#cite\_note-3) The Ministry of Culture of Malta sanctioned Touring Club Malta to set up a Transport Museum.[[4]](#cite\_note-4) ## Land transport [(/w/index.php?title=Transport\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=1)] ### Roads [(/w/index.php?title=Transport\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=2)] Main article: [Roads in Malta](/wiki/Roads\_in\_Malta) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Malta\_-\_Hamrun\_-\_Triq\_l-Indipendenza\_02\_ies.jpg/250px-Malta\_-\_Hamrun\_-\_Triq\_l-Indipendenza\_02\_ies.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_-\_Hamrun\_-\_Triq\_l-Indipendenza\_02\_ies.jpg) Triq l-Indipendenza in [Ħamrun](/wiki/%C4%A6amrun) Traffic in Malta [drives on the left](/wiki/Driving\_on\_the\_left\_or\_right), being one of only four countries in Europe to do so, along with the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United\_Kingdom), [Ireland](/wiki/Republic\_of\_Ireland) and [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus).

Car ownership in Malta is exceedingly high given the very small size of the islands. The country has a car ownership rate of 766 motor vehicles per 1,000 people.[[5]](#cite\_note-5) As of October 2021, the number of registered cars amounted to 411,056,[[6]](#cite\_note-6) giving an auto-mobile density of 1253.8 per km². Malta has 3,096 kilometres of road, 2,704 km (87.3%) of which are paved and 392 km are unpaved as of 2008.[[7]](#cite\_note-7) 114 km of Malta's roads are on the [Trans-European Transport Network](/wiki/Trans-European\_Transport\_Network) but it has no motorways.[[8]](#cite\_note-8) Roads in Malta are maintained and operated by Infrastructure Malta.[[9]](#cite\_note-9) The official road user guide for Malta is \*[The Highway Code](/wiki/The\_Highway\_Code\_(Malta))\*.[[10]](#cite\_note-10) ### Buses [(/w/index.php?title=Transport\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=3)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Maltesisk\_buss\_1.jpg/250px-Maltesisk\_buss\_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maltesisk\_buss\_1.jpg) Traditional Maltese bus [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Malta\_Buses\_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Malta\_Buses\_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_Buses\_(cropped).jpg) Modern buses at Valletta City Gate Bus Station Main article: [Malta bus](/wiki/Malta\_bus) [Buses](/wiki/Bus) are the primary method of public transport for the Maltese Islands and have been in operation there since 1905, offering a cheap and frequent service to many parts of Malta and [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo). The vast majority of buses on Malta depart from a terminus in [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta).

Malta's buses carried over 40 million passengers in 2015.[[11]](#cite\_note-11) The traditional classic Maltese buses, which were in operation until 2011 and still provide tourist-oriented services to this day, have become visitor attractions in their own right due to their uniqueness, and are depicted on many Maltese advertisements to promote tourism as well as on gifts and merchandise for tourists. Prior to their reform there were approximately 500 buses in public transit service, most of them privately owned by the bus drivers themselves, and operated to a unified timetable set by the transport authority. On any one day, half the bus fleet worked on the public transport network (called "route buses"), while the other half were used for private tours and school transport. In July 2011 a new public transport network was installed by [Transport Malta](#Transport\_Malta) (the regulating authority) and on 3 July 2011 it started being operated by [Arriva Malta](/wiki/Arriva\_Malta), which was owned by [Arriva](/wiki/Arriva) (67%) and [Tumas Group](/wiki/Tumas\_Group) (33%), operating as the sole operator on a 10-year contract and running a new 264-strong fleet of buses in a turquoise and cream livery. Unlike the system it replaced, the buses were owned and operated by a single company with the drivers working as employees of Arriva Malta.

When Arriva ceased operations on 1 January 2014 due to financial difficulties, the company was nationalised as Malta Public Transport by the Maltese government as an interim measure while a new bus operator could be found.[[12]](#cite\_note-12)[[13]](#cite\_note-13) As of October 2014 the government has chosen Autobuses Urbanos de León ([Alsa](/wiki/Alsa\_(bus\_company)) subsidiary) as its preferred bus operator for the country, and although the agreement has yet to be fully determined and signed, it is planned that they will be in operation in January 2015.[[14]](#cite\_note-14) During the closing days of December 2014, the [Times of Malta](/wiki/Times\_of\_Malta) and other newspapers were reporting that the company had now signed contracts and purchased the existing operation for 8 million euros.[[15]](#cite\_note-15)[[16]](#cite\_note-16) They duly took over the business on January 8, 2015 with their takeover being effected as a "soft launch". The existing name - Malta Public Transport - is to be retained instead of using Autobuses Urbanos de León and nothing will have changed from a passenger perspective initially.

The buses are to be repainted into a new livery of light green and white and during a press announcement to mark the formal takeover of operations on the day, several repainted buses were lined up for a photo call to show off the new livery, these being two of the leased in 2014 [Optare Solos](/wiki/Optare\_Solo), one of the leased in 2014 Wright Volvos, one each of the new in 2011 [King Long](/wiki/King\_Long) XMQ6900J and XMQ6127J buses. By February the sub contracted buses from UBS were replaced - temporarily - with 32 dual-door [Mercedes-Benz Citaro](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz\_Citaro)[[17]](#cite\_note-17) buses operated by ALESA (as opposed to the situation until then of sub contracting of both bus and driver from UBS) until new [Otokar](/wiki/Otokar) Vectio C dual-door single deck buses currently on order[[18]](#cite\_note-18) have arrive later in 2015. These new buses will number 142 in total and used to augment the existing fleet as the revised route network is incrementally rolled out during the course of 2015 with the full service planned not expected to be fully realized until 2016, at which time the 23 million euro subsidy for 2015 will rise to 29 million thereafter.[[19]](#cite\_note-19) In January 2019, the [Government](/wiki/Government\_of\_Malta) has said that young people who are between 16 and 20-years old in 2018 can now travel by bus for free.

People between 17 and 19-years old will travel for free between 1 January 2019 and 31 December 2019. The government also said that those who will turn 16 this year will start travelling for free on their birthday and will keep benefiting from the scheme until their 17th birthday. 20-year-olds will benefit until they reach 21.[[20]](#cite\_note-20) Since October 2022 public transport (which includes buses) in Malta and Gozo has been free of charge for all residents with a Tallinja Card (a personalised public transport card), of which any Malta resident can register for.[[21]](#cite\_note-21)[[1]](#cite\_note-European\_Commission-1) ### Railway [(/w/index.php?title=Transport\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=4)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Malta\_Railway\_Map.svg/250px-Malta\_Railway\_Map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Malta\_Railway\_Map.svg) Old Malta Railway tracks (1883–1931) Main article: [Malta Railway](/wiki/Malta\_Railway) The [Malta Railway](/wiki/Malta\_Railway) was the only [railway line](/wiki/Railway\_line) ever on the island of [Malta](/wiki/Malta), and it consisted of a single railway line from [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta) to [Mdina](/wiki/Mdina). It was a [single-track](/wiki/Single\_track\_(rail)) line in [metre gauge](/wiki/Metre\_gauge\_railway), operating from 1883 to 1931 between the capital city of [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta) and the army barracks at [Mtarfa](/wiki/Mtarfa) / [Mdina](/wiki/Mdina). The railway was known in [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_language) as \*il-vapur tal-art\* (the land ship). ### Tramways [(/w/index.php?title=Transport\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=5)] Main article: [Malta Tramways](/wiki/Malta\_Tramways) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Malta\_Trams\_Map\_EN.svg/250px-Malta\_Trams\_Map\_EN.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Malta\_Trams\_Map\_EN.svg) Map of the Maltese tramways, 1905–1929 Electric [tramways](/wiki/Tram) operated in Malta from 23 February 1905 till 1929.[[22]](#cite\_note-22) The tramway was connected with two lines,[[23]](#cite\_note-Baedeker1907-23) considered to be a direct line:[[24]](#cite\_note-Baedeker1929-24) 1. [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta) – [Marsa](/wiki/Marsa,\_Malta) – [Paola](/wiki/Paola,\_Malta) – [Cospicua](/wiki/Cospicua) 2.

Valletta – [Ħamrun](/wiki/Hamrun) – [Qormi](/wiki/Qormi) – [Żebbuġ](/wiki/%C5%BBebbu%C4%A1) 3. Valletta – Ħamrun – [Birkirkara](/wiki/Birkirkara) There was no immediate extension of the track to [Mosta](/wiki/Mosta).[[23]](#cite\_note-Baedeker1907-23)[[24]](#cite\_note-Baedeker1929-24) The tracks ran on the road parallel to the [Valletta-Mdina railway line](/wiki/Malta\_Railway), which also allowed the traffic system to be used by cars and buses. The road operation was terminated after the bankruptcy of the company on December 15, 1929 and the infrastructure was rebuilt.[[25]](#cite\_note-25) In 2008, the Halcrow report[[26]](#cite\_note-26) suggested the government to reintroduce two tram lines in Malta: [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta) to [Sliema](/wiki/Sliema) along the coast road, and [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta) to [Ta' Qali](/wiki/Ta%27\_Qali). The report was largely overlooked, as the government focused on reforming the bus transport. In 2016 the government announced a new study on reintroducing the tram.[[27]](#cite\_note-27) The new study, which is expected by late 2020, should also look at metro and monorail options.[[28]](#cite\_note-28) \* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Stra%C3%9Fenbahn-Er%C3%B6ffnungszeremonie\_in\_Malta\_im\_Jahr\_1905\_%28retuschiert%29.jpg/120px-Stra%C3%9Fenbahn-Er%C3%B6ffnungszeremonie\_in\_Malta\_im\_Jahr\_1905\_%28retuschiert%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stra%C3%9Fenbahn-Er%C3%B6ffnungszeremonie\_in\_Malta\_im\_Jahr\_1905\_(retuschiert).jpg) Inauguration, 1905 \* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/20181022-Ansichtskarte-Stra%C3%9Fenbahn\_auf\_Piazza\_St\_Anna-Floriana.jpg/120px-20181022-Ansichtskarte-Stra%C3%9Fenbahn\_auf\_Piazza\_St\_Anna-Floriana.jpg)](/wiki/File:20181022-Ansichtskarte-Stra%C3%9Fenbahn\_auf\_Piazza\_St\_Anna-Floriana.jpg) Tramway in Floriana, Valletta in 1929 ### Metro [(/w/index.php?title=Transport\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=6)] Main article: [Malta Metro](/wiki/Malta\_Metro) In 2021 the Maltese government unveiled a proposal for a €6.2 billion metro network,[[29]](#cite\_note-29) to consist of three lines and 25 stations, of which the majority would be underground.

As of 2023, the project seems to have been shelved.[[30]](#cite\_note-30) ## Maritime transport [(/w/index.php?title=Transport\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=7)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Gozo\_Channel\_Malita.jpg/250px-Gozo\_Channel\_Malita.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gozo\_Channel\_Malita.jpg) The Gozo ferry MV \*Malita\* departs Ċirkewwa [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Malta\_-\_Floriana\_-\_Triq\_l-Ghassara\_tal-Gheneb\_-\_Jean\_de\_la\_Valette\_%28MSTHC%29\_06\_ies.jpg/250px-Malta\_-\_Floriana\_-\_Triq\_l-Ghassara\_tal-Gheneb\_-\_Jean\_de\_la\_Valette\_%28MSTHC%29\_06\_ies.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_-\_Floriana\_-\_Triq\_l-Ghassara\_tal-Gheneb\_-\_Jean\_de\_la\_Valette\_(MSTHC)\_06\_ies.jpg) The Malta-Sicily ferry MV \*Jean De La Valette\* at the [Grand Harbour](/wiki/Grand\_Harbour) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Your\_Wisdom\_ferry.jpg/250px-Your\_Wisdom\_ferry.jpg)](/wiki/File:Your\_Wisdom\_ferry.jpg) Fast ferry MV \*Your Wisdom\* departing Mgarr Malta has three large natural harbours on its main island. There are also two man-made harbours that connect the islands of Malta and [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo). \* The [Grand Harbour](/wiki/Grand\_Harbour), located at the eastern side of the capital city of [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta). The Grand Harbour, which has been used as a harbour since [Roman](/wiki/Roman\_Empire) times, has several extensive [docks](/wiki/Dock\_(maritime)) and [wharves](/wiki/Wharf), as well as a cruise liner terminal. \* [Marsamxett Harbour](/wiki/Marsamxett\_Harbour), located on the western side of Valletta, accommodates a number of yacht marinas. \* [Marsaxlokk Harbour](/wiki/Marsaxlokk\_Harbour) is sited at [Marsaxlokk](/wiki/Marsaxlokk) on the southeastern side of Malta, and is the location of the [Malta Freeport](/wiki/Malta\_Freeport), the islands' main cargo terminal. ### Ferry services [(/w/index.php?title=Transport\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=8)] Main articles: [Gozo Channel Line](/wiki/Gozo\_Channel\_Line), [Virtu Ferries](/wiki/Virtu\_Ferries), and [Gozo Highspeed](/w/index.php?title=Gozo\_Highspeed&action=edit&redlink=1) A frequent daily passenger and car ferry service runs between the islands of Malta and Gozo between [Ċirkewwa Harbour](/wiki/%C4%8Airkewwa) and [Mġarr Harbour](/wiki/M%C4%A1arr\_(Gozo)).

There are also regularly scheduled local commuter ferries, including: \* From [Marsamxett](/wiki/Marsamxett\_Harbour) (Valletta) to [Sliema](/wiki/Sliema), \* From Ta' Liesse (Valletta) to [Cottonera](/wiki/Three\_Cities\_(Malta)), \* From Ta' Liesse (Valletta) to [Mġarr, Gozo](/wiki/M%C4%A1arr,\_Gozo) (Gozo Highspeed, formerly "Gozo Fast Ferry"). More ferries are planned to be introduced starting in Summer 2025.[[31]](#cite\_note-31)[[32]](#cite\_note-32) There is also a ferry terminal at the Grand Harbour that connects Malta to [Pozzallo](/wiki/Pozzallo) and [Catania](/wiki/Catania) in [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily). ### Merchant marine [(/w/index.php?title=Transport\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=9)] Malta is one of the ten largest ship registers in the world (6th in 2016) and the largest in the [EU](/wiki/EU), with 66.2 million [gt](/wiki/Gross\_tonnage) registered. At the end of 2015, there were also 501 yachts on the register.[[33]](#cite\_note-33) ## Air transport [(/w/index.php?title=Transport\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=10)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/9H-AEP\_A320\_Air\_Malta\_%2814806272801%29.jpg/250px-9H-AEP\_A320\_Air\_Malta\_%2814806272801%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:9H-AEP\_A320\_Air\_Malta\_(14806272801).jpg) An [Air Malta](/wiki/Air\_Malta) Airbus A320 [Malta International Airport](/wiki/Malta\_International\_Airport) is the only airport serving the Maltese Islands. It is built on the land formerly occupied by the [RAF Luqa](/wiki/RAF\_Luqa) air base. A heliport is also located there, but the scheduled service to Gozo ceased in 2006. From June 2007 to August 2012, a three-times daily floatplane service, operated by [HarbourAir Malta](/w/index.php?title=HarbourAir\_Malta&action=edit&redlink=1), linked the sea terminal in Grand Harbour to [Mgarr harbour](/wiki/M%C4%A1arr\_(Gozo)) in Gozo.[[34]](#cite\_note-34) In the past there were two further airfields which were in operation during [World War II](/wiki/World\_War\_II) and into the 1960s, located at [Ta'Qali](/wiki/Ta%27Qali) and [Ħal Far](/wiki/%C4%A6al\_Far).

They have now since been closed, the land on the former has now been converted into a national park, [stadium](/wiki/Ta%27Qali\_Stadium) and the Crafts Village visitor attraction. The [Malta Aviation Museum](/wiki/Malta\_Aviation\_Museum) is also situated here, preserving several aircraft including Hurricane and Spitfire fighters which defended the island in World War II. Ħal Far has been converted into an industrial estate, a race track and an immigration reception centre. The national airline is [KM Malta Airlines](/wiki/KM\_Malta\_Airlines), which replaced [Air Malta](/wiki/Air\_Malta) in March 2024.[[35]](#cite\_note-35) ## See also [(/w/index.php?title=Transport\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=11)] \* [Buses in Malta](/wiki/Buses\_in\_Malta) \* [Plug-in electric vehicles in Malta](/wiki/Plug-in\_electric\_vehicles\_in\_Malta) ## References [(/w/index.php?title=Transport\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=12)] 1. ^ [Jump up to: \*\*\*a\*\*\*](#cite\_ref-European\_Commission\_1-0)[\*\*\*b\*\*\*](#cite\_ref-European\_Commission\_1-1)["Malta introduces free public transport"](https://urban-mobility-observatory.transport.ec.europa.eu/news-events/news/malta-introduces-free-public-transport-2022-10-13\_en?prefLang=nl). \*European Commission\*. 13 October 2022. [Coordinates](/wiki/Geographic\_coordinate\_system): ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/WMA\_button2b.png/20px-WMA\_button2b.png)[35°53′N14°27′E﻿ / ﻿35.883°N 14.450°E﻿ / 35.883; 14.450](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Malta\_(island)&params=35\_53\_N\_14\_27\_E\_region:MT\_scale:300000\_type:isle) From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Largest island of the Maltese Archipelago Malta       South of [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily), [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean\_Sea)  Coordinates   [Maltese Archipelago](/wiki/Malta#Geography)  Total islands   Filfa, St Paul's Islands, Manoel Island. Area   197 km (122.4 mi)  Highest elevation   [Ta' Dmejrek](/wiki/Ta%27\_Dmejrek)  Administration  \*\*[Malta](/wiki/Malta)\*\*  Capital city   [St. Paul's Bay](/wiki/St.\_Paul%27s\_Bay) (pop. 29,097)  Demographics  Population   1,664/km2 (4310/sq mi)  Ethnic groups  \*\*Malta\*\* is an [island](/wiki/Island) in [Southern Europe](/wiki/Southern\_Europe). It is the largest and most populous of the three major islands that constitute the [Maltese Archipelago](/wiki/Geography\_of\_Malta) and the country of [Malta](/wiki/Malta). The island is situated in the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean\_Sea) directly south of [Italy](/wiki/Italy) and north of [Libya](/wiki/Libya). It lies south-east of the smaller islands of [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo) and [Comino](/wiki/Comino). The island is 27 kilometres (17 mi) long and 14.5 kilometres (9 mi) wide, with a total area of 246 square kilometres (95 sq mi). The capital is [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta), while the largest locality is [Rabat](/wiki/Rabat,\_Malta). The island is made up of many small towns, which together form one [larger urban zone](/wiki/Larger\_urban\_zone) with a population of 409,259. The landscape is characterised by low hills with terraced fields.

## History [(/w/index.php?title=Malta\_(island)&action=edit&section=1)] Main articles: [History of Malta](/wiki/History\_of\_Malta) and [Timeline of Maltese history](/wiki/Timeline\_of\_Maltese\_history) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Malta\_-\_Qrendi\_-\_Hagar\_Qim\_and\_Mnajdra\_Archaeological\_Park\_-\_Hagar\_Qim\_08\_ies.jpg/250px-Malta\_-\_Qrendi\_-\_Hagar\_Qim\_and\_Mnajdra\_Archaeological\_Park\_-\_Hagar\_Qim\_08\_ies.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_-\_Qrendi\_-\_Hagar\_Qim\_and\_Mnajdra\_Archaeological\_Park\_-\_Hagar\_Qim\_08\_ies.jpg) [Ħaġar Qim](/wiki/%C4%A6a%C4%A1ar\_Qim) Humans have inhabited Malta since about 5200 BC, when [Stone Age](/wiki/Stone\_Age) hunters or farmers arrived from [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily). [Early Neolithic](/wiki/Early\_Neolithic) settlements were discovered in open areas and also in caves, such as [Għar Dalam](/wiki/G%C4%A7ar\_Dalam).[[1]](#cite\_note-Skeates2010-1) Around 3500 BC, a culture of [megalithic](/wiki/Megalithic) temple builders then either supplanted or arose. They built some of the oldest existing, free-standing structures in the world in the form of megalithic temples such as those at [Ħaġar Qim](/wiki/%C4%A6a%C4%A1ar\_Qim) and [Mnajdra](/wiki/Mnajdra).[[2]](#cite\_note-2) After 2500 BC, Malta was depopulated but soon became the home of [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze\_Age) settlers, who settled in sites such as [Borġ in-Nadur](/wiki/Bor%C4%A1\_in-Nadur). They built first fortifications in Malta.[[3]](#cite\_note-3) Malta was later ruled by the Phoenicians, Carthaginians, Romans, Byzantines and Arabs before it was occupied by the [County of Sicily](/wiki/County\_of\_Sicily) in 1091. The island then became part of the [Kingdom of Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom\_of\_Sicily) until it was given to the [Order of Saint John](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller) along with [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo) and [Tripoli](/wiki/Tripoli,\_Libya) (North Africa) in 1530. In 1192 [Tancred, King of Sicily](/wiki/Tancred,\_King\_of\_Sicily) created [Margaritus of Brindisi](/wiki/Margaritus\_of\_Brindisi) the first [Count of Malta](/wiki/Count\_of\_Malta). In 1565, the Order and the Maltese withstood a major [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman\_Empire) invasion which became known as the [Great Siege of Malta](/wiki/Great\_Siege\_of\_Malta).

The Order ruled Malta for over 250 years and built many great pieces of architecture, including the capital city [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta). In 1798, the French under [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon)[occupied Malta](/wiki/French\_occupation\_of\_Malta) and ruled from 1798–1800. Napoleon's stay lasted from 12–18 June when he resided at [Palazzo Parisio](/wiki/Palazzo\_Parisio\_(Valletta)) in Valletta. The French remained until the [Maltese rebelled](/wiki/Siege\_of\_Malta\_(1798%E2%80%931800)) against their rule. In 1800, the British took control over Malta and the island initially became a [British protectorate](/wiki/Malta\_Protectorate), and a [colony](/wiki/Malta\_Colony) a couple of years later. The British ruled for about 150 years and Malta became independent in 1964. Ten years later the [State of Malta](/wiki/State\_of\_Malta) became the [Republic of Malta](/wiki/Malta). Malta joined the [European Union](/wiki/European\_Union) in 2004 and adopted the [Euro](/wiki/Euro) four years later.[[4]](#cite\_note-4) ## Geography [(/w/index.php?title=Malta\_(island)&action=edit&section=2)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/View\_of\_Nature\_Reserve\_from\_St.\_Agatha%27s\_Tower.JPG/250px-View\_of\_Nature\_Reserve\_from\_St.\_Agatha%27s\_Tower.JPG)](/wiki/File:View\_of\_Nature\_Reserve\_from\_St.\_Agatha%27s\_Tower.JPG) Maltese landscape, [Għadira](/wiki/G%C4%A7adira) Main article: [Geography of Malta](/wiki/Geography\_of\_Malta) Malta is the largest island in an [archipelago](/wiki/Archipelago) in the central [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean), some 80 km (50 mi) south of the Italian island of [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily) across the [Malta Channel](/wiki/Malta\_Channel). Malta is located east of its sister islands of [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo) and [Comino](/wiki/Comino).

It lies on the Malta plateau, a shallow shelf formed from the high points of a [land bridge](/wiki/Land\_bridge) between Sicily and North Africa that became isolated as sea levels rose after the [last ice age](/wiki/Last\_Glacial\_Period).[[5]](#cite\_note-5) Malta is therefore situated in the zone between the Eurasian and African tectonic plates.[[6]](#cite\_note-6) Numerous bays along the indented coastline of the islands provide good harbours. The landscape consists of low hills with terraced fields. The highest point in Malta is [Ta' Dmejrek](/wiki/Ta%27\_Dmejrek), at 253 m (830 ft), near [Dingli](/wiki/Dingli). Although there are some small rivers at times of high rainfall, there are no permanent rivers or lakes on Malta. However, some watercourses have fresh water running all year round, such as those at [Ras ir-Raħeb](/wiki/Ras\_ir-Ra%C4%A7eb) near [Baħrija](/wiki/Ba%C4%A7rija) and at l-Imtaħleb. [Phytogeographically](/wiki/Phytogeography), Malta belongs to the Liguro-Tyrrhenian province of the [Mediterranean Region](/wiki/Mediterranean\_Region) within the [Boreal Kingdom](/wiki/Boreal\_Kingdom).

According to the [WWF](/wiki/World\_Wide\_Fund\_for\_Nature), the territory of Malta belongs to the [ecoregion](/wiki/Ecoregion) of "Mediterranean Forests, Woodlands and Scrub".[[7]](#cite\_note-7) Some minor islands off the main island of Malta include:   ---   \* Mistra Rocks ([San Pawl il-Baħar](/wiki/San\_Pawl\_il-Ba%C4%A7ar)) \* Qawra Point/Ta' Fraben Island ([San Pawl il-Baħar](/wiki/San\_Pawl\_il-Ba%C4%A7ar)) \* Sala Rock ([Żabbar](/wiki/%C5%BBabbar)) \* Xrobb l-Għaġin Rock ([Marsaxlokk](/wiki/Marsaxlokk))  Climate data for Malta ([Balzan](/wiki/Balzan) in the central part of the island) 1985–  ---  Month  Feb  Apr  Jun  Aug  Oct  Dec   16.1(61.0)  17.8(64.0)  24.2(75.6)  33.72(92.70)  28.4(83.1)  21.0(69.8)  23.16(73.69)  Daily mean °C (°F)  16(61)  19(66)  27(81)  33.4(92.1)  25(77)  14.7(58.5)   10.3(50.5)  11.3(52.3)  16.6(61.9)  22.8(73.0)  21.6(70.9)  15.0(59.0)  16.26(61.27)  Average [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation) mm (inches)  63.4(2.50)  26.3(1.04)  5.4(0.21)  6.0(0.24)  77.2(3.04)  107.7(4.24)   15  9  3  0  5  13  90  Mean monthly [sunshine hours](/wiki/Sunshine\_duration)  178.1  253.8  336.9  352.2  223.8  161.2   ### Urbanization [(/w/index.php?title=Malta\_(island)&action=edit&section=4)] According to [Eurostat](/wiki/Eurostat), Malta Island is a single [Larger Urban Zone](/wiki/Larger\_Urban\_Zone) nominally referred to as "Valletta".

According to [Demographia](/wiki/Demographia), the whole country is identified as urban area.[[14]](#cite\_note-14) According to [European Spatial Planning Observation Network](/wiki/European\_Spatial\_Planning\_Observation\_Network), Malta is identified as Functional Urban Area (FUA).[[15]](#cite\_note-15) According to United Nations, about 95% of Malta is urban area and the number grows every year.[[16]](#cite\_note-16) Also, according to the results of ESPON and EU Commission studies, "\*the whole territory of Malta constitutes a single urban region\*".[[17]](#cite\_note-17) ## Economy [(/w/index.php?title=Malta\_(island)&action=edit&section=5)] Main article: [Economy of Malta](/wiki/Economy\_of\_Malta) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/AIDAcara\_Valetta.jpg/250px-AIDAcara\_Valetta.jpg)](/wiki/File:AIDAcara\_Valetta.jpg) Valletta's maritime industrial zone [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Malta\_-\_Birzebbuga\_-\_Triq\_il-Qajjenza\_%2B\_San\_Lucian\_Oil\_Company\_01\_ies.jpg/250px-Malta\_-\_Birzebbuga\_-\_Triq\_il-Qajjenza\_%2B\_San\_Lucian\_Oil\_Company\_01\_ies.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_-\_Birzebbuga\_-\_Triq\_il-Qajjenza\_%2B\_San\_Lucian\_Oil\_Company\_01\_ies.jpg) Oil tanks in [Birżebbuġa](/wiki/Bir%C5%BCebbu%C4%A1a) The main island of Malta contributes significantly to the country's overall economy, which itself is classified as an [advanced economy](/wiki/Advanced\_economy) according to the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International\_Monetary\_Fund) (IMF).[[18]](#cite\_note-18) Until 1800 Malta depended on cotton, tobacco and its shipyards for exports. Once under British control, they came to depend on [Malta Dockyard](/wiki/Malta\_Dockyard) for support of the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal\_Navy), especially during the [Crimean War](/wiki/Crimean\_War) of 1854. Malta's economy was boosted by the opening of the [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez\_Canal) in 1869, as there was a massive increase of civilian shipping which entered the port. Ships stopping at Malta's docks for refuelling helped the [Entrepôt](/wiki/Entrep%C3%B4t) trade, which brought additional benefits to the island. Towards the end of the 19th century the economy began declining, and by the 1940s Malta's economy was in serious crisis.

One factor was the longer range of newer merchant ships that required less frequent refuelling stops. The economy was boosted again after World War II when the island needed to be rebuilt. [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Malta\_-\_Siggiewi\_-\_Triq\_Panoramika\_-\_quarries\_01\_ies.jpg/250px-Malta\_-\_Siggiewi\_-\_Triq\_Panoramika\_-\_quarries\_01\_ies.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_-\_Siggiewi\_-\_Triq\_Panoramika\_-\_quarries\_01\_ies.jpg) Limestone quarries in [Siġġiewi](/wiki/Si%C4%A1%C4%A1iewi) Currently, Malta's major resources are [limestone](/wiki/Limestone), a favourable geographic location and a productive labour force. Malta produces only about 20% of its food needs, has limited freshwater supplies because of the drought in the summer and has no domestic energy sources, aside from the potential for [solar energy](/wiki/Solar\_energy) from its plentiful sunlight.[[19]](#cite\_note-19) The economy is dependent on foreign trade (serving as a freight trans-shipment point), manufacturing (especially electronics and textiles) and tourism. [Film production](/wiki/Films\_shot\_in\_Malta) is a growing contributor to the Maltese economy. Despite its size, Malta continues to attract international film productions from around the world and this is the best sign of confidence that a small country can get from foreign producers. The first film shot in Malta was \*Sons of the Sea\* in 1925. Since then, over 100 other films were partially or fully shot in Malta, including \*Malta Story\* (1953), \*[Midnight Express](/wiki/Midnight\_Express\_(film))\* (1978), \*[Popeye](/wiki/Popeye\_(film))\* (1980), \*[Gladiator](/wiki/Gladiator\_(2000\_film))\* (2000), \*[Munich](/wiki/Munich\_(2005\_film))\* (2005), \*[World War Z](/wiki/World\_War\_Z\_(film))\* (2013) and \*[Captain Phillips](/wiki/Captain\_Phillips\_(film))\* (2013).

In preparation for Malta's membership in the [European Union](/wiki/European\_Union), which it joined on 1 May 2004, it [privatised](/wiki/Privatization) some state-controlled firms and liberalised markets. For example, the government announced on 8 January 2007 that it was selling its 40% stake in [MaltaPost](/wiki/MaltaPost), to complete a privatisation process which had been ongoing for the previous five years. By 2010, Malta had privatised telecommunications, postal services, shipyards and shipbuilding. ### Banking and finance [(/w/index.php?title=Malta\_(island)&action=edit&section=6)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Malta\_-\_St.\_Julian%27s\_%28Triq\_it-Torri\_%28Sliema%29%29\_03\_ies.jpg/250px-Malta\_-\_St.\_Julian%27s\_%28Triq\_it-Torri\_%28Sliema%29%29\_03\_ies.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_-\_St.\_Julian%27s\_(Triq\_it-Torri\_(Sliema))\_03\_ies.jpg) [Portomaso Business Tower](/wiki/Portomaso\_Business\_Tower), the second highest building on Malta. The two largest commercial banks are [Bank of Valletta](/wiki/Bank\_of\_Valletta) and [HSBC Bank Malta](/wiki/HSBC\_Bank\_Malta), both of which can trace their origins back to the 19th century. The [Central Bank of Malta](/wiki/Central\_Bank\_of\_Malta) (Bank Ċentrali ta' Malta) has two key areas of responsibility: the formulation and implementation of monetary policy and the promotion of a sound and efficient financial system. It was established by the Central Bank of Malta Act on 17 April 1968.

The Maltese government entered [ERM II](/wiki/ERM\_II) on 4 May 2005, and adopted the euro as the country's currency on 1 January 2008.[[20]](#cite\_note-20) [FinanceMalta](http://www.financemalta.org/) is the quasi-governmental organisation tasked with marketing and educating business leaders in coming to Malta and runs seminars and events around the world highlighting the emerging strength of Malta as a jurisdiction for banking and finance and insurance.[[21]](#cite\_note-21) ### Transport [(/w/index.php?title=Malta\_(island)&action=edit&section=7)] Main article: [Transport in Malta](/wiki/Transport\_in\_Malta) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Malta\_Buses\_%28cropped\_2%29.jpg/250px-Malta\_Buses\_%28cropped\_2%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_Buses\_(cropped\_2).jpg) Maltese buses operated by the Malta Public Transport[[22]](#cite\_note-22) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Sundown\_in\_the\_harbour\_%289963498255%29.jpg/250px-Sundown\_in\_the\_harbour\_%289963498255%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sundown\_in\_the\_harbour\_(9963498255).jpg) [Grand Harbour](/wiki/Grand\_Harbour) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/047.Freeport.jpg/250px-047.Freeport.jpg)](/wiki/File:047.Freeport.jpg) [Malta Freeport](/wiki/Malta\_Freeport) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Malta\_International\_Airport3.jpg/250px-Malta\_International\_Airport3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_International\_Airport3.jpg) [Malta International Airport](/wiki/Malta\_International\_Airport) Traffic in Malta [drives on the left](/wiki/Driving\_on\_the\_left\_or\_right). Car ownership in Malta is exceedingly high, given the very small size of the island. The main roads of Malta from the southernmost point to the northernmost point are these: Triq Birżebbuġa in [Birżebbuġa](/wiki/Bir%C5%BCebbu%C4%A1a), [Għar Dalam](/wiki/G%C4%A7ar\_Dalam) Road and [Tal-Barrani](/wiki/Tal-Barrani) Road in [Żejtun](/wiki/%C5%BBejtun), Santa Luċija Avenue in [Paola](/wiki/Paola,\_Malta), Aldo Moro Street (Trunk Road), 13 December Street and [Ħamrun](/wiki/%C4%A6amrun)-Marsa Bypass in [Marsa](/wiki/Marsa,\_Malta), Regional Road in [Santa Venera](/wiki/Santa\_Venera)/[Msida](/wiki/Msida)/[Gżira](/wiki/G%C5%BCira)/[San Ġwann](/wiki/San\_%C4%A0wann), St Andrew's Road in [Swieqi](/wiki/Swieqi)/[Pembroke, Malta](/wiki/Pembroke,\_Malta), Coast Road in [Baħar iċ-Ċagħaq](/wiki/Ba%C4%A7ar\_i%C4%8B-%C4%8Aag%C4%A7aq), Salina Road, Kennedy Drive, St. Paul's Bypass and [Xemxija](/wiki/Xemxija) Hill in [San Pawl il-Baħar](/wiki/San\_Pawl\_il-Ba%C4%A7ar), Mistra Hill, Wettinger Street (Mellieħa Bypass) and Marfa Road in [Mellieħa](/wiki/Mellie%C4%A7a). [Buses](/wiki/Malta\_bus) (\*xarabank\* or \*karozza tal-linja\*) are the primary method of public transport.

Established in 1905, the service underwent an extensive reform in July 2011. The management structure changed from having self-employed drivers driving their own vehicles to a service being offered by a single company through a public tender. The public tender was won by Arriva Malta, a member of the [Arriva](/wiki/Arriva) group, but the company ceased operations on 1 January 2014 having been nationalised as \*Malta Public Transport\* by the Maltese government, with a new bus operator to take over their operations in the near future.[[23]](#cite\_note-23)[[24]](#cite\_note-24) Malta has three large natural harbours: \* The [Grand Harbour](/wiki/Grand\_Harbour) (or Port il-Kbir), located at the eastern side of the capital city of [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta), has been a harbour since [Roman](/wiki/Roman\_Empire) times. It has several extensive [docks](/wiki/Dock\_(maritime)) and [wharves](/wiki/Wharf), as well as a cruise liner terminal. A terminal at the Grand Harbour serves [ferries](/wiki/Ferries) that connect Malta to [Pozzallo](/wiki/Pozzallo) & [Catania](/wiki/Catania) in Sicily. \* [Marsamxett Harbour](/wiki/Marsamxett\_Harbour), located on the western side of Valletta, accommodates a number of yacht marinas. \* [Marsaxlokk Harbour](/wiki/Marsaxlokk\_Harbour) (Malta Freeport), at [Birżebbuġa](/wiki/Bir%C5%BCebbu%C4%A1a) on the south-eastern side of Malta, is the islands' main cargo terminal. Malta Freeport is the [11th busiest container port in continent of Europe and 46th in the World](/wiki/List\_of\_world%27s\_busiest\_container\_ports) with a trade volume of 2.3 million [TEU's](/wiki/Twenty-foot\_equivalent\_unit) in 2008.[[25]](#cite\_note-25) There is also a man-made harbour at [Ċirkewwa](/wiki/%C4%8Airkewwa).

This is connected with [Mġarr Harbour](/wiki/M%C4%A1arr\_(Gozo)) on [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo) by the [Gozo ferry](/wiki/Gozo\_Channel\_Line) which makes numerous trips each day. The only airport on Malta is [Malta International Airport](/wiki/Malta\_International\_Airport). It is built on the land formerly occupied by the [RAF Luqa](/wiki/RAF\_Luqa) air base. Two further airfields at [Ta' Qali](/wiki/Ta%27\_Qali) and [Ħal Far](/wiki/%C4%A6al\_Far) were operated during World War II and into the 1960s but are now closed. Today, [Ta' Qali](/wiki/Ta%27\_Qali) houses a national park, [stadium](/wiki/Ta%27\_Qali\_Stadium), the Crafts Village visitor attraction and the [Malta Aviation Museum](/wiki/Malta\_Aviation\_Museum). This museum preserves several aircraft, including [Hurricane](/wiki/Hawker\_Hurricane) and [Spitfire](/wiki/Spitfire) fighters that defended the island in World War II. The national airline is [Air Malta](/wiki/Air\_Malta). [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Mediterraneo\_dolphin\_show\_1.JPG/250px-Mediterraneo\_dolphin\_show\_1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mediterraneo\_dolphin\_show\_1.JPG) The dolphin show at Mediterraneo Marine Park ### Tourism [(/w/index.php?title=Malta\_(island)&action=edit&section=8)] Malta is a popular tourist destination, with 1.2 million tourists per year.[[26]](#cite\_note-Malta\_in\_Figures-26) Three times more tourists visit than there are residents. Tourism infrastructure has increased dramatically over the years and a number of good-quality hotels are present on the island, although overdevelopment and the destruction of traditional housing is of growing concern. An increasing number of Maltese now travel abroad on holiday.[[27]](#cite\_note-27) In recent years, Malta has advertised itself as a [medical tourism](/wiki/Medical\_tourism) destination,[[28]](#cite\_note-28) and a number of [health tourism providers](/wiki/Health\_tourism\_provider) are developing the industry. However, no Maltese hospital has undergone independent [international healthcare accreditation](/wiki/International\_healthcare\_accreditation).

Malta is popular with British medical tourists,[[29]](#cite\_note-29) pointing Maltese hospitals towards seeking UK-sourced accreditation, such as with the [Trent Accreditation Scheme](/wiki/Trent\_Accreditation\_Scheme). Dual accreditation with the American-oriented [Joint Commission](/wiki/Joint\_Commission) is necessary if hospitals in Malta wish to compete with the Far East and Latin America for medical tourists from the United States. ## Demographics [(/w/index.php?title=Malta\_(island)&action=edit&section=9)] Main article: [Demographics of Malta](/wiki/Demographics\_of\_Malta) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Panorama\_of\_Valletta.jpg/250px-Panorama\_of\_Valletta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Panorama\_of\_Valletta.jpg) [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta), Malta's capital city Native [Maltese people](/wiki/Maltese\_people) make up the majority of the island. However, there are minorities, the largest of which are [British](/wiki/British\_people), many of whom have retired to Malta. According to the government's estimate of 2013, the population of Malta stood at 409,259 people which is about 91.6% of the country's total population. The largest city is [Birkirkara](/wiki/Birkirkara) with a population of 22,319. The capital [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta) has a relatively small population of 6675 people.[[30]](#cite\_note-30) The main language spoken on Malta is the [Maltese language](/wiki/Maltese\_language), a [Semitic language](/wiki/Semitic\_language) descended from the now defunct [Siculo-Arabic](/wiki/Siculo-Arabic) dialect of [southern Italy](/wiki/Southern\_Italy).[[31]](#cite\_note-MED-31) The language has substantial borrowing from [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian\_language), Italian, a little French, and more recently and increasingly, English.[[32]](#cite\_note-32) The hybrid character of Maltese was established by a long period of Maltese-Sicilian urban bilingualism gradually transforming rural speech and which ended in the early 19th century with Maltese emerging as the vernacular of the entire native population.

The language includes different dialects that can vary greatly from one town to another or from one island to another. The [Eurobarometer](/wiki/Eurobarometer) states that 100% of the population speak Maltese. Also, 88% of the population speak English, 66% speak Italian, and 17% speak French.[[33]](#cite\_note-33) This widespread knowledge of [second languages](/wiki/Second\_language) makes Malta one of the most multilingual countries in the [European Union](/wiki/European\_Union). A study collecting public opinion on what language was "preferred" discovered that 86% of the population express a preference for Maltese, 12% for English, and 2% for Italian.[[34]](#cite\_note-LinguisticView-34) Still, Italian television channels from Italy-based broadcasters, such as [Mediaset](/wiki/Mediaset) and [RAI](/wiki/RAI), reach Malta and remain popular.[[34]](#cite\_note-LinguisticView-34)[[35]](#cite\_note-35)[[36]](#cite\_note-36) ### Administrative divisions [(/w/index.php?title=Malta\_(island)&action=edit&section=10)] Main article: [Local councils of Malta](/wiki/Local\_councils\_of\_Malta) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Malta\_location\_map.svg/250px-Malta\_location\_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Malta\_location\_map.svg) [Administrative divisions](/wiki/Local\_councils\_of\_Malta) of Malta. Malta has had a system of local government since 1993, based on the [European Charter of Local Self-Government](/wiki/European\_Charter\_of\_Local\_Self-Government). The main island is divided into [four regions](/wiki/Regions\_of\_Malta), each having its own Regional Committee:[[37]](#cite\_note-37) \* [Central Region](/wiki/Central\_Region,\_Malta) \* [Northern Region](/wiki/Northern\_Region,\_Malta) \* [South Eastern Region](/wiki/South\_Eastern\_Region) \* [Southern Region](/wiki/Southern\_Region,\_Malta) Each region is further divided into [local councils](/wiki/Local\_councils\_of\_Malta). Some hamlets have [administrative committees](/wiki/Local\_councils\_of\_Malta#List\_of\_Maltese\_local\_communities\_councils), which are part of larger local councils. Currently there are 54 local councils and 13 administrative committees in Malta.

The island is also divided into [five districts](/wiki/Districts\_of\_Malta), but these serve primarily for statistical purposes: \* [Northern District](/wiki/Districts\_of\_Malta#Northern\_District) \* [Northern Harbour District](/wiki/Districts\_of\_Malta#Northern\_Harbour\_District) \* [South Eastern District](/wiki/Districts\_of\_Malta#South\_Eastern\_District) \* [Southern Harbour District](/wiki/Districts\_of\_Malta#Southern\_Harbour\_District) \* [Western District](/wiki/Districts\_of\_Malta#Western\_District) ## See also [(/w/index.php?title=Malta\_(island)&action=edit&section=11)] \* ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Flag\_of\_Malta.svg/40px-Flag\_of\_Malta.svg.png)[Malta portal](/wiki/Portal:Malta) \* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Ic%C3%B4ne\_Ile.svg/40px-Ic%C3%B4ne\_Ile.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ic%C3%B4ne\_Ile.svg)[Islands portal](/wiki/Portal:Islands) \* [Malta](/wiki/Malta) (country) \* [Geography of Malta](/wiki/Geography\_of\_Malta) \* [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo) \* [Comino](/wiki/Comino) \* [Islands Cleanup campaign in Malta](/wiki/Islands\_Cleanup\_campaign) ## References [(/w/index.php?title=Malta\_(island)&action=edit&section=12)] 1. \*\*[^](#cite\_ref-Skeates2010\_1-0)\*\*Robin Skeates (2010). Census population and average annual growth rate  Pop. ---   114,499   123,496   134,055   141,775   149,782   165,037   184,742   211,564   212,258   241,621   305,991   319,620   314,216   345,418   378,132   404,962   417,432   519,562   ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/timeline/6hd31fqw4p93til542ljmm7ekiirzis.png) ## Characteristics [(/w/index.php?title=Demographics\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=1)] [Malta](/wiki/Malta) is the most densely populated country in the EU and one of the most densely populated countries in the world, with about 1,265 inhabitants per square kilometre (3,280 inhabitants/mi2). This compares with about 32 per square kilometre (85 per square mile) for the United States[\*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual\_of\_Style/Dates\_and\_numbers#Chronological\_items)\*]. Inhabited since prehistoric times, Malta was first colonized by [Sicilians](/wiki/Sicily). Subsequently, [Phoenicians](/wiki/Phoenicia), [Romans](/wiki/Ancient\_Rome), [Byzantines](/wiki/Byzantine), [Arabs](/wiki/Arab) in 870 AD[[3]](#cite\_note-3) who may have completely depopulated the islands[[4]](#cite\_note-4) but in 1224 were themselves expelled from Malta,[[5]](#cite\_note-5)[Normans](/wiki/Normans), [Sicilians](/wiki/Sicilians), Spanish, French and the British have influenced Maltese life and culture to varying degrees.

Roman Catholicism is established by law as the religion of Malta with 98%; however, full liberty of conscience and freedom of worship is guaranteed, and a number of faiths have places of worship on the island (rather small groups, a combined total of 2% of the people are [Protestants](/wiki/Protestant), [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern\_Orthodox\_Church), [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims) and [Jews](/wiki/Jews)). Malta has two official languages--[Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_language) (a Semitic language derived from [Siculo-Arabic](/wiki/Siculo-Arabic) and heavily influenced by [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian\_language) and Italian), and English, which includes a local dialect, [Maltese English](/wiki/Maltese\_English). Both languages are compulsory subjects in Maltese primary and secondary schools. A large portion of the population is also fluent in Italian, which was, until 1936, the national language of Malta. The literacy rate has reached 93%, compared to 63% in 1946. Schooling is compulsory until age 16. ### Age distribution [(/w/index.php?title=Demographics\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=2)] Since 2000, the shift in the age composition towards an older population continued to materialise. In fact, the average age of the Maltese population increased from 38.5 in 2005 to 40.5 in 2011. This resulted from the increase in the number of persons aged 55 and over, together with a decrease in the number of persons under 25 years of age. The average in Gozo and Comino (41.6 years) was higher than that observed for Malta.

Persons aged 65 and over more represent 16.3% of the total population in 2011, compared to 13.7% in 2005. In contrast, persons aged 14 and under make up 14.8% of the population in 2011, compared to 17.2% in 2005.[[6]](#cite\_note-6) ### Migrant background and foreign nationality [(/w/index.php?title=Demographics\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=3)] \*\*Foreign nationals in Malta\*\* As of 2016 and 2017, the numbers of selected groups of resident foreign nationals (non-naturalized residents) in Malta were as follows: This list does not include foreign nationals who acquired Maltese nationality and foreign nationals without resident status.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Malta\_-\_Valletta\_-\_Triq\_il-Mediterran-Triq\_it-Tramuntana\_-\_Mediterranean\_Conference\_Centre\_01\_ies.jpg/250px-Malta\_-\_Valletta\_-\_Triq\_il-Mediterran-Triq\_it-Tramuntana\_-\_Mediterranean\_Conference\_Centre\_01\_ies.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_-\_Valletta\_-\_Triq\_il-Mediterran-Triq\_it-Tramuntana\_-\_Mediterranean\_Conference\_Centre\_01\_ies.jpg) The Sacra Infermeria was used as a hospital from the 16th to 20th centuries. It is now the [Mediterranean Conference Centre](/wiki/Mediterranean\_Conference\_Centre). [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Mater\_Dei\_01.jpg/250px-Mater\_Dei\_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mater\_Dei\_01.jpg) [Mater Dei Hospital](/wiki/Mater\_Dei\_Hospital) Malta has a long history of providing [publicly funded health care](/wiki/Publicly\_funded\_health\_care).

The first hospital recorded in the country was already functioning by 1372.[[1]](#cite\_note-1) Today, Malta has both a public healthcare system, known as the government healthcare service, where healthcare is free at the point of delivery, and a private healthcare system.[[2]](#cite\_note-2)[[3]](#cite\_note-3) Malta has a strong general practitioner-delivered primary care base and the public hospitals provide secondary and tertiary care. The Maltese Ministry of Health advises foreign residents to take out private medical insurance.[[4]](#cite\_note-4) ## Comparisons [(/w/index.php?title=Healthcare\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=1)] In 2000, Malta was ranked number five in the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World\_Health\_Organization)'s ranking of the world's health systems,[[5]](#cite\_note-5) compared to the [United States](/wiki/Health\_care\_in\_the\_United\_States) (at 37), [Australia](/wiki/Health\_care\_in\_Australia) (at 32), [United Kingdom](/wiki/Health\_care\_in\_the\_United\_Kingdom) (at 18) and [Canada](/wiki/Health\_care\_in\_Canada) (at 30). The healthcare system in Malta closely resembles the British system,[[6]](#cite\_note-ph-6) as healthcare is free at the point of delivery. It moved up four places in the [Euro health consumer index](/wiki/Euro\_health\_consumer\_index) from position 27 in 2014 to 23 in 2015. It was said to have decent accessibility to healthcare, but indifferent treatment results.[[7]](#cite\_note-7) ## Facilities [(/w/index.php?title=Healthcare\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=2)] The [Mater Dei Hospital](/wiki/Mater\_Dei\_Hospital), Malta's primary hospital, opened in 2007. It has one of the largest medical buildings in Europe. The [University of Malta](/wiki/University\_of\_Malta) has a [medical school](/wiki/Medical\_school) and a Faculty of [Health Sciences](/wiki/Health\_Sciences), the latter offering diploma, degree (BSc) and postgraduate degree courses in a number of health care disciplines.

Malta also has voluntary organisations such as the Emergency Fire & Rescue Unit (E.F.R.U.), St John Ambulance and Red Cross Malta who provide first aid services during events involving crowds. The [Medical Association of Malta](/wiki/Medical\_Association\_of\_Malta) represents practitioners of the medical profession. The [Malta Medical Students' Association](/wiki/Malta\_Medical\_Students%27\_Association) (MMSA) is a separate body representing Maltese medical students, and is a member of [EMSA](/wiki/European\_Medical\_Students%27\_Association) and [IFMSA](/wiki/International\_Federation\_of\_Medical\_Students%27\_Associations). MIME, the Maltese Institute for Medical Education, is an institute set up recently to provide CME to physicians in Malta as well as medical students. The [Foundation Program](/wiki/Foundation\_Programme\_(United\_Kingdom\_medical\_education)) followed in the UK has been introduced in Malta to stem the 'brain drain' of newly graduated physicians to the British Isles. The Malta Association of Dental Students (MADS) is a student association set up to promote the rights of Dental Surgery Students studying within the faculty of Dental Surgery of the University of Malta. It is affiliated with [IADS](/wiki/IADS), the International Association of Dental Students. A 30-year concession on healthcare services in the Gozo, Karin Grech and St Lukes hospitals was granted to [Vitals Global Healthcare](/wiki/Vitals\_Global\_Healthcare) in 2015.

The [Medical Association of Malta](/wiki/Medical\_Association\_of\_Malta) objected because of "the complete lack of experience of the operator, and the dangers of government relinquishing its control of management and the resulting profit motive.”[[8]](#cite\_note-8) In January 2018 it was transferred to [Steward Global Healthcare](/wiki/Steward\_Global\_Healthcare) and precipitated a doctors' strike.[[9]](#cite\_note-9) ## See also [(/w/index.php?title=Healthcare\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=3)] \* [List of hospitals in Malta](/wiki/List\_of\_hospitals\_in\_Malta) \* [Health in Malta](/wiki/Health\_in\_Malta) \* [Abortion in Malta](/wiki/Abortion\_in\_Malta) ## References [(/w/index.php?title=Healthcare\_in\_Malta&action=edit&section=4)] 1. \*\*[^](#cite\_ref-1)\*\*["Civil Hospitals in Malta in the Last Two Hundred Years"](https://web.archive.org/web/20091027121422/http://geocities.com/hotsprings/2615/medhist/hospital2.htm). BrincatBrincatBrincat   ---   \*[Ethnologue](/wiki/Ethnologue)\* reports a total of 530,000 Maltese speakers: 450,000 in Malta and 79,000 in the [diaspora](/wiki/Emigration\_from\_Malta). Most speakers also use English,[[1]](#cite\_note-e27-1) usually the local dialect known as [Maltese English](/wiki/Maltese\_English). The largest diaspora community of Maltese speakers is [in Australia](/wiki/Maltese\_Australians), with 36,000 speakers reported in 2006 (down from 45,000 in 1996, and expected to decline further).[[24]](#cite\_note-24) The Maltese linguistic community in [Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisia) originated in the 18th century.

Numbering several thousand in the 19th century, it was reported to be only 100 to 200 people as of 2017.[[25]](#cite\_note-25) ## Classification [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=3)] Maltese is descended from Siculo-Arabic, a Semitic language within the [Afroasiatic family](/wiki/Afroasiatic\_languages).[[26]](#cite\_note-26) In the course of its [history](/wiki/History\_of\_Malta), Maltese has been influenced by Sicilian, Italian, to a lesser extent by [Norman](/wiki/Norman\_language), and, more recently, English. Today, the core vocabulary (including both the most commonly used vocabulary and [function words](/wiki/Function\_word)) is Semitic, with a large number of [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword).[[11]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEBrincat2005-11) Due to the Sicilian influence on Siculo-Arabic, Maltese has many language contact features and is most commonly described as a language with a large number of loanwords.[[27]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEBorg1997-27) Maltese has historically been classified in various ways, with some arguing that it was derived from ancient [Punic](/wiki/Punic\_language) (another Semitic language) instead of Siculo-Arabic,[[20]](#cite\_note-Academy-20)[[28]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEVella2004263-28)[[29]](#cite\_note-29) and others arguing it is one of the [Berber languages](/wiki/Berber\_languages) (another language family within Afroasiatic).[[20]](#cite\_note-Academy-20) Implausibly, [Fascist Italy](/wiki/Fascist\_Italy\_(1922%E2%80%931943)) classified it as [regional Italian](/wiki/Regional\_Italian).[[30]](#cite\_note-30) ## Dialects [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=4)] Main articles: [Żejtun dialect](/wiki/%C5%BBejtun\_dialect), [Qormi dialect](/wiki/Qormi\_dialect), [Cottonera dialect](/wiki/Cottonera\_dialect), and [Maltralian](/wiki/Maltralian) Urban varieties of Maltese are closer to Standard Maltese than rural varieties,[[31]](#cite\_note-isser-31) which have some characteristics that distinguish them from Standard Maltese.

They tend to show some archaic features[[31]](#cite\_note-isser-31) such as the realisation of ⟨kh⟩ and ⟨gh⟩ and the [imāla](/wiki/Im%C4%81la) of Arabic ā into ē (or ī especially in Gozo), considered archaic because they are reminiscent of 15th-century transcriptions of this sound.[[31]](#cite\_note-isser-31) Another archaic feature is the realisation of Standard Maltese ā as ō in rural dialects.[[31]](#cite\_note-isser-31) There is also a tendency to diphthongise simple vowels, e.g., ū becomes eo or eu.[[31]](#cite\_note-isser-31) Rural dialects also tend to employ more Semitic roots and [broken plurals](/wiki/Broken\_plurals) than Standard Maltese.[[31]](#cite\_note-isser-31) In general, rural Maltese is less distant from its Siculo-Arabic ancestor than is Standard Maltese.[[31]](#cite\_note-isser-31) ## Phonology [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=5)] ### Consonants [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=6)] Consonant phonemes[[32]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEHume1996165-32)[[33]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEBorg1997248-33)  [Dental](/wiki/Dental\_consonant)/[Alveolar](/wiki/Alveolar\_consonant)  [Velar](/wiki/Velar\_consonant)  [Glottal](/wiki/Glottal\_consonant)  ---  ---  ---   [m](/wiki/Voiced\_bilabial\_nasal)   [p](/wiki/Voiceless\_bilabial\_plosive)  [t](/wiki/Voiceless\_dental\_plosive)  [k](/wiki/Voiceless\_velar\_plosive)  [ʔ](/wiki/Glottal\_stop)  [Affricate](/wiki/Affricate\_consonant)  [d͡z](/wiki/Voiced\_alveolar\_affricate)  [d͡ʒ](/wiki/Voiced\_postalveolar\_affricate)  [Fricative](/wiki/Fricative\_consonant)  [v](/wiki/Voiced\_labiodental\_fricative)  [z](/wiki/Voiced\_alveolar\_fricative)  [ʒ](/wiki/Voiced\_postalveolar\_fricative)   [r](/wiki/Voiced\_alveolar\_trill)  [Approximant](/wiki/Approximant\_consonant)  [j](/wiki/Voiced\_palatal\_approximant)  [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Silta\_minn\_Vassalli.jpg/250px-Silta\_minn\_Vassalli.jpg)](/wiki/File:Silta\_minn\_Vassalli.jpg) [Vassalli](/wiki/Mikiel\_Anton\_Vassalli)'s \*Storja tas-Sultân Ċiru\* (1831), is an example of Maltese orthography in the 19th century, before the later standardisation introduced in 1924. Note the similarities with the various varieties of romanised Arabic. Voiceless stops are only lightly aspirated and voiced stops are fully voiced. Voicing is carried over from the last segment in [obstruent](/wiki/Obstruent)[clusters](/wiki/Consonant\_cluster); thus, two- and three-obstruent clusters are either voiceless or voiced throughout, e.g. /niktbu/ is realised [ˈniɡdbu] "we write" (similar assimilation phenomena occur in languages like French or Czech).

Maltese has [final-obstruent devoicing](/wiki/Final-obstruent\_devoicing) of voiced obstruents and word-final voiceless stops have [no audible release](/wiki/No\_audible\_release), making voiceless–voiced pairs phonetically indistinguishable in word-final position.[[34]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEBorg1997249–250-34) [Gemination](/wiki/Gemination) is distinctive word-medially and word-finally in Maltese. The distinction is most rigid [intervocalically](/wiki/Intervocalic\_consonant) after a stressed vowel. Stressed, word-final [closed syllables](/wiki/Syllable#open\_syllable) with short vowels end in a long consonant, and those with a long vowel in a single consonant; the only exception is where historic \\*ʕ and \\*ɣ meant the [compensatory lengthening](/wiki/Compensatory\_lengthening) of the succeeding vowel. Some speakers have lost length distinction in clusters.[[35]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEBorg1997251–252-35) The two nasals /m/ and /n/ assimilate for [place of articulation](/wiki/Place\_of\_articulation) in clusters.[[36]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEBorg1997255-36)/t/ and /d/ are usually [dental](/wiki/Dental\_stop), whereas /t͡sd͡zsznrl/ are all alveolar. /t͡sd͡z/ are found mostly in words of Italian origin, retaining length (if not word-initial).[[37]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEBorg1997254-37)/d͡z/ and /ʒ/ are only found in loanwords, e.g.

/ɡɐdˈd͡zɛt.tɐ/\*ga\*\*zz\*\*etta\* "newspaper" and /tɛ.lɛˈvɪ.ʒɪn/\*televi\*\*x\*\*in\* "television".[[38]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEBorg1997247-38) The pharyngeal fricative /ħ/ is velar ([[x](/wiki/Voiceless\_velar\_fricative)]), uvular ([[χ](/wiki/Voiceless\_uvular\_fricative)]), or glottal ([[h](/wiki/Voiceless\_glottal\_fricative)]) for some speakers.[[39]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEBorg1997260-39) ### Vowels [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=7)] Vowel phonemes  [Central](/wiki/Central\_vowel)   ---   long  long  long  [Close](/wiki/Close\_vowel)   [ɪ](/wiki/Near-close\_near-front\_unrounded\_vowel)  [ʊ](/wiki/Near-close\_near-back\_rounded\_vowel)   [ɛ](/wiki/Open-mid\_front\_unrounded\_vowel)  [ɔ](/wiki/Open-mid\_back\_rounded\_vowel)   [ɐ](/wiki/Near-open\_central\_vowel)   /ɐɪ//ɐʊ//ɛɪ//ɛʊ//ɪʊ//ɔɪ//ɔʊ/            ---  ---  ---  ---  ---  ---  ---  ---  ---  Classical Arabic  ت  ث  ض  ظ  ص  خ  غ  ق   t  ṯ  ḍ  ẓ  ṣ  ẖ  ġ  q   /t/  /θ/  /dˤ/  /ðˤ/  /sˤ/  /x~χ/  /ɣ~ʁ/  /q/   Letter  D d  Ħ ħ  Q q   /t/  /s/  /ʕ/  While many of these changes (chiefly the merger of [emphatic consonants](/wiki/Emphatic\_consonant) with their non-[pharyngealized](/wiki/Pharyngealization) counterparts) are the result of European influence, others (such as the merger of ق/q/ into /ʔ/) are found in other [varieties of Arabic](/wiki/Varieties\_of\_Arabic), and may be either independent developments or features of the [Sicilian Arabic](/wiki/Sicilian\_Arabic) dialect which Maltese descends from. ## Orthography [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=9)] ### Alphabet [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=10)]   ---   This section needs editing to \*\*comply with Wikipedia's [Manual of Style](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual\_of\_Style).\*\* In particular, it has problems with punctuation and text styling in the table. Please help [improve the content](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit).\*(October 2022)\* \*([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance\_template\_removal))\*  Letter  IPA (letter name)  IPA (orthographically representing)   ---  ---  ---  \*\*A a\*\*  aː  ɐ,aː,æː   be  \*baħar\*'sea'  \*\*b\*\*ar, but at the end of a word, it is devoiced to [p].

\*\*Ċ ċ\*\*  t͡ʃeː  t͡ʃ   de  \*dar\*'home'  \*\*d\*\*ay, but at the end of a word, it is devoiced to [t]. \*\*E e\*\*  eː  eː,ɛ,øː,ə   effe  \*faħam\*'coal'  \*\*f\*\*ar  \*\*Ġ ġ\*\*  d͡ʒøː  d͡ʒ   ge  \*gawwija\*'seagull'  \*\*g\*\*ame, but at the end of a word, it is devoiced to [k]. \*\*Għ għ\*\*  ajn,æːn  (ˤ)ː, ħː   akka  \*hu\*'he'   ħe  \*ħanut\*'shop'  No English equivalent; sounds similar to /h/ but is articulated with a lowered [larynx](/wiki/Larynx). \*\*I i\*\*  iː  i̞ː,iː,ɪ   ie  \*ieqaf\*'stop'  Sounds similar to y\*\*ie\*\*ld or RP n\*\*ear\*\*, or opened up slightly towards b\*\*e\*\*d or RP squ\*\*are\*\*  \*\*J j\*\*  jə,jæ,jɛ  j   ke  \*kelb\*'dog'  \*\*k\*\*ettle  \*\*L l\*\*  ɛl(ː)ᵊ  l   emme  \*mara\*'woman'  \*\*m\*\*arch  \*\*N n\*\*  ɛn(ː)ᵊ  n   o  \*ors\*'bear'  [o] as in somewhere between similar to Scottish English o in n\*\*o\*\*[ɔ] like 'aw' in [RP](/wiki/Received\_Pronunciation) l\*\*aw\*\*, but short or [ɒ] as in w\*\*a\*\*ter in some American English dialects. \*\*P p\*\*  peː,pə  p   qe  \*qattus\*'cat'  [glottal stop](/wiki/Glottal\_stop), found in the Cockney English pronunciation of "bo\*\*tt\*\*le" or the phrase "uh-oh" /ʔʌʔoʊ/. \*\*R r\*\*  ɛɹ(ː)ᵊ,æɹ(:)ᵊ,ɚ(ː)ᵊ or ɛr(ː)ᵊ,ær(:)ᵊ,ər(ː)ᵊ  r,ɹ   esse  \*[sliem](/wiki/Sliem)\*'peace'  \*\*s\*\*and  \*\*T t\*\*  teː  t   u  \*uviera\*'egg cup'  [u] as in General American English b\*\*oo\*\*t, or in some dialects, it may be realized as [ʉ] as in some American English realizations of st\*\*u\*\*dent; short \*u\* is [ʊ] p\*\*u\*\*t.

\*\*V v\*\*  vøː,veː,və  v   ve doppja /u doppja/we  \*widna\*'ear'  \*\*w\*\*est  \*\*X x\*\*  ʃə,ʃøː  ʃ/ʒ   że/żeta  \*żarbun\*'shoe'  ma\*\*z\*\*e, but at the end of a word, it is devoiced to [s]. \*\*Z z\*\*  t͡sə,t͡søː,t͡seːt(ɐ)  t͡s/d͡z  Final vowels with grave accents (à, è, ì, ò, ù) are also found in some Maltese words, mostly of Italian origin, such as \*sigurtà\* (old Italian: \*sicurtà\*'security'), \*kafè\* (Italian: \*caffè\*'coffee'), \*Marì\* (a name), \*però\* (Italian: però 'but') and tiramisù (Italian: tiramisù 'tiramisu'). The official rules governing the structure of the Maltese language are recorded in the official guidebook \*Tagħrif fuq il-Kitba Maltija\* (English: \*Knowledge on Writing in Maltese\*) issued by the \*Akkademja tal-Malti\* (Academy of the Maltese language). The first edition of this book was printed in 1924 by the Maltese government's printing press. The rules were further expanded in the 1984 book, \*iż-Żieda mat-Tagħrif\*, which focused mainly on the increasing influence of Romance and English words. In 1992 the academy issued the \*Aġġornament tat-Tagħrif fuq il-Kitba Maltija\*, which updated the previous works.[[42]](#cite\_note-42) The [National Council for the Maltese Language](/wiki/National\_Council\_for\_the\_Maltese\_Language) (KNM) is the main regulator of the Maltese language (see Maltese Language Act, below). However, the academy's orthography rules are still valid and official.

### Written Maltese [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=11)] Since Maltese evolved after the [Italo-Normans](/wiki/Italo-Normans) ended Arab rule of the islands, a written form of the language was not developed for a long time after the Arabs' expulsion in the middle of the thirteenth century. Under the rule of the [Knights Hospitaller](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller), both French and Italian were used for official documents and correspondence. During the [British colonial period](/wiki/Crown\_Colony\_of\_Malta), the use of English was encouraged through education, with Italian being regarded as the next-most important language. In the late 18th century and throughout the 19th century, philologists and academics such as [Mikiel Anton Vassalli](/wiki/Mikiel\_Anton\_Vassalli) made a concerted effort to standardise written Maltese. Many examples of written Maltese exist from before this period, always in the Latin alphabet, \*[Il-Kantilena](/wiki/Il-Kantilena)\* from the 15th century being the earliest example of written Maltese. In 1934, Maltese was recognised as an official language. ### Samples [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=12)] Maltese has both [Semitic](/wiki/Semitic\_languages) vocabulary and words derived from [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance\_languages), primarily [Italian](/wiki/Italian\_language). Words such as \*tweġiba\* (Arabic origin) and \*risposta\* (Italian origin) have the same meaning ("answer") but are both used in Maltese, rather like "answer" and "response" in English.

Below are two versions of the same translations, one with vocabulary mostly derived from Semitic root words and the other using Romance [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword) from the [Treaty establishing a Constitution for Europe](/wiki/Treaty\_establishing\_a\_Constitution\_for\_Europe)[[a]](#cite\_note-43)  Maltese (Semitic vocabulary)   ---   \*L-Għaqda hija mibnija fuq is-siwi ta' għadir għall-ġieħ il-bniedem, ta' ħelsien, ta' għażil il-ġemgħa, ta' ndaqs bejn il-ġnus, tas-saltna tad-dritt[[b]](#cite\_note-44) u tal-għadir għall-ħaqq tal-bniedem, wkoll il-ħaqq ta' wħud li huma f'minoranzi.[[c]](#cite\_note-45) Dan is-siwi huwa mqassam bejn il-Pajjiżi[[d]](#cite\_note-46) Msieħba, f'nies li tħaddan il-kotrija, li ma tgħejjibx, li ddann, li tgħaqqad u li tiżen indaqs in-nisa u l-irġiel.\*  Below is the [Lord's Prayer](/wiki/Lord%27s\_Prayer) in Maltese compared to other Semitic languages ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic) and [Syriac](/wiki/Syriac\_language)) with [cognates](/wiki/Cognates) highlighted:  Maltese[[43]](#cite\_note-47)  Syriac (Romanised)[[45]](#cite\_note-49)  ---  ---   Missier\*\*na\*\*, \*\*li\*\* inti \*\*fis-smewwiet\*\*, \*\*jitqaddes ismek\*\*, tiġi saltnatek, \*\*ikun\*\* li trid int, kif \*\*fis-sema\*\*, hekkda f\*\*l-art\*\*. \*\*\*Ħobżna\*\* ta' kuljum \*\*agħtina\*\*\*\*llum\*\*. \*\*Aħfrilna\*\*\*\*dnubietna\*\*, bħal\*\*ma\*\*\*\*naħfru\*\* lil min hu ħati \*\*għalina\*\*\*. \*\*U la ddaħħalniex fit-tiġrib\*\*, iżda eħlisna mid-deni. \*\*Ammen\*\*  Abun, \*\*d-bashmayo\*\*, \*\*nithqadash\*\*\*\*shmokh\*\*, \*\*tithe\*\* malkuthokh, nehwe sebyonokh aykano \*\*d-bashmayo\*\* oph bar`o. \*hab lan lahmo d-sunqonan \*\*yowmono\*\* washbuq lan hawbayn \*\*wahtohayn\*\*\* aykano doph hnan shbaqan l-hayobayn lo ta`lan l-nesyuno elo paso lan \*\*men\*\* bisho \*\*Amin\*\*  English[[46]](#cite\_note-50)  Standard Arabic (Romanised)[[48]](#cite\_note-52)  ---  ---  All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. The following table compares additional cognates in Maltese and some other varieties of Arabic (all forms are written phonetically, as in the source):[[54]](#cite\_note-58)  [Cairene](/wiki/Egyptian\_Arabic)  [Iraqi](/wiki/Mesopotamian\_Arabic)  Yemenite(Sanaani)  Standard Arabic   ---  ---  ---  ---   \*'alb\*  \*galeb\*  \*galb\*  قلب (\*qalb\*) /qalb/   \*wa't\*  \*waket\*  \*wagt\*  وقت (\*waqt\*) /waqt/   \*'amar\*  \*qamar\*  \*gamar\*  قمر (\*qamar\*) /qamar/   \*kalb\*  \*čaleb\*  \*kalb\*  كلب (\*kalb\*) /kalb/  Siculo-Arabic dialect which was spoken in Sicily and Malta is the [ancestor](/wiki/Genetic\_relationship\_(linguistics)) of the Maltese language,[[11]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEBrincat2005-11) some Siculo-Arabic words are still used in modern [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian\_language) (a Romance language spoken in Sicily):  [Siculo-Arabic](/wiki/Siculo-Arabic)(in [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian\_language))  English  ---  ---   \*[babbaluci](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/babbaluci#Sicilian)\*  snail  \*[ġiebja](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%C4%A1iebja#Maltese)\*  جابية (\*jābiya\*)   \*[giuggiulena](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/giuggiulena#Sicilian)\*  sesame seed  \*[sieqja](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/sieqja#Maltese)\*  ساقية (\*sāqiya\*)   \*[tannura](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/tannura#Sicilian)\*  oven  \*[żagħfran](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%C5%BCag%C4%A7fran#Maltese)\*  زعفران (\*zaʿfarān\*)   \*[zagara](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/zagara#Sicilian)\*  blossom  \*[żbib](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%C5%BCbib#Maltese)\*  زبيب (\*zabīb\*)   \*[zuccu](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/zuccu#Sicilian)\*  tree trunk  \*[tebut](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/tebut#Maltese)\*  تابوت (\*tābūt\*)  The Maltese language has merged many of the original Arabic consonants (in particular the [emphatic consonants](/wiki/Emphatic\_consonant)) with others common to European languages. Thus, original [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic\_language)/d/, /ð/, and /dˤ/ all merged into Maltese /d/. The vowels, meanwhile, separated from the three in Classical Arabic (/aiu/) into five, as is more typical of many European languages (/aɛiou/). Some unstressed short vowels have been elided. The common Arabic greeting \*as salāmu 'alaykum\* is cognate with \*is-sliem għalikom\* in Maltese (lit. "the peace for you", peace be with you), as are similar greetings in other Semitic languages (e.g. \*shalom ʿalekhem\* in [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew\_language)).

### Romance (Sicilian and Italian) [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=15)] An analysis of the etymology of the 41,000 words in Aquilina's \*Maltese–English Dictionary\* shows that words of Romance origin make up 52% of the Maltese vocabulary,[[11]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEBrincat2005-11) although other sources claim from 40%[[12]](#cite\_note-autogenerated2-12) to 55%. Romance vocabulary tends to deal with more complex concepts. Most words come from [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian\_language) and thus exhibit Sicilian phonetic characteristics, such as /u/ rather than Italian /o/, and /i/ rather than Italian /e/ (e.g. \*tiatru\* not \*teatro\* and \*fidi\* not \*fede\*). Also, as with Old Sicilian, /ʃ/ (English \*sh\*) is written \*x\* and this produces spellings such as: \*ambaxxata\*/ambaʃːaːta/ ('embassy'), \*xena\*/ʃeːna/ ('scene'; compare Italian \*ambasciata\*, \*scena\*). [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian\_language)  [English](/wiki/English\_language)  ---  ---   \*[scola](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/scola#Sicilian)\*  school  \*[gvern](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/gvern#Maltese)\*  \*[governo](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/governo#Italian)\*   \*[ripùbblica](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/rip%C3%B9bblica#Sicilian)\*  republic  \*[re](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/re#Maltese)\*  \*[re](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/re#Italian)\*   \*[natura](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/natura#Sicilian)\*  nature  \*[pulizija](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/pulizija#Maltese)\*  \*[polizia](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/polizia#Italian)\*   \*[centru](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/centru#Sicilian)\*  centre  \*[teatru](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/teatru#Maltese)\*  \*[teatro](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/teatro#Italian)\*  A tendency in modern Maltese is to adopt further influences from English and Italian. Complex Latinate English words adopted into Maltese are often given Italian or Sicilian forms,[[11]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEBrincat2005-11) even if the resulting words do not appear in either of those languages. For instance, the words \*evaluation\*, \*industrial action\*, and \*chemical armaments\* become \*evalwazzjoni\*, \*azzjoni industrjali\*, and \*armamenti kimiċi\* in Maltese, while the Italian terms are \*valutazione\*, \*vertenza sindacale\*, and \*armi chimiche\* respectively.

(The origin of the terms may be narrowed even further to [British English](/wiki/British\_English); the phrase \*[industrial action](/wiki/Industrial\_action)\* is meaningless in the United States.) This is comparable to the situation with English borrowings into the [Italo-Australian dialect](/wiki/Italo-Australian\_dialect). English words of Germanic origin are generally preserved relatively unchanged. Some influences of [African Romance](/wiki/African\_Romance) on the Arabic and [Berber](/wiki/Berber\_languages) spoken in the [Maghreb](/wiki/Maghreb) are theorised; these may then have passed into Maltese.[[55]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEKossmann201375-59) For example, in [calendar](/wiki/Calendar)[month](/wiki/Month) names, the word \*furar\* 'February' is only found in the Maghreb and in Maltese – proving the word's ancient pedigree. The region also has a form of another Latin month in \*awi/ussu\* < \*augustus\*.[[55]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEKossmann201375-59) This word does not appear to be a loan word through Arabic, and may have been taken over directly from Late Latin or African Romance.[[55]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEKossmann201375-59) Scholars theorise that a Latin-based system provided forms such as \*awi/ussu\* and \*furar\* in African Romance, with the system then mediating Latin/Romance names through Arabic for some month names during the Islamic period.[[56]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEKossmann201376-60) The same situation exists for Maltese which mediated words from [Italian](/wiki/Italian\_language), and retains both non-Italian forms such as \*awissu/awwissu\* and \*frar\*, and Italian forms such as \*april\*.[[56]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEKossmann201376-60) ### Berber [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=16)] Like the [Maghrebi Arabic dialects](/wiki/Maghrebi\_Arabic), Maltese has a significant vocabulary derived from [Berber languages](/wiki/Berber\_languages).

Whether these words entered Maltese by being inherited from [Siculo-Arabic](/wiki/Siculo-Arabic) or were directly loaned from Berber languages is not yet known. These include:[[57]](#cite\_note-61)  Berber languages   ---   \*azrem ašal\*, lit. 'land worm', ([Kabyle](/wiki/Kabyle\_language))   \*tifakrunin\* ([Jerbi](/wiki/Jerba\_Berber)), \*ifekran\* ([Tashelhiyt](/wiki/Shilha\_language)), \*ifkran\* (Kabyle)   \*aqadum\*, lit. 'face, frown' (Kabyle)   \*gandūz\*, lit. 'young calf' (Jerbi)   \*ageržum\* ([Mozabite](/wiki/Mozabite\_language), Tashelhiyt)   \*tilfaf\* ([Ouargli](/wiki/Ouargli\_language)), \*tifāf, tilfāf, tiffāf\* ([Tarifit](/wiki/Tarifit))   \*talaɣūda\* ([Tunisian Arabic](/wiki/Tunisian\_Arabic)), \*telɣūda\* ([Algerian Arabic](/wiki/Algerian\_Arabic))   \*kuskesu, kuskus\* (Kabyle)   \*aferḍas\* (Ouargli, Kabyle)   ‘\*affež\* (Algerian Arabic), \*effeẓ\* (Ouargli, Mozabite)   \*tažrant\* (Jerbi)   \*zrar\* (Mozabite, Ouargli), \*azrar\* (Kabyle, [Nafusi](/wiki/Nafusi\_language))   \*wárẓag\* ([Mrazig](/wiki/Mrazig))   \*buqišrem\* (Kabyle)   \*fudalɣem\* (Kabyle)   \*uzur\* (Kabyle), \*uzzur\* (Tarifit)   \*lelleš\* (Mozabite)   \*bbesbes\* (Ouargli)   \*ṭṭebṭeb\* (Ouargli)   \*webben\* (Mozabite)  ### English [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=17)] English loanwords, which are becoming more commonplace, may constitute up to 20% of Maltese vocabulary,[[12]](#cite\_note-autogenerated2-12) though other sources claim as little as 6%.[[11]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEBrincat2005-11) This percentage discrepancy is due to the fact that a number of new English loanwords are sometimes not considered part of the official Maltese vocabulary, hence they are not included in certain dictionaries.[[11]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEBrincat2005-11) Also, English loanwords of Latinate origin are very often Italianized, as discussed above. English loanwords are generally transliterated, although standard English pronunciation is virtually always retained. Below are a few examples:  English  ---   football  baskitbol   club  friġġ   computer  kejk  "Fridge" is a common [shortening](/wiki/Clipping\_(morphology)) of "refrigerator".

"Refrigerator" is a Latinate word which could be imported into Maltese as \*rifriġeratur\*, whereas the [Italian](/wiki/Italian\_language) word is \*frigorifero\* or \*refrigeratore\*. ### Calendar [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=18)] The days of the week (Maltese: \*jiem il-ġimgħa\*) in Maltese, which are derived from Arabic, are referred to by number which is also typical in other Semitic languages, Days of the week are commonly preceded by the word \*nhar\* meaning 'day'.
