Il-waranofsinhar it-tajjeb. Good evening. Il-lejl it-tajjeb. / Il-lejla t-tajba  Goodbye. ## Grammar [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=22)] Maltese grammar is fundamentally derived from [Arabic](/wiki/Varieties\_of\_Arabic), although [Latin](/wiki/Romance\_languages) and [English](/wiki/English\_language) noun pluralisation patterns are also used on borrowed words. ### Adjectives and adverbs [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=23)] [Adjectives](/wiki/Adjective) follow [nouns](/wiki/Noun). There are no separately formed native [adverbs](/wiki/Adverb), and word order is fairly flexible. Both nouns and adjectives of [Semitic](/wiki/Semitic\_languages) origin take the definite article (for example, \*It-tifel il-kbir\*, lit. "The boy the elder"="The elder boy"). This rule does not apply to adjectives of Romance origin. ### Nouns [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=24)] Nouns are pluralised and also have a [dual](/wiki/Dual\_grammatical\_number) marker. Semitic plurals are complex; if they are regular (\*sħaħ\*), they are marked by \*-iet\*/\*-ijiet\*, e.g., \*art\*, \*artijiet\* "lands (territorial possessions or property)" (cf. Arabic \*-at\* and Hebrew \*-ot\*/\*-oth\*) or \*-in\* (cf. Arabic \*-īn\* and Hebrew \*-im\*). If irregular, they fall in the '\*plural miksur'\* ([broken plural](/wiki/Broken\_plural)) category, in which a word is pluralised by internal vowel changes: \*ktieb\*, \*kotba\*" book", "books"; \*raġel\*, \*irġiel\* "man", "men".

Some nouns can also take both kinds of plurals, like \*bieb\* (door) = \*bwieb, bibien, triq\* (road) = \*toroq, triqat,\* and \*tazza\* (cup) = \*tazzez, tazzi\* Words of Romance origin are usually pluralised in two manners: addition of \*-i\* or \*-jiet\* . For example, \*lingwa\*, \*lingwi\* "languages", from Sicilian \*lingua\*, \*lingui\*. Words of English origin are pluralised by adding either an "-s" or "-jiet", for example, \*friġġ\*, \*friġis\* from the word \*fridge\*. Some words can be pluralised with either of the suffixes to denote the plural. A few words borrowed from English can amalgamate both suffixes, like \*brikksa\* from the English \*brick\*, which can adopt either collective form \*brikks\* or the plural form \*brikksiet\*. Interestingly, some nouns of English origins still take Semitic plurals, like \*senter\* (gun) coming from 'centre', takes the plural \*snieter,\* like in \*ħanut\* (shop) = \*ħwienet\*, although it is no longer done with new loanwords. #### Derivation [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=25)] As in [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic), nouns are often derived by changing, adding or removing the vowels within a [triliteral root](/wiki/Triliteral\_root).

These are some of the patterns used for nouns:[[59]](#cite\_note-63) \* CaCiC – \*xadin\* (monkey), \*sadid\* (rust) \* CCiC – \*żbib\* (raisin), ġbid (pulling) \* CaCCa – \*baqra\* (cow), \*basla\* (onion) \* CeCCa – \*werqa\* (leaf), \*xewqa\* (wish) \* CoCCa – \*borka\* (wild duck), \*forka\* (gallows) \* CaCC – \*qalb\* (heart), \*sajd\* (fishing) \* CeCC – \*kelb\* (dog), \*xemx\* (sun) \* CCuCija – \*tfulija\* (childhood), \*xbubija\* (maidenhood) \* CCuCa – \*rtuba\* (softness), \*bjuda\* (whiteness) \* CaCCaC – \*tallab\* (beggar), \*bajjad\* (whitewasher) The so-called mimated nouns (nomi mimmati) use the [prefix](/wiki/Prefix)\*m-\* in addition to [vowel](/wiki/Vowel) changes. This pattern can be used to indicate [place](/wiki/Location\_(geography))[names](/wiki/Name), [tools](/wiki/Tool), [abstractions](/wiki/Abstraction), etc. These are some of the patterns used for mimated nouns: \* ma-CCeC – \*marden\* (spindle) \* mi-CCeC – \*minkeb\* (elbow), \*miżwed\* (pod) \* mu-CCaC – \*musmar\* (nail), \*munqar\* (beak) ### Article [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=26)] The [proclitic](/wiki/Proclitic)\*il-\* is the [definite article](/wiki/Definite\_article), equivalent to "the" in English and "al-" in Arabic. The Maltese article becomes \*l-\* before or after a vowel.

\* \*l-omm\* (the mother) \* \*rajna l-Papa\* (we saw the Pope) \* \*il-missier\* (the father) The Maltese article [assimilates](/wiki/Assimilation\_(linguistics)) to a following non-ġ [coronal consonant](/wiki/Coronal\_consonant) (called \*konsonanti xemxin\* "[sun consonants](/wiki/Sun\_letter)"), namely: \* Ċ \*iċ-ċikkulata\* (the chocolate) \* D \*id-dar\* (the house) \* N \*in-nar\* (the fire) \* R \*ir-razzett\* (the farm) \* S \*is-serrieq\* (the saw) \* T \*it-tifel\* (the child) \* X \*ix-xemx\* (the sun) \* Ż \*iż-żarbuna\* (the shoe) \* Z \*iz-zalzett\* (the sausage) ### Verbs [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=27)] Verbs show the Semitic [triliteral](/wiki/Triliteral) pattern, in which a verb is [conjugated](/wiki/Grammatical\_conjugation) with [prefixes](/wiki/Prefix\_(linguistics)), [suffixes](/wiki/Suffix), and [infixes](/wiki/Infix) (for example \*ktibna\*, Arabic \*katabna\*, Hebrew \*kathabhnu\* (Modern Hebrew: katavnu) "we wrote"). An example is the Semitic root X-M-X ('sun'), for example \*xemx\* (sun), \*xmux\* (suns), \*xemxi\* (sunny), \*xemxata\* (sunstroke), \*nixxemmex\* (I sunbathe), \*ma xxemmixtx\* (I didn't sunbathe), \*tixmix\* (the act of sunbathing). Maltese also features the [agglutination](/wiki/Agglutination) of verb suffixes indicating direction of action, for example \*agħmilhomli\* "make them for me"← \*agħmel\* "make" in the imperative + \*hom\* from \*huma\* "them" + \*li\* suffix indicating first person singular and \*ħasletielu\* "she washed it for him"←\*ħaslet\* "she washed" from the verb \*ħasel\* "to wash" + \*ie\* the object + \*lu\* suffix indicating third person masculine singular. The two [tenses](/wiki/Grammatical\_tense) are present and perfect.

The Maltese verb system incorporates Romance verbs and adds Maltese suffixes and prefixes to them, for example; \*iddeċidejna\* "we decided" ← \*(i)ddeċieda\* "decide", a Romance verb + \*-ejna\*, a Maltese first person plural perfect marker. There is no infinitive in Maltese, yet something similar called the \*mamma\* is used, where the 3rd person male perfect singular is considered the root where the consonants are used to form the root (\*l-għerq),\* and the verb form found in dictionaries. In order to make a future sentence, we use the present tense with the addition of \*ħa\* or \*sejjer\* (conjugated, abbreviated to \*se\* or \*ser\* for simplicity). Ex. Għada ħa mmur naħsel il-ħwejjeġ (tomorrow I'll go wash my clothes). ## Media [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=28)] Main article: [Language in the media in Malta](/wiki/Languages\_of\_Malta#Media) As Malta is a multilingual country, the use of Maltese in the mass media is shared with other European languages, namely [English](/wiki/English\_language) and [Italian](/wiki/Italian\_language). The majority of television stations broadcast from Malta in English or Maltese, although broadcasts from [Italy](/wiki/Italy) in Italian are also received on the islands. Similarly, there are more Maltese-language radio programs than English ones broadcast from Malta, but again, Italian broadcasts are also picked up. Coverage in newspaper periodicals is generally equally split between Maltese and English.

Maltese is little used on the internet and few websites are written in the language. In a survey of Maltese cultural websites conducted in 2004 on behalf of the Maltese government, 12 of 13 were in English only and the remaining one was multilingual but did not include Maltese.[[60]](#cite\_note-64) In 2011, only 6.5 per cent of Maltese internet users reported employing Maltese online, which may be a consequence of the lack of online support for the language.[[61]](#cite\_note-65) ## Code-switching [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_language&action=edit&section=29)] The Maltese population, being fluent in both Maltese and English, displays [code-switching](/wiki/Code-switching) (referred to as [Maltenglish](/wiki/Maltenglish)) in certain localities and between certain social groups.[[11]](#cite\_note-FOOTNOTEBrincat2005-11) ## Malta](/wiki/Republic\_of\_Malta) The following [outline](/wiki/Outline\_(list)) is provided as an overview of and topical guide to Malta: \*\*[Malta](/wiki/Malta)\*\* is a small and densely populated [sovereign](/wiki/List\_of\_sovereign\_states)[island nation](/wiki/Island\_nation) located in [Europe](/wiki/Europe), in the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean\_Sea).[[1]](#cite\_note-CIA\_World\_Factbook-1) Malta comprises an [archipelago](/wiki/Archipelago) of [seven islands](/wiki/Maltese\_islands), of which the three largest are inhabited.

Malta is located 93 km (58 mi) south of [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily), and 288 km (179 mi) north of [North Africa](/wiki/North\_Africa), giving the country a warm, [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean\_climate).[[2]](#cite\_note-2) The nation's [capital](/wiki/Capital\_(political)) is the 16th century city of [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta). Throughout much of its history, Malta has been considered a crucial location due in large part to its position in the Mediterranean Sea.[[3]](#cite\_note-3) It was held by several ancient cultures including [Sicilians](/wiki/Sicily), [Romans](/wiki/Roman\_Empire), [Phoenicians](/wiki/Phoenicia), [Byzantines](/wiki/Byzantine) and others. The island is commonly associated with the [Knights of St. John](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller) who ruled it. This, along with the historic pseudo-historic and religiously claimed shipwreck of [St. Paul](/wiki/Paul\_of\_Tarsus) on the island, and since the 12th century ingrained a [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman\_Catholic\_Church) legacy which is still the official [religion in Malta](/wiki/Religion\_in\_Malta) today combined with secular values. The country's official languages are [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_language) and [English](/wiki/English\_language); the former is the national language and the latter a legacy from Malta's period as a [British colony](/wiki/British\_colony). Malta gained [independence](/wiki/Independence) in 1964 and is, as of 2025, a member of the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth\_of\_Nations), as well as the [European Union](/wiki/European\_Union), which it joined in 2004.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Culinary traditions of Malta \*\*Maltese cuisine\*\* reflects [Maltese history](/wiki/History\_of\_Malta), showing strong [Italian](/wiki/Italian\_cuisine) influences, as well as influences from [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish\_cuisine), [French](/wiki/French\_cuisine), [Provençal](/wiki/Proven%C3%A7al\_cuisine) and other [Mediterranean cuisines](/wiki/Mediterranean\_cuisine), with some later [British](/wiki/British\_cuisine) culinary influence. Being positioned along important trade routes, having to import most of its foodstuffs, and having to cater for the resident foreign powers who ruled the islands opened Maltese cuisine to outside influences.[[1]](#cite\_note-1) The traditional Maltese stewed rabbit, \*[stuffat tal-fenek](/wiki/Stuffat\_tal-Fenek)\*, is often identified as the [national dish](/wiki/National\_dish). ## History [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_cuisine&action=edit&section=1)] Main articles: [History of Malta](/wiki/History\_of\_Malta) and [Timeline of Maltese history](/wiki/Timeline\_of\_Maltese\_history) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Traditional\_Maltese\_Kusksu\_%28Fava\_Bean\_Soup%29.jpg/250px-Traditional\_Maltese\_Kusksu\_%28Fava\_Bean\_Soup%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Traditional\_Maltese\_Kusksu\_(Fava\_Bean\_Soup).jpg) A typical serving of \*[kusksu](/wiki/Kusksu)\* made with seasonal broad beans Malta's history and geography, at the center of trade routes, had an important influence on its cuisine. Foreign dishes and tastes were absorbed, transformed and adapted.[[2]](#cite\_note-malta.academia.edu-2)[Italian](/wiki/Italian\_cuisine) (specifically [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian\_cuisine)), [Middle Eastern](/wiki/Middle\_Eastern\_cuisine) and [Arab foods](/wiki/Arab\_cuisine) exerted a strong influence, but the presence in Malta of the [Knights of St John](/wiki/Knights\_of\_St\_John) and the [British](/wiki/British\_colonies) also brought elements from further afield. The Knights hailed from many European countries, particularly France, Italy, and Spain, bringing influences from these countries.

\*[Aljotta](/wiki/Aljotta)\*, for example, a fish broth with garlic, herbs, and tomatoes, is the Maltese adaptation of [bouillabaisse](/wiki/Bouillabaisse).[[3]](#cite\_note-3) The Knights' contacts and wealth brought also food from the New World; it has been suggested that Malta may have been one of the first countries in Europe (after Spain) where chocolate was first tasted.[[4]](#cite\_note-4) The British military presence meant a market of a garrison and their families and, later, mass tourism from the United Kingdom. British food products, condiments and sauces like [English mustard](/wiki/English\_mustard), [Bovril](/wiki/Bovril), [HP Sauce](/wiki/HP\_Sauce), and [Worcestershire sauce](/wiki/Worcestershire\_sauce) are still a subtle but pervasive presence in Maltese cooking. Other imports were only nominal. The Maltese word \*aljoli\* is one of the variations on [aioli](/wiki/Aioli) across the Mediterranean; the Maltese version of the sauce is based on herbs, olives, anchovies, and olive oil. Similarly, while the Maltese word \*taġen\* is related to "[tajine](/wiki/Tajine)", in Maltese the word refers exclusively to a metal frying pan. ## Cuisine and identity [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_cuisine&action=edit&section=2)] Main article: [Culture of Malta](/wiki/Culture\_of\_Malta) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Malta\_Rabbit\_fried\_with\_wine\_and\_garlic.JPG/250px-Malta\_Rabbit\_fried\_with\_wine\_and\_garlic.JPG)](/wiki/File:Malta\_Rabbit\_fried\_with\_wine\_and\_garlic.JPG) Rabbit fried with wine and garlic [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Imqaret\_with\_ice\_cream\_from\_Malta.jpg/250px-Imqaret\_with\_ice\_cream\_from\_Malta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Imqaret\_with\_ice\_cream\_from\_Malta.jpg) Imqaret served with ice cream There are a number of junctures in which development in Maltese cuisine related to issues of identity.

The most significant example is the traditional Maltese \*stuffat tal-fenek\* (rabbit stew), often identified as the national dish, quite possibly started off as a form of symbolic resistance to the hunting restrictions imposed by the [Knights of St John](/wiki/Knights\_of\_St\_John).[[5]](#cite\_note-:0-5) The dish became popular after the lifting of restrictions in the late 18th century (and by which time the indigenous breed, the [Tax-Xiber](/wiki/Tax-Xiber) rabbit, had multiplied and prices dropped) and the domestication of rabbits, a technique which could have been imported from France thanks to the French Knights.[[6]](#cite\_note-6) The popularity of pork and its presence in various dishes could be attributed to Malta being on the edge of the Christian world. Consuming a food which is prohibited in the [Muslim culinary culture](/wiki/Islamic\_dietary\_laws) could have been a way of self-identification by distinguishing oneself from the other. In addition to pork dishes (such as grilled pork cuts or stuffed flank) and the exclusive predominance of pork in indigenous Maltese sausages, adding some pork to dishes such as \*[kawlata](/wiki/Kawlata)\* (a vegetable soup) and \*ross il-forn\* (baked rice) has been common practice in the Maltese vernacular cuisine for centuries.[[2]](#cite\_note-malta.academia.edu-2) For the [Café Europe](/wiki/Caf%C3%A9\_Europe) event held during the [Austrian Presidency of the EU](/wiki/Presidency\_of\_the\_Council\_of\_the\_European\_Union) in 2006, the "representative" Maltese pastry chosen was the \*[maqrut](/wiki/Maqrut)\*.

## Variations [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_cuisine&action=edit&section=3)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Malta\_Gbejniet.JPG/250px-Malta\_Gbejniet.JPG)](/wiki/File:Malta\_Gbejniet.JPG) Selection of fresh and cured Ġbejniet [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Ravjul\_w\_Gbejna.JPG/250px-Ravjul\_w\_Gbejna.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ravjul\_w\_Gbejna.JPG) Gozitan ravjul ### Regional [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_cuisine&action=edit&section=4)] Further information: [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo) Despite Malta's small size, there are some regional variations, especially with the island of [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo). This is evidenced in some names such as the Gozitan cheeselet \*[ġbejna](/wiki/%C4%A0bejna)\* (\*ġbejna t'Għawdex\*) and \*ftira Għawdxija\*, [flatbread](/wiki/Flatbread) topped or filled with potatoes or [ġbejniet](/wiki/%C4%A0bejniet) with eggs, grated cheese, tomatoes, anchovies, olives, ricotta, and Maltese sausage as other possible ingredients.[[7]](#cite\_note-7) Other Gozitan variants include the use of unusual ingredients. Gozitan cheeselets, for example, are used as filling for [ravioli](/wiki/Ravioli) instead of the usual [ricotta](/wiki/Ricotta). ### Seasonal [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_cuisine&action=edit&section=5)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/e/eb/Heart\_figolla.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heart\_figolla.jpg) Figolla – the traditional Easter sweet The strongest seasonal variations are seen in desserts and sweets. \*Prinjolata\*,[[8]](#cite\_note-8)[[9]](#cite\_note-9)\*[kwareżimal](/wiki/Kware%C5%BCimal)\*, \*karamelli tal-ħarrub\*, \*ftira tar-Randan\*, \*figolla\* and \*qagħaq tal-għasel\* are all examples of sweets associated with a particular season. Because Catholic fasting during [Lent](/wiki/Lent) involved mostly meats and dairy products, fish such as [lampuki](/wiki/Lampuki),[whitebait](/wiki/Whitebait) and [salted cod](/wiki/Salted\_cod) were a popular dish during this period, as were stewed [snails](/wiki/Helix\_aspersa) ([Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_language): \*bebbux\*), stuffed [artichokes](/wiki/Artichoke) ([Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_language): \*qaqoċċ mimli\*) and [fritters](/wiki/Fritter) ([Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_language): \*sfineġ\*) of [ġbejna](/wiki/%C4%A0bejna), and vegetables. During [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy\_Week), bakers also bake a large [bagel](/wiki/Bagel) typically studded with some almonds on top called \*qagħqa tal-appostli\* (lit. "apostles' bagel").

Usually coinciding with the spring, there are also seasonal variations to certain dishes at the time of [Lent](/wiki/Lent) as in, for example, adding fresh [broad beans](/wiki/Broad\_bean) to dishes such as \*[kusksu](/wiki/Kusksu)\*, a vegetable and pasta dish.[[10]](#cite\_note-10) During November, \*għadam tal-mejtin\* (lit. "bones of the dead", in [Italian](/wiki/Italian\_language): \*ossa dei morti\*) are prepared. These are a layer of marzipan sandwiched between sweet pastry and topped with white icing (normal icing and not buttercream icing). \*Qagħaq tal-għasel\* (honey and [treacle](/wiki/Treacle) rings) are a traditional Maltese sweet. They consist of a pastry with a stuffing of sorts inside, made into a ring shape, with slits along the ring for the filling to dark filling to ooze out. The filling is traditionally a mixture of [honey](/wiki/Honey), [molasses](/wiki/Molasses), [aniseed](/wiki/Aniseed), [cinnamon](/wiki/Cinnamon), and [cloves](/wiki/Cloves); some people add cocoa powder for a hint of chocolate. Although traditionally made for [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas), it became so popular that people started making it all year round, and nowadays many are unaware that it was originally made for Christmas. The ring symbolizes "eternal happiness" and the filling oozes out signifies "overflowing with goodness". ## See also [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_cuisine&action=edit&section=6)] \* [List of Maltese dishes](/wiki/List\_of\_Maltese\_dishes) \* [Maltese bread](/wiki/Maltese\_bread) \* [Maltese wine](/wiki/Maltese\_wine) ## References [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_cuisine&action=edit&section=7)] 1. \*\*[^](#cite\_ref-1)\*\*[What they ate](https://www.um.edu.mt/library/oar/bitstream/handle/123456789/19252/What\_they\_ate\_food\_and\_foodways\_in\_Mdina.pdf?sequence=1&isAllowed=y)[\*[permanent dead link](/wiki/Wikipedia:Link\_rot)\*], Heland Project 2. ^ [Jump up to: \*\*\*a\*\*\*](#cite\_ref-malta.academia.edu\_2-0)[\*\*\*b\*\*\*](#cite\_ref-malta.academia.edu\_2-1)Billiard, E.

It is classified as an [advanced economy](/wiki/Developed\_country) by the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International\_Monetary\_Fund)[[27]](#cite\_note-28) and is considered a [high-income country](/wiki/Developed\_country) by the [World Bank](/wiki/World\_Bank)[[28]](#cite\_note-29) and an innovation-driven economy by the [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World\_Economic\_Forum).[[29]](#cite\_note-30) It is a member of the [European Union](/wiki/European\_Union) and of the [eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone), having formally adopted the euro on 1 January 2008.[[30]](#cite\_note-31) The strengths of [Malta](/wiki/Malta)'s economy are its advantageous location, being situated in the middle of the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean\_Sea) at a crossroads between Europe, [North Africa](/wiki/North\_Africa) and the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle\_East), its fully developed open [market economy](/wiki/Market\_economy), [multilingual](/wiki/Multilingual) population (88% of Maltese people speak English),[[31]](#cite\_note-32) productive [labour force](/wiki/Labour\_force), low corporate tax[[32]](#cite\_note-33) and well developed finance and ICT clusters. The economy is dependent on foreign trade, manufacturing (especially electronics), [tourism](/wiki/Tourism) and other services in the tertiary sector of the economy.

In 2014, over 1.7 million tourists visited the island.[[33]](#cite\_note-34) Malta's [GDP](/wiki/GDP) per capita in 2024, adjusted by [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing\_power\_parity), stood at $67,682[[4]](#cite\_note-IMFWEOMT-5) and ranked 15th in the list of EU countries in terms of purchasing power standard.[[34]](#cite\_note-35) In the 2013 calendar year, Malta recorded a budget deficit of 2.7%,[[35]](#cite\_note-36) which is within the limits for eurozone countries imposed by the [Maastricht criteria](/wiki/Euro\_convergence\_criteria), and Government gross debt of 69.8%.[[36]](#cite\_note-37) At 5.9%, Malta had the sixth-lowest unemployment rate in the EU in 2015.[[37]](#cite\_note-38) Malta is the 33rd-most democratic country in the world according to the [Economist Intelligence Unit](/wiki/Economist\_Intelligence\_Unit)'s [Democracy Index](/wiki/Democracy\_Index) with an upper-middle score on V-Dem's [Democracy Indices](/wiki/V-Dem\_Democracy\_Indices). ## Transport [(/w/index.php?title=Economy\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=1)] Malta has an airport, [Luqa International Airport](/wiki/Luqa\_International\_Airport), and two majors ports, [Malta Freeport](/wiki/Malta\_Freeport) and Valletta Cruise Port. Malta Freeport is used for all cargo ships and imports-exports while Valletta Cruise Port is only used by cruise ships. The Malta Freeport reported revenues of 170 million euros, while €90 million came from the Valletta Cruise Port, totaling €260 million. The Freeport connects Malta with 110 others ports in the world including 55 in the Mediterranean. Furthermore, 2,189 container ships carried 2.80 million TEU of container traffic in and out of Malta Freeport in 2022. The Valletta Cruise Port welcomed 900,000 passengers in 2023, contributing €53 million to the Maltese economy.

Luqa International Airport serves 35 different airlines with 115 destinations. In 2023, 7.8 million people travelled through the airport, providing $2.7 billion in revenues to the Maltese economy, equivalent to 24% of Malta's Gross Domestic Product (GDP). Specifically, the former air flag carrier of Malta [Air Malta](/wiki/Air\_Malta), [KM Malta Airlines](/wiki/KM\_Malta\_Airlines) since 2024, had a revenue of €679.4 million in 2022. Another new airline operating from Luqa Airport, [Universal Air](/wiki/Universal\_Air), started operations in April 2024 with a fleet of 6 aircraft. The airlines, airport, and surrounding businesses employ almost 7,000 people. ## History [(/w/index.php?title=Economy\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=2)] In the mid-thirteenth century, Abate Giliberto indicated that the population of the Maltese islands consisted of 1,119 families.[[38]](#cite\_note-:7-39) At the time, Maltese agriculture transitioned from [subsistence agriculture](/wiki/Subsistence\_agriculture) to a more commerce-based one through the cultivation for export of [cotton](/wiki/Cotton) and [cumin](/wiki/Cumin), which were introduced during the [Arab period](/wiki/History\_of\_Islam\_in\_southern\_Italy).[[39]](#cite\_note-:8-40) As a result, there was a need to import food from neighbouring [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily). Eventually, in the late fourteenth century, cotton production became the principal agricultural product in the archipelago, since it was one of the few production hubs in the [western Mediterranean](/wiki/Western\_Mediterranean). Processing cotton to weave it into the fabric was a labour-intensive activity, so the boost in cotton trade led to the population surging to 33,000.[[38]](#cite\_note-:7-39)[[39]](#cite\_note-:8-40) In an exploratory mission in 1524, the [Knights of St.

John](/wiki/Knights\_of\_St.\_John) noted how the islands were dependent on grain imports since agriculture was dedicated to the production of cotton, honey and cumin.[[40]](#cite\_note-41) During the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic\_Wars) (1800–1815), Malta's economy prospered and became the focal point of a major trading system. In 1808, two-thirds of the cargo consigned from Malta went to [Levant](/wiki/Levant) and [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt). Later, one half of the cargo was usually destined for [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste). Cargo consisted of largely British and colonial-manufactured goods. Malta's economy became prosperous from this trade and many artisans, such as weavers, found new jobs in the port industry. In 1820, during the [Battle of Navarino](/wiki/Battle\_of\_Navarino), which took place in [Greece](/wiki/Greece), the British fleet was based in Malta. In 1839, [P&O](/wiki/P%26O) and [East India Company](/wiki/East\_India\_Company) used Malta as a calling port on their Egypt and Levant runs. In 1869, the opening of the [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez\_Canal) benefited Malta's economy greatly as there was a massive increase in the shipping which entered in the port. The economy had entered a special phase. The [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean\_Sea) became the "world highway of trade" and a number of ships called at Malta for [coal](/wiki/Coal) and various supplies on their way to the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian\_Ocean) and the [Far East](/wiki/Far\_East). From 1871 to 1881, about 8,000 workers found jobs in the Malta docks and a number of banks opened in Malta.

By 1882, Malta reached the height of its prosperity. However, the boom did not last long. By the end of the 19th century, the economy began declining and by the 1940s, Malta's economy was in serious crisis. This was primarily due to the invention of large ships which had become oil-fired and therefore had no need to stop in the Grand Harbor of Malta to refuel. The British Government had to extend the dockyard. At the end of [World War II](/wiki/World\_War\_II), Malta's strategic importance had reached a low point. Modern air warfare technology and the invention of the [atomic bomb](/wiki/Nuclear\_weapon) had changed the importance of the military base. The British lost control of the [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez\_Canal) and withdrew from the naval dockyard, transforming it for commercial shipbuilding and ship repair purposes. ## Modern economy [(/w/index.php?title=Economy\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=3)] The Maltese economy is dependent on foreign trade, manufacturing (especially electronics and pharmaceuticals), and tourism. Malta adopted the [Euro](/wiki/Euro) currency on 1 January 2008. Tourist arrivals and foreign exchange earnings derived from tourism have steadily increased since 1987.[[41]](#cite\_note-42) Following the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September\_11\_attacks), the tourist industry suffered a temporary setback.

With the help of a favorable international economic climate, the availability of domestic resources, and industrial policies that support foreign export-oriented investment, the economy has been able to sustain a period of rapid growth.[\*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed)\*] Growing public and private sector demand for [credit](/wiki/Credit) has led — in the context of interest rate controls — to [credit rationing](/wiki/Credit\_rationing) to the private sector and the introduction of non-interest charges by banks. Despite these pressures, consumer price [inflation](/wiki/Inflation) has remained low (2.2% according to the Central Bank of Malta in 2007), reflecting the impact of a fixed exchange rate policy (100% hard peg to the euro, in preparation for currency changeover) and lingering [price controls](/wiki/Price\_controls). There is a strong manufacturing base for high value-added products like [electronics](/wiki/Consumer\_electronics) and [pharmaceuticals](/wiki/Medication), and the manufacturing sector has more than 250 foreign-owned, export-oriented enterprises. Tourism generates around 15% of GDP. [Film production in Malta](/wiki/List\_of\_films\_shot\_in\_Malta) is another growing industry (approx. 35 million euros between 1997 and 2011), despite stiff competition from other film locations in [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern\_Europe) and [North Africa](/wiki/North\_Africa), with the Malta Film Commission providing support services to foreign film companies for the production of [feature cinema](/wiki/List\_of\_films\_shot\_in\_Malta), commercials and television series.[[42]](#cite\_note-43) From 2001 to 2005 the mean GDP real growth was 0.4%[[43]](#cite\_note-44) due to Malta losing pace in tourism and other industries.

Unemployment was down to 4.4%, its lowest level in 3 years. Many formerly state-owned companies are being privatised—and the market liberalised. Fiscal policy has been directed toward bringing down the budget deficit after public debt grew from a negative figure in 1988 to 56% in 1999 and 69.1% in 2009.[[44]](#cite\_note-autogenerated1-45) By 2007, the deficit-to-GDP ratio was comfortably below 3% as required for [eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone) membership, but due to pre-election spending has gone up to 4.4% in 2008 and 3.8% in 2009.[[44]](#cite\_note-autogenerated1-45) ### Energy [(/w/index.php?title=Economy\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=4)] Main article: [Energy in Malta](/wiki/Energy\_in\_Malta)   ---   This article needs to be \*\*updated\*\*. Color (beta) \* Automatic Light Dark This page is always in light mode. From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia This is a \*\*list of cities in Malta\*\*. By the usual standards that most other countries use when designating a [city](/wiki/City), [Malta](/wiki/Malta)'s cities would be too small to be considered such, and in fact Malta is sometimes regarded as a single [city-state](/wiki/City-state).[[1]](#cite\_note-1) Malta's cities are regarded as such because they received the designation of \*città\* at some point during their history. In Maltese law, no distinction is made between cities, towns, and villages; city status is purely honorary and colloquial. Malta is divided into 68 [local councils](/wiki/Local\_councils\_of\_Malta). The local councils which formerly had city status all feature a [mural crown](/wiki/Mural\_crown) on the [crest](/wiki/Crest\_(heraldry)) of their [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat\_of\_arms). The table shows the historical cities:  Historical city  Image  Notes  ---  ---  ---   Città Vittoriosa (\*Victorious City\*)   Capital city from 1530 to 1571, and one of the [Three Cities](/wiki/Three\_Cities\_(Malta)). City status confirmed following the [Great Siege of Malta](/wiki/Great\_Siege\_of\_Malta) in 1565.[[3]](#cite\_note-3)  [Bormla](/wiki/Cospicua)  1722  5,395   Città Notabile (\*Notable City\*)  292   Città Pinto (\*[Pinto](/wiki/Manuel\_Pinto\_da\_Fonseca)'s City\*)   City status granted on 25 May 1743 by Grand Master [Manuel Pinto da Fonseca](/wiki/Manuel\_Pinto\_da\_Fonseca), following a request made by Don Giuseppe Vella.[[5]](#cite\_note-5)  [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,\_Gozo)  1887  6,901   Città Invicta (\*Invincible City\*)   One of the [Three Cities](/wiki/Three\_Cities\_(Malta)).

City status granted following the [Great Siege of Malta](/wiki/Great\_Siege\_of\_Malta) in 1565.[[7]](#cite\_note-7)  [Siġġiewi](/wiki/Si%C4%A1%C4%A1iewi)  1797  8,367   Città Umilissima (\*Humblest City\*)   Capital city since 18 March 1571.[[9]](#cite\_note-9)  [Żabbar](/wiki/%C5%BBabbar)  1797  15,404   Città Rohan (\*[Rohan](/wiki/Emmanuel\_de\_Rohan-Polduc)'s City\*)   City status granted on 21 June 1777 by Grand Master [Emmanuel de Rohan-Polduc](/wiki/Emmanuel\_de\_Rohan-Polduc), following a petition made by the inhabitants. From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia               \*\*Maltese literature\*\* is any literature originating from [Malta](/wiki/Malta) or by [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_People) writers or literature written in the [Maltese language](/wiki/Maltese\_language). This article will give an overview of the history of Maltese-language literature. ## History [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_literature&action=edit&section=1)] ### Written Maltese [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_literature&action=edit&section=2)] As Maltese evolved after [Count Roger I of Sicily](/wiki/Count\_Roger\_I\_of\_Sicily) ended Arab rule on the island at the end of the 11th century, there was little interest in developing a written form of the language. Initially only the [clergy](/wiki/Clergy), [aristocracy](/wiki/Aristocracy) and [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie) were able to read and write and much of their communication was conducted in [Latin](/wiki/Latin). Throughout the centuries use of the [Maltese language](/wiki/Maltese\_language) was often discouraged with varying enthusiasm, ostensibly in the hope that supplanting it would strengthen ties with the country which held possession of Malta at that particular point in time. Under the [Knights of St John](/wiki/Knights\_of\_St\_John) both [Italian](/wiki/Italian\_language) and [French](/wiki/French\_language) were used for official documents and correspondence. During the [British colonial period](/wiki/British\_Empire) the use of [English](/wiki/English\_language) was encouraged through education, with Italian regarded as the next most influential language. It was not until 1936 that Maltese was recognised as the island's official language.

Uniquely, no other European country lacked a standardised written form of its language until the 19th century, when men such as [Mikiel Anton Vassalli](/wiki/Mikiel\_Anton\_Vassalli) made a concerted effort to transcribe spoken Maltese into a written form, although examples of written Maltese exist from as early as the 15th century. #### Diglossia [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_literature&action=edit&section=3)] The development of native Maltese literary works has historically been hampered by the [diglossia](/wiki/Diglossia) that has characterized the culture of Malta throughout its history. For many centuries, Maltese was known as the language "of the kitchen and the workshop", while [Italian](/wiki/Italian\_language) was the language of education, literature, the arts, law and commerce.[[1]](#cite\_note-1) Hence, until the early 20th century, the vast majority of literary works in Malta were written in Italian by the community of [Maltese Italians](/wiki/Maltese\_Italians). In early Maltese history, diglossia manifested itself in the co-existence of a developed form of [Siculo-Arabic](/wiki/Siculo-Arabic) and the language of a series of rulers, most notably Latin, [Greek](/wiki/Greek\_language), [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian\_language), [French](/wiki/French\_language), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish\_language) and Italian. From 1800 onwards these were largely supplanted by English. The Maltese language is today heavily overlaid with [Romance](/wiki/Romance\_languages) and English influences as a result.

According to [Oliver Friggieri](/wiki/Oliver\_Friggieri): > Maltese writers developed an uninterrupted local "Italian" literary movement which went on up to about four decades ago, whereas Maltese as a literary idiom started to coexist on a wide scale in the last decades of the 19th century. Whilst Maltese has the historical priority on the level of the spoken language, Italian has the priority of being the almost exclusive written medium, for the socio-cultural affairs, for the longest period. The native tongue had only to wait for the arrival of a new mentality which could integrate an unwritten, popular tradition with a written, academically respectable one.[[2]](#cite\_note-aboutmalta.com-2) #### The emergence of Maltese literature [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_literature&action=edit&section=4)] The oldest known literary text in the Maltese language is [Pietru Caxaro](/wiki/Pietru\_Caxaro)'s poem, \*[Il-Kantilena](/wiki/Il-Kantilena)\* (c. 1470 to 1485) (also known as \*Xidew il-Qada\*), followed by Gian Francesco Bonamico's sonnet of praise to Grand Master [Nicolas Cotoner](/wiki/Nicolas\_Cotoner), \*Mejju gie' bl'Uard, u Zahar\*\*(The month of May has arrived, with roses and orange blossoms\*), c. 1672. The earliest known Maltese dictionary was written by Francois de Vion Thezan Court (c. 1640). In 1700, an anonymous Gozitan poet wrote \*Jaħasra Mingħajr Ħtija\*\*(Unfortunately Innocent)\*.

A Maltese translation of the [Lord's Prayer](/wiki/Lord%27s\_Prayer) appeared in Johannes Heinrich Maius's work \*Specimen Lingua Punicæ in hodierna Melitensium superstitis\* (1718), demonstrating the formerly wide-held belief that the language had a [Punic](/wiki/Punic) heritage. A collection of religious sermons by a certain Dun Ignazio Saverio Mifsud, published between 1739 and 1746, is regarded as the earliest known example of Maltese prose. An anonymous poem entitled \*Fuqek Nitħaddet Malta\*\*(I am talking about you, Malta)\*, was written c. 1749, regarding the [Conspiracy of the Slaves](/wiki/Conspiracy\_of\_the\_Slaves). A few years later, in 1752, a [catechism](/wiki/Catechism) entitled \*Tagħlim Nisrani ta' Dun Franġisk Wizzino\*\*(Don Francesco Wizzino's Christian Teachings)\* was published in both Maltese and Italian. The occasion of [Carnival](/wiki/Maltese\_Carnival) in 1760 saw the publication of a collection of [burlesque](/wiki/Burlesque) verses under the heading \*Żwieġ la Maltija\*\*(Marriage, in the Maltese Style)\*, by Dun Feliċ Demarco.

A child of the [Romantic](/wiki/Romanticism) movement, Maltese patriot [Mikiel Anton Vassalli](/wiki/Mikiel\_Anton\_Vassalli) (1764–1829) hailed the emergence of literary Maltese as "one of the ancient patrimonies...of the new emerging nation" and saw this nascent trend as: (1) the affirmation of the singular and collective identity, and (2) the cultivation and diffusion of the national speech medium as the most sacred component in the definition of the patria and as the most effective justification both for a dominated community's claim to be a nation and for the subsequent struggle against foreign rulers.[[2]](#cite\_note-aboutmalta.com-2) Between 1798 and 1800, while Malta was under [French occupation](/wiki/French\_occupation\_of\_Malta), a Maltese translation of \*L-Għanja tat-Trijonf tal-Libertà\*\*(Ode to the Triumph of Liberty)\*, by Citizen La Coretterie, Secretary to the French Government Commissioner, was published on the occasion of [Bastille Day](/wiki/Bastille\_Day). The first translation into Maltese of a [biblical](/wiki/Biblical) text, the [Gospel of St. John](/wiki/Gospel\_of\_St.\_John), was published in 1822 (trans. Ġużeppi Marija Cannolo), on the initiative of the Bible Society in Malta. The first Maltese language newspaper, \*l-Arlekkin Jew Kawlata Ingliża u Maltija\*\*(The Harlequin, or a mix of English and Maltese)\* appeared in 1839, and featured the poems \*l-Imħabba u Fantasija\*\*(Love and Fantasy)\* and \*Sunett\*\*(A Sonnett)\*.

The first [epic poem](/wiki/Epic\_poem) in Maltese, \*Il-Ġifen Tork\*\*(The Turkish [Caravel](/wiki/Caravel))\*, by Giovanni Antonio Vassallo, was published in 1842, followed by \*Ħrejjef bil-Malti\*\*(Legends in Maltese)\* and \*Ħrejjef u Ċajt bil-Malti\*\*(Legends and Jokes in Maltese)\* in 1861 and 1863 respectively. The same author published the first history book in the Maltese language entitled \*Storja ta’ Malta Miktuba għall-Poplu\*\*(The People's History of Malta)\* in 1862. 1863 saw the publication of the first [novel](/wiki/Novel) in Maltese, \*Elvira Jew Imħabba ta’ Tirann\*\*(Elvira, or the Love of a Tyrant)\* by the [Neapolitan](/wiki/Naples) author, Giuseppe Folliero de Luna. Anton Manwel Caruana's novel \*Ineż Farruġ\* (1889), the first Maltese novel, was modelled on traditional Italian historical novels, such as [Manzoni](/wiki/Alessandro\_Manzoni)'s \*[The Betrothed](/wiki/The\_Betrothed)\*, and has been the subject of recent scholarly attention. #### Notable Maltese writers [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_literature&action=edit&section=5)] \* [Clare Azzopardi](/wiki/Clare\_Azzopardi) \* [Antoine Cassar](/wiki/Antoine\_Cassar) \* [John A.

Vella](/wiki/Emanuel\_Benjamin\_Vella) \* [Godfrey Wettinger](/wiki/Godfrey\_Wettinger) \* [Trevor Żahra](/wiki/Trevor\_%C5%BBahra) ## Poetry [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_literature&action=edit&section=6)] ### \*Il-Kantilena\* [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_literature&action=edit&section=7)] \*[Il-Kantilena](/wiki/Il-Kantilena)\* is the oldest written text known to exist in [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_language), dating from no later than 1485, the death of [Pietru Caxaro](/wiki/Pietru\_Caxaro), the author it is attributed to. It was not rediscovered until 1966 or 1968 by [Godfrey Wettinger](/wiki/Godfrey\_Wettinger) and Fr. [Mikiel Fsadni](/wiki/Mikiel\_Fsadni) in the Valletta [Notarial Archives](/wiki/Notarial\_Archives). [Frans Sammut](/wiki/Frans\_Sammut) published a revolutionary interpretation of it in 2009. Karmenu Mallia translated it into Esperanto and wrote an article about it in 'Literatura Foiro'. ## Contemporary literature [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_literature&action=edit&section=8)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Ritratt\_f\_sammut\_b%27vassalli\_f%27idu.jpg/250px-Ritratt\_f\_sammut\_b%27vassalli\_f%27idu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ritratt\_f\_sammut\_b%27vassalli\_f%27idu.jpg) Frans Sammut holding a copy of Mikiel Anton Vassalli's collection of Maltese proverbs.

The post-War years saw the emergence of \*[Moviment Qawmien Letterarju](/w/index.php?title=Moviment\_Qawmien\_Letterarju&action=edit&redlink=1)\* (Literary Revival Movement) in 1967, an avant-garde literary movement the protagonists of which included [Oliver Friggieri](/wiki/Oliver\_Friggieri) (later Professor of Maltese at the [University of Malta](/wiki/University\_of\_Malta)), [Frans Sammut](/wiki/Frans\_Sammut) (1945–2011), the "national author", [Alfred Sant](/wiki/Alfred\_Sant) (who was Prime Minister from 1996 to 1998), [Lino Spiteri](/wiki/Lino\_Spiteri) (who was Finance Minister in two Governments),[[6]](#cite\_note-6) and others.[[7]](#cite\_note-7) Among Oliver Friggieri's most important works is the collection of his poems.[[8]](#cite\_note-amazon-8) Frans Sammut's reputation is built on his novels \*Il-Gaġġa\* (on which the film with the same name is based[[9]](#cite\_note-youtube-9)), \*Samuraj\*,[[10]](#cite\_note-adriangrima-10)\*Paceville\*[[11]](#cite\_note-google-11) and \*Il-Ħolma Maltija\*[[12]](#cite\_note-google2-12) (translated in Esperanto as \*La Malta Revo\*). Alfred Sant is known for his theatre collected mostly in his \*Fid-Dell tal-Katidral u Drammi Ohra\*,[[13]](#cite\_note-bdlbooks-13) whereas Lino Spiteri became renowned for his novel \*Rivoluzzjoni Do Minore\*[[14]](#cite\_note-nla-14) and his short stories. The \*[Moviment Qawmien Letterarju](/w/index.php?title=Moviment\_Qawmien\_Letterarju&action=edit&redlink=1)\* scene eventually gave way to a new wave of writers, whose voices have shaped the contemporary literature scene. Foremost amongst these are writers such as [Albert Marshall](/wiki/Albert\_Marshall\_(author)), [Immanuel Mifsud](/wiki/Immanuel\_Mifsud), [Adrian Grima](/w/index.php?title=Adrian\_Grima&action=edit&redlink=1), and [Antoine Cassar](/wiki/Antoine\_Cassar).

The contemporary scene has also seen the emergence of a new generation of writers including [Simone Inguanez](/wiki/Simone\_Inguanez), Nadia Mifsud, Simone Galea, [Elizabeth Grech](/wiki/Elizabeth\_Grech), [Clare Azzopardi](/wiki/Clare\_Azzopardi),[[15]](#cite\_note-transcript-15) Lon Kirkop,[[16]](#cite\_note-16) Kirsten Spiteri and Lara Calleja who won the [European Union Prize for Literature](/wiki/European\_Union\_Prize\_for\_Literature) for her book \*Kissirtu Kullimkien\*.[[17]](#cite\_note-17) Nowadays Maltese literature is dominated by literature in the Maltese language (e.g., [Alex Vella Gera](/w/index.php?title=Alex\_Vella\_Gera&action=edit&redlink=1),[[18]](#cite\_note-sundaycircle-18)[Guze' Stagno](/w/index.php?title=Guze%27\_Stagno&action=edit&redlink=1)[[19]](#cite\_note-19)), some works in English (David Samuel Hudson[[20]](#cite\_note-20) and [Francis Ebejer](/wiki/Francis\_Ebejer)[[21]](#cite\_note-zrakogled-21) for instance), and some works in Italian, such as the poems of Judge [J.J. Cremona](/w/index.php?title=J.J.\_Cremona&action=edit&redlink=1)[[22]](#cite\_note-president-22) and Albert Caruana's ebook \*Il Sindaco di Racalmusci\*.[[23]](#cite\_note-amazon2-23) [Walid Nabhan](/wiki/Walid\_Nabhan) has published two books of short stories in Maltese, and one novel titled \*L-Eżodu taċ-Ċikonji\* (2013) which won the Maltese National Prize for Literature in 2014, and the EU Prize for Literature in 2017. He published a collection of poetry in Maltese in 2014. Nabhan has also translated works of Maltese literature into Arabic.[[24]](#cite\_note-nabhan-24) ## List of books in Maltese [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_literature&action=edit&section=9)] \* \*[Taħt Tliet Saltniet](/wiki/Ta%C4%A7t\_Tliet\_Saltniet)\* \* \*[Żmien L-Ispanjoli](/w/index.php?title=%C5%BBmien\_L-Ispanjoli&action=edit&redlink=1)\* \* \*[Il-Gaġġa](/w/index.php?title=Il-Ga%C4%A1%C4%A1a&action=edit&redlink=1)\* \* \*[Samuraj](/w/index.php?title=Samuraj&action=edit&redlink=1)\* \* \*[Il-Ħolma Maltija](/w/index.php?title=Il-%C4%A6olma\_Maltija&action=edit&redlink=1)\* \* \*[Paceville](/wiki/Paceville)\* \* Perfettament Imperfetta \* [Mitt Elf Isem Ieħor: HappyVeganGirlJules](/wiki/Mitt\_Elf\_Isem\_Ie%C4%A7or:\_HappyVeganGirlJules) \* Kissirtu Kullimkien \* Lucy Min? \* [Il-Fiddien](/w/index.php?title=Il-Fiddien&action=edit&redlink=1) \* Kulħadd Ħalla Isem Warajh \* Rokit \* [L-Eżodu taċ-Ċikonji](https://www.midseabooks.com/shop/language-literature/rumanzi-u-novelli/l-ezodu-tac-cikonji/) \* \*[Naked as Water](/wiki/Naked\_as\_Water)\* ## Notes [(/w/index.php?title=Maltese\_literature&action=edit&section=10)] 1. \*\*[^](#cite\_ref-1)\*\*Attard Bezzina, Lawrence. [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Majestic\_Mdina\_is\_even\_more\_spectacular\_and\_colorful\_with\_the\_Maltese\_feast\_fireworks\_culture\_tradition.jpg/250px-Majestic\_Mdina\_is\_even\_more\_spectacular\_and\_colorful\_with\_the\_Maltese\_feast\_fireworks\_culture\_tradition.jpg)](/wiki/File:Majestic\_Mdina\_is\_even\_more\_spectacular\_and\_colorful\_with\_the\_Maltese\_feast\_fireworks\_culture\_tradition.jpg) Fireworks in [Mdina](/wiki/Mdina) during the Feast of the [Conversion of St.

Paul](/wiki/Conversion\_of\_Paul\_the\_Apostle), an annual cultural celebration [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/G%C4%A7ana\_Musician.jpg/250px-G%C4%A7ana\_Musician.jpg)](/wiki/File:G%C4%A7ana\_Musician.jpg) A traditional [Għana](/wiki/G%C4%A7ana\_(folk\_music)) musician playing an [accordion](/wiki/Accordion) The \*\*culture of Malta\*\* has been influenced by various societies that have come into contact with the [Maltese Islands](/wiki/Maltese\_Islands) throughout the centuries, including neighbouring [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean) cultures, and the cultures of the nations that ruled [Malta](/wiki/Malta) for long periods of time prior to its [independence](/wiki/History\_of\_Malta#Independence) in 1964. ## History [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=1)] Main article: [History of Malta](/wiki/History\_of\_Malta) See also: [Timeline of Maltese history](/wiki/Timeline\_of\_Maltese\_history) ### The culture of prehistoric Malta [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=2)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Malta\_16\_Mnajdra.jpg/250px-Malta\_16\_Mnajdra.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_16\_Mnajdra.jpg) The temple complex of [Mnajdra](/wiki/Mnajdra) (4th mi-3200 BC) The earliest inhabitants of the Maltese Islands are believed to have been [Sicani](/wiki/Sicani) from nearby [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily) who arrived on the island sometime before 5000 BC. They grew cereals and raised domestic livestock and, in keeping with many other ancient Mediterranean cultures, formed a [fertility cult](/wiki/Mother\_goddess) represented in Malta by statuettes of unusually large proportions. Pottery from the earliest period of Maltese civilization (known as the [Għar Dalam phase](/wiki/G%C4%A7ar\_Dalam\_phase)) is similar to examples found in [Agrigento](/wiki/Agrigento), Sicily. These people were either supplanted by, or gave rise to a culture of [megalithic](/wiki/Megalithic) temple builders, whose surviving [monuments on Malta and Gozo](/wiki/Megalithic\_Temples\_of\_Malta) are considered the oldest standing stone structures in the world.[[1]](#cite\_note-1)[[2]](#cite\_note-2)[[3]](#cite\_note-3) The temples date from 4000 to 2500 BC and typically consist of a complex [trefoil](/wiki/Trefoil) design.

Little is known about the temple builders of Malta and Gozo; however, there is some evidence that their rituals included [animal sacrifice](/wiki/Animal\_sacrifice). This culture disappeared from the Maltese Islands around 2500 BC and was replaced by a new influx of [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze\_Age) immigrants, a culture that is known to have [cremated](/wiki/Cremation) its dead and introduced smaller megalithic structures called [dolmens](/wiki/Dolmens) to Malta,[[4]](#cite\_note-4) probably imported by Sicilian population because of the similarity of Maltese dolmens with similar constructions found in the largest island of the Mediterranean sea.[[5]](#cite\_note-5) ### The development of modern Maltese culture [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=3)] The culture of modern Malta has been described as a "rich pattern of traditions, beliefs and practices," which is the result of "a long process of adaptation, assimilation and cross fertilization of beliefs and usages drawn from various conflicting sources." It has been subjected to the same complex, historic processes that gave rise to the linguistic and ethnic admixture that defines who the [people of Malta and Gozo](/wiki/Maltese\_people) are today.[[6]](#cite\_note-6) Present-day Maltese culture is essentially [Latin European](/w/index.php?title=Latin\_European&action=edit&redlink=1) with the recent British legacy also in evidence. In the early part of its history, Malta was also exposed to [Semitic-speaking communities](/wiki/Semitic\_languages). The present-day legacy of this is linguistic rather than cultural.

The Latin European element is the major source of Maltese culture because of the virtually continuous cultural impact on Malta over the past eight centuries and the fact that Malta shares the religious beliefs, traditions and ceremonies of its Sicilian and Southern European neighbors. #### Sources of Semitic influence [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=4)] ##### Phoenicians [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=5)] The [Phoenicians](/wiki/Phoenicia) inhabited the Maltese Islands from around 700 BCE,[[7]](#cite\_note-7) and made extensive use of their sheltered harbours. By 480 BCE, with the ascendancy of [Carthage](/wiki/Carthage) in the western Mediterranean, Malta became a [Punic](/wiki/Punic) colony. Phoenician origins have been suggested for the Maltese people and their customs since 1565.

A genetic study carried out by geneticists [Spencer Wells](/wiki/Spencer\_Wells) and [Pierre Zalloua](/wiki/Pierre\_Zalloua) of the [American University of Beirut](/wiki/American\_University\_of\_Beirut) argued that more than 50% of Y-chromosomes from Maltese men may have Phoenician origins.[[8]](#cite\_note-8)[[9]](#cite\_note-familytreedna.com-9)[[10]](#cite\_note-www.timesofmalta.com\_genetic-origin-of-contemporary-maltese-10) [Algerian](/wiki/Algeria) legend claims that the ancestors of the present Maltese, together with the first Algerians, fled from their original homeland of [Aram](/wiki/Aram\_(Biblical\_region)), with some choosing to settle in Malta and others in North Africa, which would suggest that the prototypical Maltese culture had [Aramaean](/wiki/Aramaean) origins.[[11]](#cite\_note-11) Another tradition suggests that the Maltese are descended from shepherd tribes who fled [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem) in the face of an advancing enemy, set sail from [Jaffa](/wiki/Jaffa), and settled in Malta.[[12]](#cite\_note-12) There is also some evidence that at least one North African tribe, the Oulad Said, claim that they share common ancestry with the Maltese.[[13]](#cite\_note-13) ##### Aghlabid conquest [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=6)] Main article: [Islam in Malta](/wiki/Islam\_in\_Malta) This period coincided with [the golden age of Moorish culture](/wiki/Golden\_Age\_of\_Islam) and included innovations like the introduction of [crop rotation](/wiki/Crop\_rotation) and irrigation systems in [Malta](/wiki/Malta) and [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily), and the cultivation of citrus fruits and mulberries. Then capital city [Mdina](/wiki/Mdina), originally called \*Maleth\* by the [Phoenicians](/wiki/Phoenicians), was at this time refortified, surrounded with a wide moat and separated from its nearest town, [Rabat](/wiki/Rabat,\_Malta). This period of [Arabic influence](/wiki/Arabization)[[14]](#cite\_note-14) followed the conquest of Malta, Sicily and [Southern Italy](/wiki/Southern\_Italy) by the [Aghlabids](/wiki/Aghlabids).

It is presently evident in the names of various Maltese towns and villages and in the [Maltese language](/wiki/Maltese\_language), a genetic descendant of [Siculo-Arabic](/wiki/Siculo-Arabic). It is noted that, during this period, Malta was administered from [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo), [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily), as part of the [Emirate of Sicily](/wiki/Emirate\_of\_Sicily). Genetic studies indicate that the Arabs who colonised Malta in this period were in fact Arabic-speaking Sicilians.,[[9]](#cite\_note-familytreedna.com-9)[[10]](#cite\_note-www.timesofmalta.com\_genetic-origin-of-contemporary-maltese-10) It is difficult to trace a continuous line of cultural development during this time. A proposed theory that the islands were sparsely populated during Fatimid rule is based on a citation in the French translation of the \*[Rawd al-mi'ṭār fī khabar al-aqṭār](/wiki/Kitab\_al-Rawd\_al-Mitar)\* ("The Scented Garden of Information about Places").[[15]](#cite\_note-15) Al-Himyari describes Malta as generally uninhabited and visited by Arabs solely for the purpose of gathering honey and timber and catching fish. No other chronicles make similar descriptions and this claim is not universally accepted.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/e/ef/Majmuna\_stone.jpg/250px-Majmuna\_stone.jpg)](/wiki/File:Majmuna\_stone.jpg) The [Majmuna Stone](/wiki/Majmuna\_Stone), a [Kufic](/wiki/Kufic) gravestone of the girl Maymūnah who died on Thursday, 21 March 1174[[16]](#cite\_note-Xewkija-16) Up to two hundred years after [Count Roger the Norman](/wiki/Roger\_I\_of\_Sicily) conquered the island, differences in the customs and usages of the inhabitants of Malta were distinct from those in other parts of the [Kingdom of Two Sicilies](/wiki/Kingdom\_of\_Two\_Sicilies): \*moribus d'aliis de vivunt d'ipsarum d'insularum de homines et constitutionibus, nostri Sicilie\*.[[17]](#cite\_note-17) The marble gravestone of a [Saracen](/wiki/Saracens) girl named Majmuna (pr. \*My-moona\*), found in a pagan temple in the [Xewkija](/wiki/Xewkija) area of [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo) dates back to 1173.[[16]](#cite\_note-Xewkija-16) Written in [Kufic](/wiki/Kufic), it concludes saying, "You who read this, see that dust covers my eyelids, in my place and in my house, nothing but sadness and weeping; what will my resurrection be like?" The population of Malta at that time amounted to no more than 1,119 households, of whom 836 were described as [Saracens](/wiki/Saracens), inhabiting the island following the Norman invasion and before their ultimate expulsion. ##### Jewish presence [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=7)] Main article: [History of the Jews in Malta](/wiki/History\_of\_the\_Jews\_in\_Malta) A number of Jewish families resided in Malta almost consistently from approximately 1500 BCE[\*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed)\*] to the 1492 [Edict of Expulsion](/wiki/Alhambra\_Decree), and again from the time of the [Knights of Malta](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller) through to the present. This is yet another source of Semitic influence in Maltese culture.

According to local legend,[[18]](#cite\_note-ReferenceA-18) the earliest Jewish residents arrived in Malta some 3,500 years ago, when the seafaring tribes of [Zebulon](/wiki/Zebulon) and [Asher](/wiki/Asher) accompanied the ancient Phoenicians in their voyages across the Mediterranean. The earliest evidence of a Jewish presence on Malta is an inscription in the inner apse of the southern temple of [Ġgantija](/wiki/%C4%A0gantija) (3600–2500 BC)[[19]](#cite\_note-19) in [Xagħra](/wiki/Xag%C4%A7ra), which says, in the Phoenician alphabet: \*"To the love of our Father Jahwe"\*. There is evidence of a Jewish community on Malta during the Roman period, in the form of carved [menorahs](/wiki/Menorah\_(Temple)) in the [catacombs](/wiki/Catacombs) in [Malta](/wiki/Rabat,\_Malta). Members of Malta's Jewish community are known to have risen to the highest ranks of the civil service during the period of Arab occupation, including the rank of [Vizier](/wiki/Vizier). By 1240, according to a report prepared for Emperor [Frederick II](/wiki/Frederick\_II,\_Holy\_Roman\_Emperor), there were 47 Christian and 25 Jewish families on Malta, and 200 Christian and 8 Jewish families on Gozo. Unlike the Jewish experience in the rest of Europe, throughout the Middle Ages the Jews of Malta generally resided among the general population rather than in [ghettos](/wiki/Ghetto), frequently becoming landowners.

The Jewish population of Malta had flourished throughout the period of Norman rule, such that one third of the population of Malta's ancient capital, [Mdina](/wiki/Mdina), is said to have been Jewish.[[20]](#cite\_note-20) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Alhambra\_Decree.jpg/250px-Alhambra\_Decree.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alhambra\_Decree.jpg) Alhambra Decree In 1492, in response to the Alhambra Decree the Royal Council had argued – unsuccessfully – that the expulsion of the Jews would radically reduce the total population of the Maltese Islands, and that Malta should therefore be treated as a special case within the Spanish Empire.[[21]](#cite\_note-Tayar-21) Nonetheless, the decree of expulsion was signed in [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo) on 18 June 1492, giving the Jewish population of Malta and Sicily three months to leave. Numerous forced [conversions](/wiki/Religious\_conversion#Christianity) to Catholicism, or exile, followed. Evidence of these conversions can be found in many Maltese family names that still survive today, such as the families \*Abela\*, \*Ellul\*, \*Salamone\*, \*Mamo\*, \*Cohen\*, and \*Azzopardi\*.[[22]](#cite\_note-22) A much smaller Jewish community developed under the rule of the Knights of Malta, but this consisted primarily of slaves and emancipated slaves. Under the rule of certain Grandmasters of the Order, the Jews were made to reside in Valletta's prisons at night, while by day they remained free to transact business, trade and commerce among the general population.

Local place names around the island, such as \*Bir Meyru\* (Meyer's Well), \*Ġnien il-Lhud\* (The Jew's Garden) and \*Ħal Muxi\* (Moshé's Farm)[[21]](#cite\_note-Tayar-21) attest to the endurance of Jewish presence in [Malta](/wiki/Malta). ##### Slaves in Malta [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=8)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/The\_Asian\_slave\_at\_Nicola\_Cottoner\_monument.jpg/250px-The\_Asian\_slave\_at\_Nicola\_Cottoner\_monument.jpg)](/wiki/File:The\_Asian\_slave\_at\_Nicola\_Cottoner\_monument.jpg) Sculpture of a Muslim slave on the funerary monument of [Nicolas Cotoner](/wiki/Nicolas\_Cotoner) Exposure to Semitic influences continued to a limited extent during the 268-year rule of the [Knights of St. John](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller) over Malta, due in part to trade between the Knights and North Africa, but primarily due to the large numbers of slaves present in Malta during the 17th and 18th centuries: upwards of 2,000 at any given time (or about 5 per cent of the population of Malta), of whom 40–45 per cent were [Moors](/wiki/Moors), and the remainder [Turks](/wiki/Turkish\_people), [Africans](/wiki/African\_diaspora) and Jews. There were so many Jewish slaves in Malta during this time that Malta was frequently mentioned for its large enslaved Jewish population in Jewish literature of the period.[[23]](#cite\_note-Hecht-23) The slaves were engaged in various activities, including construction, shipbuilding and the transportation of Knights and nobles by sedan-chair. They were occasionally permitted to engage in their own trades for their own account, including hairdressing, shoe-making and woodcarving, which would have brought them into close contact with the Maltese urban population.

[Inquisitor](/wiki/Holy\_Inquisition) Federico Borromeo \*(iuniore)\* reported in 1653 that: > [slaves] strolled along the street of [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta) under the pretext of selling merchandise, spreading among the women and simple-minded persons any kind of superstition, charms, love-remedies and other similar vanities.[[24]](#cite\_note-24) A significant number of slaves converted to Christianity, were emancipated, or even adopted by their Maltese patrons which may have further exposed Maltese culture to their customs.[[25]](#cite\_note-25) #### Sources of Latin European influence [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=9)] ##### Roman municipium [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=10)] From 218 BCE to 395 CE, Malta was under Roman political control, initially as a praetorship of [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily). The islands were eventually elevated to the status of Roman [municipium](/wiki/Municipium), with the power to control domestic affairs, mint their own money, and send ambassadors to Rome. It was during this period that [St. Paul](/wiki/Saul\_of\_Tarsus) was shipwrecked on the Maltese Islands and introduced [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity). Few archeological relics survive in Malta today from the Roman period, the sole exception being the Roman Domus, just outside the walls of [Mdina](/wiki/Mdina). From a cultural perspective, the Roman period is notable for the arrival in Malta of several highly placed Roman families, whose progeny form part of the Maltese nation today. These include the [Testaferrata](/w/index.php?title=Testaferrata&action=edit&redlink=1) family (originally, "Capo di Ferro"), today one of Malta's premier noble families.

Whether the origins of Maltese culture can be found in the Eastern Mediterranean or North Africa, the impact on Malta of [Punic](/wiki/Punic) culture is believed to have persisted long after the Island's incorporation into the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman\_Republic) in 218 BCE: > ... at least during the first few centuries of Roman rule, tradition, customs and language were still Punic despite romanization of the place. This is in agreement with what can be read in the [Acts of the Apostles](/wiki/Acts\_of\_the\_Apostles), which call the Maltese "barbarians", that is using a language that was neither [Greek](/wiki/Greek\_language) nor [Latin](/wiki/Latin), but Punic.[[26]](#cite\_note-26) With the division of the Roman Empire, in 395 CE, Malta was given to the eastern portion ruled from [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople) and this new colonization introduced [Greek](/wiki/Greeks) families to the Maltese collective,[[27]](#cite\_note-27) bringing with them various superstitions, proverbs, and traditions that exist within Maltese culture today.[[28]](#cite\_note-28) ##### Catholicism [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=11)] Main article: [Religion in Malta](/wiki/Religion\_in\_Malta) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Taghammarstation12.jpg/250px-Taghammarstation12.jpg)](/wiki/File:Taghammarstation12.jpg) The 12th station on the [Via Crucis](/wiki/Via\_Crucis) of the [Ta' Pinu Basilica](/wiki/Ta%27\_Pinu) in [Għarb](/wiki/G%C4%A7arb), [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo) It is said that in Malta, Gozo, and Comino there are more than 365 churches, or one church for every 1,000 residents (the actual total number of churches is 359).

The parish church ([Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_language): \*parroċċa\* or \*knisja parrokjali\*) is the architectural and geographic focal point of every Maltese town and village, and its main source of civic pride. This civic pride manifests itself in spectacular fashion during the local village \*\*festas\*\*, which mark the feast day of the patron saint of each parish with marching bands, religious processions, special [Masses](/wiki/Holy\_Mass), [fireworks](/wiki/Fireworks) (especially [petards](/wiki/Petard#Variants)), and other festivities. Making allowances for a possible break in the appointment of bishops to Malta during the period of the Fatimid conquest, the Maltese Church is referred to today as the only extant [Apostolic See](/wiki/Apostolic\_See) other than Rome itself. According to tradition and as recorded in the [Acts of the Apostles](/wiki/Acts\_of\_the\_Apostles), the Church in Malta was founded by [St. Paul](/wiki/Paul\_of\_Tarsus) in AD 70, following his shipwreck on these Islands. The earliest Christian place of worship in Malta is said to be the cavern in Rabat Malta, now known as St. Paul's Grotto, where the apostle was imprisoned during his stay on Malta. There is evidence of Christian burials and rituals having taken place in the general vicinity of the Grotto, dating back to the 3rd century AD.

Further evidence of Christian practices and beliefs during the period of Roman persecution can be found in the many [catacombs](/wiki/Catacombs) that lie beneath various parts of Malta, including [St Paul's Catacombs and St Agatha's Catacombs](/wiki/St.\_Paul%27s\_Catacombs) in [Rabat](/wiki/Rabat,\_Malta), just outside the walls of [Mdina](/wiki/Mdina). The latter, in particular, were beautifully frescoed between 1200 and 1480; they were defaced by marauding [Turks](/wiki/Ottoman\_Empire) in the 1550s. There are also a number of cave churches, including the grotto at [Mellieħa](/wiki/Sanctuary\_of\_Our\_Lady\_of\_Mellie%C4%A7a), which is a Shrine of the Nativity of Our Lady where, according to legend, [St. Luke](/wiki/St.\_Luke) painted a picture of the [Madonna](/wiki/The\_Madonna). It has been a place of pilgrimage since [medieval](/wiki/Medieval) times. The writings of classic Maltese historian, Gian. Francesco Abela recount the conversion to Christianity of the Maltese population at the hand of [St. Paul](/wiki/Saul\_of\_Tarsus).[[29]](#cite\_note-29) It is suggested that Abela's writings were used by [Knights of Malta](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller) to demonstrate that Malta had been ordained by God as a "bulwark of Christian, European civilization against the spread of Mediterranean Islam."[[30]](#cite\_note-30) The native Christian community that welcomed [Roger I of Sicily](/wiki/Roger\_I\_of\_Sicily)[[31]](#cite\_note-malticross-31) was further bolstered by immigration to Malta from Italy, in the 12th and 13th centuries.

For centuries, leadership over the Church in Malta was generally provided by the [Diocese of Palermo](/wiki/Diocese\_of\_Palermo), except under [Charles of Anjou](/wiki/Charles\_of\_Anjou) who caused Maltese bishops to be appointed, as did – on rare occasions – the Spanish and later, the Knights. This continued Malta's connections with [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily) and [Italy](/wiki/Italy), and contributed to, from the 15th century to the early 20th century, the dominance of Italian as Malta's primary language of culture and learning. Since 1808 all [bishops of Malta](/wiki/Roman\_Catholic\_Archdiocese\_of\_Malta) have been Maltese. During the [Norman and Spanish periods](/wiki/History\_of\_Malta#Norman\_Kingdom\_of\_Sicily\_rule) and under the rule of the Knights, Malta became the devout Catholic nation it is today. It is worth noting that the Maltese Inquisition (more properly called the [Roman Inquisition](/wiki/Roman\_Inquisition))[[32]](#cite\_note-32) had a very long tenure in Malta following its establishment by the Pope in 1530; the last Inquisitor departed from the Islands in 1798, after the Knights capitulated to the forces of [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon\_Bonaparte). ##### The Normans [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=12)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Roger\_II\_Sicily.jpg/250px-Roger\_II\_Sicily.jpg)](/wiki/File:Roger\_II\_Sicily.jpg) Under Roger II, King of Sicily, Christianity was restored as Malta's principal faith The later years of [Norman rule](/wiki/History\_of\_Malta#Norman\_Kingdom\_of\_Sicily\_rule) over Malta brought massive waves of immigration to the Islands from Sicily and from the Italian mainland, including clergy and notaries.

[Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian\_language) became the sole written language of Malta, as evidenced by notarial deeds from this period, but this was eventually supplanted by [Tuscan Italian](/wiki/Italian\_language), which became the primary literary language and the medium of legal and commercial transactions in Malta. A large number of Sicilian and Italian words were adopted into the local vernacular. Traces of [Siculo-Norman](/wiki/Siculo-Norman) architecture can still be found in Malta's ancient capital of [Mdina](/wiki/Mdina) and in [Vittoriosa](/wiki/Birgu), most notably in the Palaces of the \*[Santa Sofia](/wiki/Palazzo\_Santa\_Sofia)\*, \*Gatto Murina\*, \*Inguanez\* and \*[Falzon](/wiki/Palazzo\_Falson)\* families.[[33]](#cite\_note-33) ##### Spain [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=13)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Street\_in\_Valetta%2C\_Malta.jpeg/250px-Street\_in\_Valetta%2C\_Malta.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Street\_in\_Valetta,\_Malta.jpeg) The wooden balconies and wrought-iron railings of Valletta Traces of the ascendancy of the [Crown of Aragon](/wiki/Crown\_of\_Aragon) in the Mediterranean, and Spanish governance over Malta from 1282 to 1530, are still evident in Maltese culture today. These include culinary, religious, and musical influences. Two examples are the enduring importance of the [Spanish guitar](/wiki/Classical\_guitar) (Maltese: \*il-kitarra Spanjola\*) in Maltese folk music, and the enclosed wooden balconies (Maltese: \*gallerija\*) that grace traditional Maltese homes today. It is also possible that the traditional Maltese costume, the [Faldetta](/wiki/Faldetta), is a local variation of the Spanish [mantilla](/wiki/Mantilla). The Spanish period also saw the establishment of local nobility, with the creation of Malta's oldest extant title, the [Barony of Djar-il-Bniet e Buqana](/w/index.php?title=Barony\_of\_Djar-il-Bniet\_e\_Buqana&action=edit&redlink=1), and numerous others. Under Spanish rule Malta developed into a feudal state.

From time to time during this period, the Islands were nominally ruled by various Counts of Malta, who were typically illegitimate sons of the reigning Aragonese monarch; however, the day-to-day administration of the country was essentially in the hands of the local nobility, through their governing council known as the \*Università\*. Some of Malta's premier noble families including the \*Inguanez\* family, settled in Malta from Spain and Sicily during this time. Other Maltese families of Spanish origin include: \*Alagona\*, \*Aragona\*, \*Abela\*, \*Flores\*, \*Guzman\* and \*Xerri\*. The period of Spanish rule over Malta lasted roughly as long as the period of Arab rule; however, this appears to have had little impact on the language spoken in rural Malta, which remained heavily influenced by [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic\_language), with Semitic [morphemes](/wiki/Morphemes). This is evident in Pietro Caxaro's \*[Il-Kantilena](/wiki/Il-Kantilena)\*, the oldest known literary text in [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_language), which was written prior to 1485, at the height of the Spanish period. ##### The Knights of St. John [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=14)] Main article: [History of Malta under the Order of Saint John](/wiki/History\_of\_Malta\_under\_the\_Order\_of\_Saint\_John) The population of Malta increased considerably during the rule of the [Knights](/wiki/History\_of\_Malta#Knights\_of\_St.\_John), from 25,000 in 1535 to over 40,000 in 1621, to over 54,463 in 1632.

This was primarily due to immigration from Western Europe, but also due to generally improved health and welfare conditions, and the reduced incidence of raids from North African and Turkish [corsairs](/wiki/Barbary\_corsairs). By 1798, when the Knights surrendered Malta to the forces of [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon\_Bonaparte), the population of Malta had increased to 114,000. The period of the Knights is often referred to as Malta's \*\*Golden Age\*\*, as a result of the architectural and artistic embellishment of the Islands by their resident rulers, and as a result of advances in the overall health, education and prosperity of the local population during this period. Music, literature, theatre and the visual arts all flourished in Malta during this period, which also saw the foundation and development of many of the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance) and [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque) towns and villages, palaces and gardens of Malta, the most notable of which is the capital city, [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta). [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/St\_Sebastian\_Curtain.jpg/250px-St\_Sebastian\_Curtain.jpg)](/wiki/File:St\_Sebastian\_Curtain.jpg) The city of [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta), one of several built and fortified by the Knights of Malta Contact between the Maltese and the many Sicilian and Italian mariners and traders who called at Valletta's busy [Grand Harbour](/wiki/Grand\_Harbour) expanded under Knights, while at the same time, a significant number of Western European nobles, clerics and civil servants relocated to Malta during this period.

The wealth and influence of Malta's noble families – many of whom trace their ancestry back to the Norman and Spanish monarchs who ruled Malta prior to the Knights – was also greatly enhanced during this period. Maltese education, in particular, took a significant leap forward under the Knights, with the foundation, in 1592, of the \*[Collegium Melitense](/wiki/Collegium\_Melitense)\*, precursor to today's [University of Malta](/wiki/University\_of\_Malta), through the intercession of Pope [Clement VIII](/wiki/Clement\_VIII). As a result, the University of Malta is one of the oldest extant universities in Europe, and the oldest Commonwealth university outside of the United Kingdom. The \*\*School of Anatomy and Surgery\*\* was established by [Grand Master](/wiki/Grandmaster\_(order)) Fra [Nicolás Cotoner](/wiki/Nicol%C3%A1s\_Cotoner) at the \*[Sacra Infermeria](/wiki/Mediterranean\_Conference\_Centre)\* in Valletta, in 1676. The \*Sacra Infermeria\* itself was known as one of the finest and most advanced hospitals in Europe. ##### Sicily and the Italian mainland [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=15)] Located just 96 km (60 mi) to the north, [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily) has provided Malta with a virtually continuous exchange of knowledge, ideas, customs and beliefs throughout history. Many modern Maltese families trace their origins to various parts of Sicily and Southern Italy. The geographic proximity has facilitated a considerable amount of intermarriage, cross-migration, and trade between the two groups of islands.

It is likely that this was just as true during the period of [Arab domination over Sicily](/wiki/History\_of\_Sicily#Muslim\_period) as it has been since the [Norman conquest of Sicily](/wiki/History\_of\_Sicily#High\_Middle\_Ages) in 1060 CE. Accordingly, it is difficult to determine whether some of the Semitic influences on Maltese culture were originally imported to Malta from North Africa, or from Sicily. The Sicilian influence on Maltese culture is extensive, and is especially evident in the [local cuisine](/wiki/Maltese\_cuisine), with its emphasis on olive oil, pasta, seafood, fresh fruits and vegetables (especially the tomato), traditional appetizers such as caponata (Maltese: \*"kapunata"\*) and rice balls (\*[arancini](/wiki/Arancini)\*), speciality dishes such as rice timbale (Maltese: \*"ross fil-forn"\*), and sweets such as the \*[cassata](/wiki/Cassata)\* and \*[kannoli](/wiki/Cannoli)\*. Sicilian influence is also evident in many of the local [superstitions](/wiki/Superstitions), in simple children's [nursery rhymes](/wiki/Nursery\_rhymes), and in the devotion to certain [saints](/wiki/Saints), especially [St. Agatha](/wiki/St.\_Agatha). Centuries of dependence on the Diocese of Palermo brought many Sicilian religious traditions to Malta, including the Christmas [crib](/wiki/Nativity\_Scene) (Maltese: \*"il-presepju"\*), the ritual visiting of several Altars of Repose on [Good Friday](/wiki/Good\_Friday) (Maltese: \*"is-sepulkri"\*), and the graphic, grim realism of traditional Maltese religious images and sculpture. Despite Malta's rapid transformation into a strategic naval base during the [British period](/wiki/History\_of\_Malta#British\_rule), the influence of [Italian culture](/wiki/Culture\_of\_Italy) on Malta strengthened considerably throughout the 19th century.

This was due in part to increasing levels of literacy among the Maltese, the increased availability of Italian newspapers, and an influx of Italian [intelligentsia](/wiki/Intelligentsia) to Malta. Several leaders of the Italian [risorgimento](/wiki/Risorgimento) movement were exiled in Malta by the [Bourbon monarchs](/wiki/House\_of\_Bourbon#Later\_Bourbon\_monarchs\_outside\_France) during this period, including [Francesco Crispi](/wiki/Francesco\_Crispi), and Ruggiero Settimo. Malta was also the proposed destination of [Giuseppe Garibaldi](/wiki/Giuseppe\_Garibaldi) when he was ordered into exile; however, this never came to pass.
