The political writings of Garibaldi and his colleague, [Giuseppe Mazzini](/wiki/Giuseppe\_Mazzini) – who believed that Malta was, at heart, part of the emerging Italian nation – resonated among many of Malta's upper- and middle-classes. ##### France [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=16)] Main article: [French occupation of Malta](/wiki/French\_occupation\_of\_Malta) [French rule over Malta](/wiki/French\_occupation\_of\_Malta), although brief, left a deep and lasting impression on Maltese culture and society. Several of the [Grand Masters](/wiki/List\_of\_Grand\_Masters\_of\_the\_Knights\_Hospitaller) of the [Knights of Malta](/wiki/Knights\_Hospitaller) had been French, and though some French customs and expressions had crept into common usage in Malta as a result (such as the expressions \*"bonġu"\* for "good day", and \*"bonswa"\* for "good evening", still in use today), Napoleon's garrison had a much deeper impact on Maltese culture. Within six days following the capitulation by Grand Master Hompesch on board \*l'Orient\*, [Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon\_Bonaparte) had given Malta a Constitution and introduced the Republican concept of \*Liberté\*, \*Egalite\*, \*Fraternité\* to Malta.

Slavery was abolished, and the scions of Maltese nobility were ordered to burn their patents and other written evidence of their pedigrees before the \*arbre de la liberté\* that had been hastily erected in St. George's Square, at the centre of [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta). A secondary school system was established, the university system was revised extensively, and a new [Civil Code](/wiki/Civil\_Code) of law was introduced to the legal system of Malta. Under the rule of General Vaubois civil marriages were introduced to Malta, and all non-Maltese clergymen and women were ordered to leave the Islands. A wholesale plundering of the gold, silver and precious art of Maltese churches followed, and several monasteries were forcibly taken from the religious orders. The Maltese were scandalized by the desecration of their churches. A popular uprising culminated with the "defenestration" of Citizen Masson, commandant of the French garrison, and the [summary execution](/wiki/Summary\_execution) of a handful of Maltese patriots, led by [Dun Mikiel Xerri](/wiki/Dun\_Mikiel\_Xerri). With the French blockaded behind the walls of Valletta, a National Assembly of Maltese was formed. Petitions were sent out to the King of the [Two Sicilies](/wiki/Two\_Sicilies), and to [Lord Nelson](/wiki/Horatio\_Nelson,\_1st\_Viscount\_Nelson), soliciting their aid and support. The French garrison capitulated to Nelson in [Grand Harbour](/wiki/Grand\_Harbour), on 5 September 1800.

#### British Malta [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=17)] Main article: [Crown Colony of Malta](/wiki/Crown\_Colony\_of\_Malta) [British rule](/wiki/Crown\_Colony\_of\_Malta), from 1800 to 1964, radically and permanently transformed the language, culture and politics of Malta. Malta's position in the British Empire was unique in that it did not come about by conquest or by colonization, but at the voluntary request of the Maltese people. Britain found in Malta an ancient, Christian culture, strongly influenced by neighbouring Italy and Sicily, and loyal to the Roman Catholic Church. Malta's primary utility to Great Britain lay in its excellent natural harbours and strategic location, and for many decades, Malta was essentially a "fortress colony". Throughout the 19th century, Malta benefited from increased defence spending by Britain, particularly from the development of the dockyards and the harbour facilities. The Crimean War and the opening of the [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez\_Canal) further enhanced Malta's importance as a supply station and as a naval base. Prosperity brought with it a dramatic rise in the population, from 114,000 in 1842, to 124,000 in 1851, 140,000 in 1870, and double that amount by 1914. Malta became increasingly urbanized, with the majority of the population inhabiting the Valletta and the Three Cities. Malta's fortunes waned during times of peace in the early 20th century, and again after World War II, leading to massive waves of emigration.

Although Malta remained heavily dependent on British military spending, successive British governors brought advances in medicine, education, industry and agriculture to Malta. The British legacy in Malta is evident in the widespread use of the English language in Malta today. English was adopted as one of Malta's two official languages in 1936, and it has now firmly replaced Italian as the primary language of tertiary education, business, and commerce in Malta. [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Domen\_i\_Mosta.jpg/250px-Domen\_i\_Mosta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Domen\_i\_Mosta.jpg) Parish Church of Sta. Marija Assunta (Mosta Dome) The British period introduced the [Neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassical\_architecture) style of architecture to Malta, evident in several buildings built during this period, including the parish church of [Sta. Marija Assunta](/wiki/Rotunda\_of\_Mosta) in [Mosta](/wiki/Mosta) and [St Paul's Pro-Cathedral](/wiki/St\_Paul%27s\_Pro-Cathedral,\_Valletta) in Valletta, which has a spire dominating the city's skyline. [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Malta\_22\_Mdina.jpg/250px-Malta\_22\_Mdina.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_22\_Mdina.jpg) Neo-Gothic architecture in Mdina [Neo-Gothic](/wiki/Gothic\_Revival\_architecture) architecture was also introduced to Malta during this period, such as in the Chapel of Santa Maria Addolorata at Malta's main cemetery, and in the Carmelite Church in [Sliema](/wiki/Sliema). Sliema itself, which developed from a sleepy fishing village into a bustling, cosmopolitan town during the British period, once boasted an elegant seafront that was famed for its [Regency](/wiki/Regency\_architecture) style architecture, that was strongly reminiscent of the British seaside town of [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton).

##### Impact of World War II [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=18)] Perhaps as an indirect result of the brutal devastation suffered by the Maltese at the hands of [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito\_Mussolini)'s Regia Aeronautica and the [Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe) during [World War II](/wiki/History\_of\_Malta#World\_War\_II), the United Kingdom has replaced neighboring [Italy](/wiki/Italy) as the dominant source of cultural influences on modern Malta. The [George Cross](/wiki/George\_Cross) was awarded to the people of Malta by King [George VI of the United Kingdom](/wiki/George\_VI\_of\_the\_United\_Kingdom) in a letter dated 15 April 1942[[34]](#cite\_note-34) to the island's Governor Lieutenant-General Sir [William Dobbie](/wiki/William\_Dobbie), so as to "bear witness to the heroism and devotion of its people"[[35]](#cite\_note-35) during the [great siege](/wiki/Siege\_of\_Malta\_(World\_War\_II)) it underwent in the early parts of World War II. The George Cross is woven into the [flag of Malta](/wiki/Flag\_of\_Malta) and can be seen wherever the flag is flown. The "culture clash" between pro-British and pro-Italian elements in Malta reached its apex in February 1942, when British Governor [Lieutenant-General Sir William Dobbie](/wiki/William\_Dobbie) ordered the deportation of 47 notable Maltese, including [Enrico Mizzi](/wiki/Enrico\_Mizzi), leader of the [Nationalist Party](/wiki/Nationalist\_Party\_(Malta)), and [Sir Arturo Mercieca](/wiki/Arturo\_Mercieca), Chief Justice of Malta, who were suspected by the Colonial authorities of being sympathetic to the fascist cause. Their exile in [Uganda](/wiki/Uganda), which lasted until 8 March 1945, was a source of controversy among the Maltese.

##### British traditions in modern Malta [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=19)] British traditions that live on in Malta include an efficient civil service, a [military](/wiki/Armed\_Forces\_of\_Malta) that is based on the British model, a [Westminster-style](/wiki/Westminster\_System) parliamentary structure, a governmental structure premised on the [rule of law](/wiki/Rule\_of\_law), and a legal system based on [common law](/wiki/Common\_law). Another British legacy in Malta is the widely popular annual Christmas [pantomime](/wiki/Pantomime) at the [Manoel Theatre](/wiki/Manoel\_Theatre). Most Maltese families have adopted [turkey](/wiki/Turkey\_(bird)) and [plum pudding](/wiki/Christmas\_pudding) as Christmas treats in place of the more traditional Maltese [rooster](/wiki/Rooster) and [cassata](/wiki/Cassata). Due to Malta forming a part of the British Empire in the 19th and 20th centuries, and a considerable amount of intermarriage having taken place during that time period, the existence of British or Irish surnames is increasingly common. Examples include: \*Alden\*, \*Atkins\*, \*Crockford\*, \*Ferry\*, \*Gingell\*, \*Hall\*, \*Hamilton\*, \*Harmsworth\*, \*Harwood\*, \*Jones\*, \*Mattocks\*, \*Moore\*, \*O'Neill\*, \*Sladden\*, \*Sixsmith\*, \*Smith\*, \*Strickland\*, \*St. John\*, \*Turner\*, \*Wallbank\*, \*Warrington\*, \*Kingswell\* and \*Woods\*.[[36]](#cite\_note-36) ### Contemporary culture of Malta [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=20)] #### Emigration and immigration [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=21)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Malta\_-mix-\_2019\_by-RaBoe\_067.jpg/250px-Malta\_-mix-\_2019\_by-RaBoe\_067.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_-mix-\_2019\_by-RaBoe\_067.jpg) Child Migrants' Memorial at the [Valletta Waterfront](/wiki/Valletta\_Waterfront), commemorating the 310 Maltese child migrants who travelled to Australia between 1950 and 1965.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Valletta\_European\_Capital\_of\_Culture.png/250px-Valletta\_European\_Capital\_of\_Culture.png)](/wiki/File:Valletta\_European\_Capital\_of\_Culture.png) Valletta served as the [European Capital of Culture](/wiki/European\_Capital\_of\_Culture) in 2018 together with [Leeuwarden](/wiki/Leeuwarden) Main articles: [Emigration from Malta](/wiki/Emigration\_from\_Malta) and [Immigration to Malta](/wiki/Immigration\_to\_Malta) Malta has always been a maritime nation, and for centuries, there has been extensive interaction between Maltese sailors and fishermen and their counterparts around the Mediterranean and into the Atlantic Ocean. More significantly, by the mid-19th century the Maltese already had a long history of migration to various places, including [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt), [Tripolitania](/wiki/Tripolitania), Tunisia, [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria), Cyprus, the [Ionian Islands](/wiki/Ionian\_Islands), Greece, [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily) and [Lampedusa](/wiki/Lampedusa). Intermarriage with other nationals (especially Italians and Sicilians) was not uncommon. Migrants would periodically return to Malta, bringing with them new customs and traditions that over time have been absorbed into mainstream Maltese culture. There was heavy migration from Malta in the early 20th century, and again after World War II until the early 1980s; however the destinations of choice during this period tended to be more distant, English-speaking countries rather than the traditional, Mediterranean [littoral](/wiki/Littoral).

Over 10,000 Maltese settled in Australia, Canada, the United Kingdom and the United States between 1918 and 1920, followed by another 90,000 – or 30 percent of the population of Malta – between 1948 and 1967.[[37]](#cite\_note-37) By 1996, the net emigration from Malta during the 20th century exceeded 120,000, or 33.5% of the population of Malta.[[38]](#cite\_note-38) Emigration dropped dramatically after the mid-1970s and has since ceased to be a social phenomenon of significance. However, since Malta joined the EU in 2004 [expatriate](/wiki/Expatriate) communities emerged in a number of European countries particularly in Belgium and [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg). ### Education [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=22)] Main article: [Education in Malta](/wiki/Education\_in\_Malta) Education is compulsory between the ages of 5 and 16 years. While the state provides education free of charge, the [Church](/wiki/Roman\_Catholic\_Church) and the private sector run a number of schools in Malta and [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo). Most of the teachers' salary in Church schools is paid by the state. Education in Malta is based on the [British Model](/wiki/Education\_in\_the\_United\_Kingdom). ### Religion [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=23)] Main article: [Religion in Malta](/wiki/Religion\_in\_Malta) Today, the Constitution of Malta provides for [freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom\_of\_religion) but establishes Roman Catholicism as the [state religion](/wiki/State\_religion). [Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom\_House) and the \*[World Factbook](/wiki/World\_Factbook)\* report that 98 percent of the Maltese profess Roman Catholicism as their religion, making Malta one of the most Catholic countries in the world.

However, the [[2]](http://www.maltachurch.org.mt/Press/02006/SUNDAY\_MASS\_CENSUS\_-\_%20FINDINGS.pdf)[\*[permanent dead link](/wiki/Wikipedia:Link\_rot)\*] commissioned by the Church of Malta reports that, as of 2005, only 52.6% of the population attended religious services on a regular basis. ### Languages [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=24)] Main article: [Maltese language](/wiki/Maltese\_language) The national language of Malta is [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese\_language), the only official [Semitic language](/wiki/Semitic\_languages) within the [European Union](/wiki/European\_Union). The [Maltese alphabet](/wiki/Maltese\_alphabet) is based on the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin\_alphabet), but uses the [diacritically](/wiki/Diacritic) altered letters \*[ż](/wiki/%C5%BB)\*, also found in [Polish](/wiki/Polish\_language), as well as the letters \*[ċ](/wiki/%C4%8A)\*, \*[ġ](/wiki/%C4%A0)\* and \*[ħ](/wiki/%C4%A6)\*. The official languages are Maltese and English. [Maltese English](/wiki/Maltese\_English) is the local dialect of English. Italian, French and German are also widely spoken and taught in secondary schools, though the latter two less so. ### Telecommunications [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=25)] Main article: [Communications in Malta](/wiki/Communications\_in\_Malta) Radio shows, television programs and the easy availability of foreign newspapers and magazines throughout the 20th century further extended and enhanced the impact of both [British](/wiki/Culture\_of\_the\_United\_Kingdom) and [Italian culture](/wiki/Italian\_culture) on Malta. Globalization and increased Internet usage (approx.

78.1% of the population of Malta as of September 2005) is having a significant effect on Maltese culture; as of 22 December 2006, Malta had the fourth highest rate of Internet usage in the world.[[39]](#cite\_note-39) ### Nightlife in Malta [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=26)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Malta\_-\_St.\_Julian%27s\_-\_Triq\_il-Wilga-Triq\_San\_Gorg\_-\_Paceville\_Piazza\_01\_ies.jpg/250px-Malta\_-\_St.\_Julian%27s\_-\_Triq\_il-Wilga-Triq\_San\_Gorg\_-\_Paceville\_Piazza\_01\_ies.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malta\_-\_St.\_Julian%27s\_-\_Triq\_il-Wilga-Triq\_San\_Gorg\_-\_Paceville\_Piazza\_01\_ies.jpg) [Paceville](/wiki/Paceville) main square The long, warm summer nights of Malta lend themselves to a vibrant nightlife, which is at odds with Malta's traditional conservatism and the staunch Catholicism of older generations. \*Clubbing\* and \*pub-crawling\* – especially in the traffic-free zones of [Paceville](/wiki/Paceville) near [St. Julian's](/wiki/St.\_Julian%27s), and [Buġibba](/wiki/Bu%C4%A1ibba) – is a rite of passage for Maltese teenagers, young adults and crowds of tourists. Evenings start late and many clubbers continue the festivities into the early hours of the morning. Clubs frequently have large outdoor patios, with local and visiting DJs spinning a mix of Euro-beat, House, chill-out, R&B, hardcore, rock, trance, techno, retro, old school, and classic disco. Pubs, especially Irish pubs, are often the meeting place of choice for the start of a night of clubbing. Laid back \*wine bars\* are increasingly popular among young professionals and the more discriminating tourists, and are popping up in the \*kantinas\* of some of the more picturesque, historic cities and towns, including [Valletta](/wiki/Valletta) and [Vittoriosa](/wiki/Birgu).

They typically offer a mix of local and foreign wines, traditional Maltese appetizer platters, and occasionally, live entertainment. Despite rapidly increasing tolerance and acceptance of alternative lifestyles, Malta offers its gay and lesbian locals and visitors less nightlife options than other Southern European destinations. With the exception of several staple bars (including \*Tom's\*, Valletta and \*Klozett\*, Paceville), \*gay bars\* in Malta have a tendency to pop up, relocate, and disappear from one summer season to the next. However, the local gay population is usually very much in evidence – and welcome – at the mainstream clubs of Paceville and elsewhere. ### Transportation [(/w/index.php?title=Culture\_of\_Malta&action=edit&section=27)] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/FBY749.jpg/250px-FBY749.jpg)](/wiki/File:FBY749.jpg) A traditional [Ma

chunks.page_title: Visit Malta - The Official Tourism Site for Malta, Gozo and Comino
chunks.chunk_text: Visit Malta is the official tourism website for Malta, Gozo, and Comino. The destination offers 3000 hours of sunshine per year, making it an ideal year-round destination. Main Experience Categories include Experience the sea which covers Mediterranean water activities, Experience taste which covers Gastronomy and local food, Experience Our Identity which covers Culture and traditions, and Experience Gozo which covers Island of Gozo tourism. This comprehensive portal provides visitors with all the information needed to plan their perfect Maltese holiday, from booking tickets to discovering new places to visit and finding amazing things to do.

chunks.page_title: Visit Malta - The Official Tourism Site for Malta, Gozo and Comino
chunks.chunk_text: Things To Do in Malta include Swimming Spots which feature beaches and crystal-clear blue Mediterranean water, Gastronomy which features outstanding restaurants and spectacular local produce, Nightlife which features festivals, theatre, clubs, and bars, Diving which features over 120 captivating dive sites with great visibility. Additional activities include Activities and Sports featuring sun, sea, and active pursuits, History covering from prehistoric temples to cities built by knights, Culture featuring Feasts, fireworks, and Maltese traditions, and Short Breaks ideal for visitors with limited time. Special Interest Sections include For the Family, Film tourism featuring famous movie locations like Game of Thrones, Gladiator, and Troy, Studying in Malta, 359 churches across the islands, Weddings, and Sustainable Malta options.

Malta is packed with attractions and places of interest, with 7000 years of history offering endless sites to explore. Historical Sites include Valletta which is the Capital City featuring regular plays and concerts, numerous exhibitions and street events, Grand Master's Palace which is an impressive historic palace, and St. John's Co-cathedral which houses Caravaggio's masterpiece Beheading of St. John. Fortifications and Towers include Fort St. Elmo which is Home to the War Museum with Sunday military parade in period costumes re-enactment. Mdina and Rabat features the fortified medieval city known as the Silent City, the Cathedral of Mdina with its museum, Palazzo Falson which is a medieval palace with antique collection, and Fontanella Tea rooms famous for chocolate cake with spectacular bastion views.

Museums and Galleries in Malta include the Museum of Archaeology in Valletta with an exceptionally rich collection of prehistoric artefacts, War Museum at Fort St. Elmo, National Museum of Fine Arts, Museum of Roman Antiquities in Rabat, and St. John's Cavalier Art Centre. Family Attractions include Popeye Village which is the original movie set where the 1979 film starring Robin Williams was filmed. Markets and Local Culture feature Marsaxlokk which is a fishing village with open-air market on Sundays, It-Tokk Market in Victoria, Gozo on Sundays, and open-air market outside Valletta. The island offers diverse attraction categories including Museums and Galleries, Archaeological Sites, Beaches and Bays, Activity and Fun venues, Religious Sites, Fortifications and Towers, Natural Attractions, Local Crafts, and UNESCO World Heritage sites.

Gozo is Malta's sister island and is definitely worth a visit, either as a stand-alone destination or for a day trip using one of the ferry-boats. The island offers various Gozo-related content including Things To Do in Malta and Gozo, Gozo and Comino main section, Swimming Spots featuring beaches and crystal-clear Mediterranean water, Diving with over 120 captivating dive sites with great visibility, Churches, Activities and Sports, and For the Family. Gozo is particularly suitable for Short Breaks and offers Sustainable Holidays options for eco-conscious travelers. The island is known for its unique character, beautiful beaches, and rich history compared to the main island of Malta.

Malta is consistently voted one of the best diving destinations in the World, thanks to its fantastically favourable climate and astonishing array of underwater sites. Diving Conditions feature crystal clear waters, wide choice of shore and boat dives at varying depths, suitable for divers of all abilities from beginners to advanced. The minimum age for diving qualifications is 10 years old, making it an ideal destination for family holidays. Dive Types Available include Shore Dives accessible directly from the shore, Boat Dives which are boat-based diving excursions, and Heritage Malta Wreck Sites featuring historical wreck diving locations. With over 120 captivating dive sites and great visibility, Malta offers unforgettable underwater experiences.

The Maltese Islands are described as one big heritage park with open-air sites and indoor museums covering every historical era from Prehistory to World War II. The Knights of St. John were great patrons of the arts and during their 250 years rule left a legacy of masterpieces found in museums, palaces and churches across the Islands. Visitors will find magnificent Baroque architecture and sacred art treasures. The islands have long inspired artists, photographers and sculptors, both local and foreign. Notable galleries include Spazju Kreattiv at St. James Cavalier in Valletta showcasing contemporary work, and the National Museum of Archaeology holding regular exhibitions of art, sculpture and ceramics. Museums listed include 111 Art Gallery in Ta' Xbiex, ART BOX Gallery of Modern Art in Qala Gozo, Arthall in Victoria Gozo, Blitz in Valletta, Casa Bernard in Rabat, and Casa Rocca Piccola in Valletta.

Malta has beaches for everyone, from windsurfers to sunbathers. The islands offer diverse beach types including golden sand, red sand, rocks, blue lagoons, and even inland seas. Some beaches and rocky shores are off the beaten track but worth seeking out for their seclusion. With Malta's climate, beach life lasts well into October. Popular Northern Malta Beaches include Mellieħa Bay which is the largest and most popular beach in Malta, Għajn Tuffieħa which is a popular beach with golden sand, and Golden Bay which is a major sandy beach in northern Malta. Paradise Bay is at the tip of Malta overlooking Gozo, while Armier Bay is at the extreme northern fringe facing Comino and Gozo islands. These northern beaches offer stunning views and excellent swimming opportunities.

Gozo Beaches include Ramla l-Ħamra which is the most beautiful beach in Gozo, featuring a large beach of unusual red sand nestling by countryside. Daħlet Qorrot is a tiny picturesque fishing cove and tranquil isolated spot on the north-east coast below Nadur and Qala. Dwejra Bay is the archipelago's most spectacular natural landmark featuring the Inland Sea, Fungus Rock, sheer cliffs, and rocky coastline. Comino features the Blue Lagoon with sheltered dazzlingly blue waters that has appeared on screen in Madonna's Swept Away and the mini-series Helen of Troy. Water Sports available include Windsurfing, Jet skiing, Water skiing, Parakiting, Fun rides, and Snorkelling, particularly in Gozo and Comino.

chunks.page_title: UNESCO World Heritage Sites in Malta
chunks.chunk_text: The Maltese Islands have three sites inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List. First, the Megalithic Temples: in total seven megalithic temples are found on the islands of Malta and Gozo, representing a unique architectural tradition that flourished between 3600 and 2500 B.C. On Gozo, the Ġgantija Temples are the oldest free-standing monuments in the world, built at least 1000 years before the famous Egyptian pyramids of Giza. On Malta, the temples include Ħaġar Qim and Mnajdra which are unique architectural masterpieces, Tarxien, Ta' Ħagrat, and Skorba. Second, the Ħal Saflieni Hypogeum in Paola is a rock-cut underground complex used as both a sanctuary and burial site, discovered in 1902 with three underground levels dating from around 3600 to 2400 B.C.

chunks.page_title: UNESCO World Heritage Sites in Malta
chunks.chunk_text: Third, the City of Valletta is a fortified capital city of Malta inextricably linked to the history of the military and charitable Order of St John of Jerusalem. Built after the Great Siege of 1565 and named after Grandmaster Jean Parisot de la Valette, it contains hundreds of monuments all within a relatively small space, making it one of the most concentrated historic areas in the world. The city features impressive architecture from the Knights period, numerous churches, museums, and cultural attractions. Valletta serves as the cultural heart of Malta and hosts regular events, concerts, and exhibitions throughout the year.

Malta offers a rich gastronomic experience deeply rooted in its cultural heritage. The islands boast outstanding produce, fresh seafood from the surrounding sea, and recipes dating back centuries. Food and drink are considered the very essence of Maltese culture. Dining Experiences include Quick snacks for casual eating, Michelin-starred restaurants for fine dining experiences, and Hearty local cuisine for traditional Maltese fare. Cuisine and Food Categories include MICHELIN Guide for award-winning restaurants, Wineries and Vineyards for local wine production, Craft Beer and Breweries for the local beer scene, Our Cuisine and Produce for traditional dishes and local ingredients, and Our Delicacies for specialty food items.

Transportation information from Visit Malta covers transportation to and around the Maltese Islands. The three main transportation categories are Getting to Malta covering information about reaching Malta by air and sea routes, Getting Around Malta covering local transportation options within Malta, and Airport Transfers covering transfer options from Malta International Airport. Getting to Malta typically involves flights to Malta International Airport or ferry services from Sicily. Once in Malta, visitors can use public buses, ferries to Gozo and Comino, taxis, and car rentals to navigate the islands. The transportation infrastructure makes it easy to explore all the attractions Malta has to offer.

The Maltese Islands host some of the region's coolest and most popular events. Visitors are advised to get tickets early and in time for major events. Ticket Availability includes tickets available through the VisitMalta website. Event Categories allow users to filter events by Date Range, Category, Most Popular, Show Offers, and Sort options. Related Event Categories include Nightlife featuring festivals, theatre, clubs, and bars, Culture featuring Maltese cultural events, and Gastronomy featuring food and drink events. The Maltese Islands have a vibrant events calendar throughout the year, from religious festivals to music concerts and cultural celebrations.

Malta offers diverse accommodation options across the main island and Gozo. Visitors can choose from established international brands, charming local hotels, cozy guesthouses, and stylish boutique properties. Accommodation Types Available include International Hotels which are established global hotel brands, Local Hotels which are charming locally-owned hotels, Guesthouses which are cozy accommodations with local character, Boutique Properties which are stylish boutique hotels, Apartments which are self-catering options available, and Villas which are private villa rentals available. Whether you're looking for luxury resorts or budget-friendly options, Malta has accommodation to suit all preferences and budgets.

The Maltese Islands contain an exceptional number of archaeological sites, setting their history apart from other Mediterranean destinations. The archipelago was home to a unique temple-building civilization for three millennia, from around 5200 B.C. Malta and Gozo's temples are thought to be the oldest free-standing buildings known to man. Key Site Types include UNESCO World Heritage Sites, Megalithic monuments, Bronze Age dolmens, Punic tombs, Roman Villa remains, and Prehistoric traces including mysterious cart tracks. Recommended Starting Points are the National Museum of Archaeology in Valletta and the Hypogeum itself. The archaeological heritage spans thousands of years, from prehistoric temple builders through to Roman and later periods.

Individual archaeological sites include Ġgantija Temples in Xagħra Gozo which is one of the most important archaeological sites in the world, dating from around 3600 to 3200 BC. Due to the gigantic dimensions of the megaliths, locals believed that the temples were the work of giants. The temple complex is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and is managed by Heritage Malta. Visitors can explore the impressive stone structures and learn about the prehistoric temple-building civilization that once thrived on the islands. The site provides remarkable insights into Malta's ancient past and is a must-visit for history enthusiasts.

Ħaġar Qim in Qrendi Malta dates from c. 3600-3200 BC and stands on a hilltop overlooking the sea and the islet of Filfla. Ħal Saflieni Hypogeum in Paola is a unique monument and superb example of architecture in the negative, a labyrinth of underground chambers probably used as both a burial site and a temple. Excavation has yielded pottery, human bones, personal ornaments such as beads and amulets, little carved animals and larger figurines. Borġ in-Nadur Temples in Birżebbuġa reveals a four-apse temple c.2000 BC and a fortified Bronze Age domestic settlement. All sites are managed by Heritage Malta and are open to the public.

Buġibba Ancient Temples are preserved in the grounds of a hotel and belong to the same category of pre-historic monuments as Tarxien and Ħaġar Qim. Clapham Junction in Buskett at the limits of Ħad-Dingli features the most impressive and dense concentration of cart ruts on scrubland south of Buskett Gardens. These mysterious cart tracks defy explanation and are a unique feature of Malta's prehistoric landscape. The archaeological sites collectively represent over 7000 years of human history, from the earliest temple builders through various periods of occupation including Phoenician, Roman, Arab, Norman, and the Knights of St. John.

Essential Information for visitors to the Maltese Islands covers Travel and Entry Requirements including Travel Visas and Embassies and Consulates in Malta. Health and Safety information includes Emergency Numbers and Hospitals in Malta and general Safety in Malta. Financial Information covers Currency and Banks. Practical Information includes Weather and Climate, Geography and Time Zone, Language, Public Holidays, Electricity specifications, Driving regulations in Malta, and Luggage Storage Facilities. Living and Working sections cover Working in Malta and Moving and Living in Malta for those considering longer stays.

The Maps and Guides page on Visit Malta provides detailed maps and guides to help visitors plan their itineraries across the Maltese Islands. The resources cover Valletta, Mdina, and other important localities, helping tourists discover venues and attractions in these areas. Available Resources include Maps featuring detailed maps of key destinations including Valletta and Mdina, Brochures, VisitMalta+ App available for download on Google Play Store and Apple App Store, and Discover our Walks featuring a dedicated section for hiking and walking tours linking to hikes and walks across Malta and Gozo.

Tourist information offices and centers in Malta and Gozo are accessible through the Main Menu under Travel Information to Tourist Info Centres. Related Travel Information Sections include Accessibility information, Transportation options, Who Can Help, Live Malta Cams for real-time views, Essential Information, Maps and Guides, and Malta FAQ. Additional Resources include VisitMalta+ App available for download on Google Play and Apple App Store, Social Media platforms including Facebook, Instagram, X, and YouTube, and Newsletter subscription for updates on events and attractions.

Mdina known as the Silent City is a fortified medieval city that offers nighttime visits with lamp-lit streets for romantic strolls. The city features the Cathedral of Mdina with its museum, Palazzo Falson which is a medieval palace in Mdina with antique collection, and Fontanella Tea rooms famous for chocolate cake with spectacular bastion views. Valletta the capital city features regular plays and concerts, numerous exhibitions and street events throughout the year. The city is home to Grand Master's Palace which is an impressive historic palace, and St. John's Co-cathedral which houses Caravaggio's masterpiece Beheading of St. John, one of the most important artworks in the world.

Individual beach details include Anchor Bay at the limits of Mellieħa known for Popeye Village, the original movie set from the 1979 film. Balluta Bay features a long stretch of rocky coastline from Qui-si-Sana in Sliema to St. Julian's, popular for evening walks. Birżebbuġa is a small seaside resort near Marsaxlokk with Pretty Bay sandy beach. Buġibba is Malta's largest seaside resort town with promenade stretching from Salina Bay to St. Paul's Bay. These coastal areas offer various amenities including cafes, restaurants, and water sports facilities. The beach season in Malta typically lasts well into October thanks to the favorable climate.

chunks.page_title: Visit Malta - The Official Tourism Site for Malta, Gozo and Comino
chunks.chunk_text: Special Interest Sections on Visit Malta include For the Family with activities, beaches, and fun for everyone. Film tourism features famous movie locations including Game of Thrones, Gladiator, and Troy, with many tours available to visit these iconic filming sites. Studying in Malta offers learning opportunities under the sun with various educational institutions. The island boasts 359 churches around Malta, almost enough to visit one per day for a year. Weddings section covers splendid venues for tying the knot. Sustainable Malta covers nature parks and sustainable local produce for eco-conscious travelers.

Featured galleries in Valletta include Spazju Kreattiv at St. James Cavalier showcasing contemporary work, and the National Museum of Archaeology holding regular exhibitions of art, sculpture and ceramics. The Museum of Archaeology in Valletta has an exceptionally rich collection of prehistoric artefacts dating back to the temple period. Other notable museums include Casa Rocca Piccola in Valletta which is the only privately owned palace open to the public, offering a fascinating glimpse into noble family life. 111 Art Gallery in Ta' Xbiex offers modern art curation and artist representation services.

Gozo museums and galleries include ART BOX Gallery of Modern Art located in Qala Gozo at 39 Charming B and B dar ta' Zeppi. Arthall is situated at No. 8 Triq Agius de Soldanis in the heart of Victoria, Gozo. These galleries showcase works by excellent contemporary artists and provide insights into the local art scene. The Knights of St. John were great patrons of the arts and during their 250 years rule left a legacy of masterpieces found in museums, palaces and churches across the Islands. Visitors will find magnificent Baroque architecture and sacred art treasures throughout the islands.

Nightlife in Malta features festivals, theatre, clubs, and bars with options for every taste. Maltese cultural events showcase the islands' rich traditions, including religious festivals, fireworks displays, and village feasts. Food and drink events allow visitors to experience Maltese gastronomy through dedicated festivals and tastings. The Malta Tourism Authority notes that it assumes no liability for any third-party activities featured on its website and is not responsible for any acts or omissions of listed parties. Visitors are encouraged to book through reputable providers and check event details carefully.

Gozo offers a distinctive travel experience different from the main island of Malta. The page provides links to various Gozo-related content including Things To Do in Malta and Gozo, Gozo and Comino main section for combined island visits, Swimming Spots featuring beaches and crystal-clear Mediterranean water, Diving with over 120 captivating dive sites with great visibility, Churches for religious and cultural exploration, Activities and Sports for active travelers, and For the Family. Gozo is particularly suitable for Short Breaks due to its compact size and offers Sustainable Holidays options for eco-conscious travelers seeking responsible tourism experiences.

Comino features the Blue Lagoon with sheltered dazzlingly blue waters that has appeared on screen in famous productions including Madonna's Swept Away and the mini-series Helen of Troy. The tiny island of Comino is a popular day trip destination from both Malta and Gozo, accessible by ferry. The Blue Lagoon offers excellent swimming opportunities with its crystal-clear waters and sheltered bay. Visitors can also explore the surrounding coastline and enjoy snorkeling in the pristine waters. Comino is essentially car-free, making it a peaceful retreat for those seeking to escape the busier mainland areas.

Fort St. Elmo is a major fortification in Valletta that houses the War Museum with Sunday military parade in period costumes re-enactment. This historic fort played a crucial role in Malta's military history, particularly during the Great Siege of 1565. Visitors can explore the fort's architecture and learn about Malta's strategic importance in Mediterranean naval history. The War Museum displays artifacts from various periods of Malta's military history including World War II. Fort St. Elmo is one of several fortifications and towers that dot the Maltese coastline, reflecting the islands' long history of strategic military significance.

chunks.page_title: UNESCO World Heritage Sites in Malta
chunks.chunk_text: The Megalithic Temples of Malta and Gozo represent a unique architectural tradition that flourished between 3600 and 2500 B.C. The temples include Ħaġar Qim, Mnajdra, and Tarxien on Malta, and Ġgantija on Gozo. These temples were inscribed on the World Heritage List as a group and represent the oldest free-standing monuments in the world, built at least 1000 years before the famous Egyptian pyramids of Giza. Ta' Ħagrat and Skorba show how the tradition of temple-building was handed down through generations. The temples were built using massive stone blocks and demonstrate remarkable engineering skills for their time.

Maltese cuisine features outstanding produce, fresh seafood from the surrounding sea, and recipes dating back centuries. Food and drink are considered the very essence of Maltese culture. Traditional dishes reflect the islands' Mediterranean location and historical influences from various civilizations. Fresh seafood is a staple due to Malta's maritime location, with local restaurants offering daily catches. The MICHELIN Guide in Malta features award-winning restaurants for fine dining experiences, while local eateries provide hearty traditional Maltese fare. Wineries and vineyards showcase local wine production, and craft beer and breweries represent the growing local beer scene.

Weather and Climate in Malta features Mediterranean weather with hot dry summers and mild winters. Geography positions Malta in the center of the Mediterranean. Time Zone is Central European Time. The official languages include Maltese and English. Public Holidays include various religious and national celebrations throughout the year. Electricity is 230V with UK-style three-pin plugs. Driving is on the left side of the road. These practical details help visitors prepare for their Maltese holiday and ensure a smooth travel experience.

Diving in Malta is suitable for divers of all abilities from beginners to advanced. The possibility for anyone over the age of 10 to attain recognized diving qualifications makes it an ideal destination for family holidays. Malta is consistently voted one of the best diving destinations in the World thanks to its fantastically favourable climate and astonishing array of underwater sites. With crystal clear waters and excellent visibility, divers can explore underwater caves, reefs, shipwrecks, and marine life. The dive sites cater to various experience levels and interests, from gentle shore dives to challenging deep water explorations.

The Museum of Roman Antiquities in Rabat houses artifacts from Malta's Roman period, showcasing the islands' ancient history under Roman rule. Casa Bernard in Rabat is a well-preserved late 16th Century Palazzo, home to a Maltese noble family who still live there today. Visitors can experience authentic Maltese noble architecture and hospitality. The Museum of Roman Antiquities provides insights into daily life during Roman times through its collection of pottery, jewelry, architectural elements, and other artifacts. Rabat also features catacombs that can be explored as part of Malta's underground archaeological heritage.

Marsaxlokk is a traditional fishing village in Malta famous for its open-air market held every Sunday. The colorful luzzu boats that dot the harbor are a quintessential Maltese sight, reflecting the village's fishing heritage. Visitors can enjoy fresh seafood at local restaurants overlooking the harbor. The market offers a variety of goods from fresh produce to crafts and souvenirs. Marsaxlokk provides an authentic glimpse into traditional Maltese coastal life and is a popular destination for both tourists and locals alike, especially on Sunday market days.

The Ħal Saflieni Hypogeum in Paola is considered one of the essential prehistoric monuments in the world. It was found during construction works in 1902 and consists of three underground levels dating from around 3600 to 2400 B.C. The underground complex was used both as a sanctuary and for burial purposes by the temple builders. Excavation has yielded a wealth of archaeological material including pottery, human bones, personal ornaments such as beads and amulets, little carved animals and larger figurines. Due to its delicate nature, access to the Hypogeum is strictly regulated and booking in advance is essential.

Victoria also known as Rabat is the main town of Gozo, featuring the It-Tokk Market held every Sunday. The town serves as the commercial and administrative center of Gozo, offering shops, restaurants, and cultural attractions. The Citadel overlooking Victoria offers panoramic views of the island and houses several museums and churches. Gozo offers a more tranquil pace of life compared to Malta, with its rolling hills, traditional villages, and pristine natural areas. The island is popular for cycling, walking, and exploring historical sites away from the crowds.

Mellieħa Bay is the largest and most popular beach in Malta, offering extensive sandy shores and shallow waters ideal for families. The beach is well-equipped with facilities including cafes, restaurants, and water sports rentals. Anchor Bay at the limits of Mellieħa is known for Popeye Village, the original movie set from the 1979 film starring Robin Williams, now converted into a tourist attraction and amusement park. Armier Bay is at the extreme northern fringe of Malta, facing Comino and Gozo islands, offering a more secluded beach experience.

chunks.page_title: UNESCO World Heritage Sites in Malta
chunks.chunk_text: Valletta the fortified capital city is inextricably linked to the history of the military and charitable Order of St John of Jerusalem. Built after the Great Siege of 1565 and named after Grandmaster Jean Parisot de la Valette, it contains hundreds of monuments all within a relatively small space. The city is one of the most concentrated historic areas in the world, with architectural treasures from the Baroque period. The city hosts regular plays and concerts, numerous exhibitions and street events throughout the year. Valletta was named European Capital of Culture in 2018, showcasing its rich cultural heritage.

chunks.page_title: Visit Malta - The Official Tourism Site for Malta, Gozo and Comino
chunks.chunk_text: VisitMalta is the official tourism website operated by the Malta Tourism Authority. The website offers information in multiple languages including English, Italian, French, German, Polish, Spanish, Dutch, and Portuguese. The site provides comprehensive travel information including things to do, accommodation options, events and festivals, travel information, and booking capabilities. Visitors can download the VisitMalta+ mobile app available on Google Play and Apple App Store for planning their trip. The website also features a newsletter subscription for receiving news and updates about Malta tourism.

Golden Bay is a major sandy beach in northern Malta popular with locals and tourists alike. Located near Mellieħa, it offers golden sands and clear waters. Għajn Tuffieħa is another popular beach with golden sand, accessible via a scenic walk down a winding road. Both beaches offer stunning views of the coastline and are well-equipped with facilities. These northern beaches are particularly popular during the summer months and offer various water sports activities. The area is known for its beautiful sunset views over the Mediterranean Sea.

Getting to Malta involves flights to Malta International Airport which connects to major European cities and some international destinations. Alternatively, visitors can arrive by ferry from Sicily. Once in Malta, visitors can navigate using public buses, the preferred mode of public transport, or opt for taxis and car rentals. Airport transfers are available from Malta International Airport to various destinations across the islands. For getting to Gozo and Comino, regular ferry services operate between Malta and Gozo, with boat trips to Comino available from various operators.

Popular areas for accommodation in Malta include Sliema and St. Julian's which offer numerous hotels and apartments near the seafront and nightlife. Valletta provides historic boutique hotels in restored palazzos. Northern Malta near Mellieħa and the beaches offers resort-style accommodation. Gozo provides quieter rural accommodation including farmhouses and boutique guesthouses. Accommodation options span various budgets from budget hostels to luxury five-star resorts. The island's accommodation sector is well-developed with international hotel chains present alongside charming locally-owned properties offering authentic Maltese hospitality.

Clapham Junction in Buskett at the limits of Ħad-Dingli features the most impressive and dense concentration of cart ruts on scrubland south of Buskett Gardens. These mysterious cart tracks are a unique feature of Malta's prehistoric landscape, with parallel grooves carved into the limestone dating back thousands of years. The purpose and mechanism of these cart ruts remain a mystery, with various theories proposed by archaeologists. The area is popular with hikers and those interested in Malta's prehistoric past. Visitors should bring sturdy footwear as the terrain can be rugged.

The War Museum at Fort St. Elmo in Valletta displays artifacts from Malta's military history including World War II memorabilia. The National Museum of Fine Arts in Valletta houses an impressive art collection spanning centuries. St. John's Cavalier Art Centre showcases contemporary art exhibitions and cultural events. The National Museum of Archaeology in Valletta holds regular exhibitions of art, sculpture and ceramics alongside its prehistoric collections. With their deep colours and rugged landscape, the Maltese Islands have long inspired artists, photographers and sculptors, both local and foreign.
